title: Stewardship at Scale: A New Editorial Term at AMJ
methods: Editorial (From the Editor); reflective and programmatic argument that synthesizes prior AMJ editorials and articulates an agenda for the 24th editorial term
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Roberson, Q.
theories: contributive justice; organizational justice; stewardship theory
keywords: contributive justice; developmental reviewing; editorial infrastructure; editorial stewardship; generative AI in scholarly publishing; inclusion; knowledge translation; peer review; reviewer development; scale and complexity
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Roberson advances a normative, programmatic argument rather than a causal model. She contends that AMJ's very success — roughly 2,000 annual submissions, a 13.8 five-year impact factor, and a global author base — has intensified demands on editorial, reviewing, and developmental systems in ways that threaten the journal's core commitments to intellectual pluralism and developmental reviewing. Building on the "stewardship" orientations of prior editors Beyer, Tsui, and Rynes, she argues that sustaining excellence at scale requires editorial infrastructures intentionally oriented toward contributive justice and inclusion: systems that broaden meaningful participation in authorship, reviewing, and editorial roles while preserving the standards that define the journal. The mechanism is institutional rather than individual — values endure only to the extent that the systems enacting them can adapt.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial frames editorial leadership as "inclusive stewardship" and advances contributive justice and inclusion as central editorial principles for the 24th term, extending prior editors' stewardship orientation by explicitly foregrounding individuals' belonging within an institutional context and their access to meaningful opportunities to participate in and be recognized for core scholarly activities. It reframes AMJ not simply as a publication outlet but as a field-shaping developmental institution whose continued excellence depends on the intentional evolution of editorial systems — aligning developmental effort with scholarly value creation, investing in reviewer development as field-building work, and cultivating a more inclusive, globally connected pipeline of contributors.
practical_implication: Authors, reviewers, and editors are called to approach submission, review, and editorial service as a collective enterprise. The 24th editorial team will streamline workflows, publish synthesized guidance from prior "From the Editors" essays to support manuscript readiness, run review development workshops and Community Accelerator outreach, expand knowledge-translation efforts (e.g., The Lit Review podcast; an "AMJ Behind the Scenes" feature), and ensure transparent, ethical use of generative AI consistent with AOM's AI policy. Success will be monitored through mixed-methods indicators — including topical and methodological diversity, review timeliness, and qualitative feedback on clarity, developmental value, and perceived fairness — treated as tools for responsible oversight rather than targets.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Roberson sets a forward agenda for the 24th editorial term: developing pre-submission guidance that synthesizes accumulated wisdom from prior "From the Editors" essays; aligning developmental initiatives with AOM Divisions, Interest Groups, and PDWs; investing in reviewer development workshops and Community Accelerator outreach to broaden access to AMJ's editorial processes; and expanding knowledge-translation efforts such as The Lit Review podcast and an "AMJ Behind the Scenes" feature. She also calls for continued, transparent engagement with generative AI in authoring and reviewing, and for mixed-methods monitoring of whether editorial systems enact the journal's pluralistic and developmental commitments at scale.
abstract: FROM THE EDITOR STEWARDSHIP AT SCALE: A NEW EDITORIAL TERM AT AMJ

title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence: Principles, Challenges, and Opportunities, by Luciano Floridi. Oxford University Press, 2023. 272 pp.
methods: Book review; critical essay engaging Floridi's normative framework for AI ethics
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Acosta-Navas, D.
theories: informational ethics; principlism (bioethics)
keywords: AI ethics; AI governance; engineering approach; ethics of artificial intelligence; good AI society; productive AI; sociotechnical systems; value tradeoffs
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper
practical_implication: The reviewer argues that Floridi's normative frameworks and taxonomies offer compelling resources to motivate deeper philosophical engagement and to guide ethically informed practical discussions in AI ethics. The book is positioned as rich input for business ethics researchers interested in the responsibilities of organizations that develop, adopt, deploy, or regulate AI, and for understanding the role of frontier technologies in channeling large-scale societal transformation. Business ethicists are uniquely situated to contribute frameworks for the responsibilities of private actors in the AI ecosystem.
limitations: The reviewer identifies three limitations of Floridi's framework. First, despite its global aspirations, the book tends toward Eurocentrism in policy recommendations, emphasizing bureaucratic regulatory bodies that may be difficult to realize in countries with fewer technical and financial resources. Second, the book overlooks the problem of tradeoffs, assuming that optimal balances can be attained between conflicting societal values (e.g., open source vs. safety, privacy vs. fairness in facial recognition) rather than acknowledging that tragic choices may be unavoidable. Third, Floridi's "engineering approach" refuses to define AI and dismisses productive AI's capabilities based on a decade-old example (Watson), leaving conceptual ambiguities that powerful actors could exploit and failing to account for recent advances in agentic and reasoning systems.
future_research: The reviewer identifies three ripe areas for future research. First, developing policy recommendations beyond Eurocentric bureaucratic models, expanding on international cooperation, industry-wide standards, accountability, and scalable education for the public, government workers, and technologists. Second, investigating how to address value tradeoffs responsibly, including identifying procedural constraints such as participatory frameworks for reaching acceptable compromises. Third, examining the sociotechnical systems and meso-level institutions (research labs, corporations, professional associations) in which AI is developed and deployed, including the conditions for legitimate decision making, operational constraints, and relationships with governments. The reviewer also calls for a fully-fledged theory of agency and intelligence to ground AI regulation.
abstract: fter the public launch of ChatGPT, the Future of Life Institute published an open letter calling for a moratorium on the development of frontier artificial intelligence (AI) systems. It was signed by over thirty thousand scholars, industry leaders, and public intellectuals. Judging by media headlines at the time, the signatories were not alone. Open AI’s launch created widespread concern regarding the rapid pace of AI’s development, and the risks it posed for humanity. Luciano Floridi seems to disagree with the reasoning behind this initiative. In his book The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence, he states: “It is because we lack a clear sense of socio-political direction that we are worried by the speed of our technological travel. We should be. Yet the solution is not to slow down, but to decide together where we want to go” (79). With adequate normative frameworks for AI, he says, we can simultaneously mitigate its worst risks and reap its greatest benefits. The book sets out to provide systematic and comprehensive guidelines for what he calls a “good AI society.”

title: Is academia addressing relevant questions to support a circularity transition in Latin America and the Caribbean?
methods: Convergent mixed-methods design combining 19 semi-structured expert interviews (generating 51 priority stakeholder questions), an updated PRISMA systematic literature review (335 peer-reviewed records, 405 research objectives), and thematic analysis following Braun and Clarke (2006); alignment between interview and literature datasets assessed with a Research Alignment Ratio (RAR) computed over 27 sub-themes within four overarching categories
industry: Cross-sectoral (waste management, manufacturing, agriculture/forestry/livestock, energy, construction)
country: Latin America and the Caribbean (11 countries plus 3 regional experts; literature dominated by Brazil 49%, Mexico 12%, Chile 8%, Colombia 6%)
time_period: Interviews March-November 2024; literature review 1997-April 2025 (updating Gallego-Schmid et al., 2024)
units: CE expert interviews and peer-reviewed scientific publications on CE in LAC
n_sample: 19 interviews (51 stakeholder questions); 335 publications (405 research objectives)
authors: Aguilar-Hernandez, G. A.; Guerrero, A. B.; Vásquez-Ibarra, L.; Rebolledo-Leiva, R.; Gallego-Schmid, A.
theories: stakeholder engagement; transdisciplinary research
keywords: Circular economy; Global South; Latin America and the Caribbean; Research gaps; Stakeholder engagement; Thematic analysis
research_question: Is academia addressing relevant questions to support the Circular Economy transition in Latin America and the Caribbean? Specifically, the paper determines (1) which stakeholder questions are being directly or indirectly addressed in current literature, (2) which areas remain underexplored or overlooked, and (3) how research agendas can be better aligned to support CE implementation.
mechanism_summary:
- Two datasets compared: 51 priority questions from 19 CE expert interviews and 405 research objectives from 335 peer-reviewed publications on CE in LAC
- Four overarching thematic categories (with 27 sub-themes): (1) technology and data, (2) culture, education and society, (3) economy and market, (4) policy
- Research Alignment Ratio (RAR): per-sub-theme ratio of interview frequency to literature frequency (centered on 1), classified as High, Medium, or Low alignment
- Three key factors driving misalignment between academic research and stakeholder needs: (1) science communication, (2) the process of setting research priorities and defining expected roles, and (3) the level of transdisciplinarity within CE research in LAC

The paper characterizes alignment as a descriptive, thematic comparison rather than a causal test: thematic overlaps at the category level coexist with considerable sub-theme misalignments, particularly in socio-cultural, economic, and policy dimensions. Drawing on the discussion, the authors argue that misalignment is sustained where science communication is fragmented and one-way rather than dialogue-based, where research priorities are set without participatory engagement with non-academic actors (leaving sub-themes such as citizen engagement, SME economic impacts, or monitoring of public policy underexplored), and where CE research remains disciplinarily siloed rather than transdisciplinary. Pragmatic stakeholder engagement, boundary organizations, stronger national-level financial instruments, and transdisciplinary research processes that bridge academic fields while actively involving practitioners and policymakers are offered as enabling conditions for closer alignment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances the debate on the societal role of academic research in sustainability transitions by providing an empirically grounded, LAC-specific assessment of alignment between stakeholder priorities and academic CE research. It contributes a Research Alignment Ratio methodology for systematically comparing thematic frequencies across stakeholder and literature datasets, and identifies three key drivers of misalignment (science communication, priority setting, and transdisciplinarity). It positions LAC as a Global South case study that invites the global CE research community to reflect on whether current research agendas adequately address pressing stakeholder needs while preserving academia's critical independence.
practical_implication: Policymakers, businesses, and practitioners gain a clearer view of where existing CE research provides an actionable knowledge base and where gaps remain—especially in culture/education/society, economy/market, and policy sub-themes such as cross-sectoral interaction, citizen engagement, SME economic impacts, policy instruments, and monitoring of public policy. For academic researchers and funders, the study calls for strengthening participatory research designs, dialogue-based science communication, national-level financial instruments for CE research, and transdisciplinary collaborations that actively involve practitioners and policymakers to translate scientific insights into CE implementation in LAC.
limitations: The interview sample size (19 experts, 35% response rate) may limit full thematic saturation, reflecting the comparatively small pool of CE experts in LAC versus regions such as Europe. There is a mismatch between interviewees' country expertise and the literature's concentration on Brazil (only 21% of interviewees specialized in Brazil while ~49% of reviewed studies focus on it), which may introduce bias. The degree of coordination and collaboration among CE researchers themselves across LAC was not directly measured.
future_research: Further country-level research—particularly in Brazil, Mexico, Chile, and Colombia—should refine understanding of research alignment in specific national contexts. Future work should investigate the degree of connection and collaboration among CE researchers across LAC, the role of gender dynamics given that ~70% of interviewees were women and CE initiatives in the region appear often driven by women, and how participatory and pragmatic stakeholder engagement approaches can be applied to LAC's cultural and institutional context to co-develop research agendas. Additional empirical work is needed on the informal sector's CE practices beyond waste management.
abstract: Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) is advancing toward a Circular Economy (CE), supported by expanding policy initiatives and a growing body of academic research. Over the past decade, CE academic studies in the region have examined developments at multiple sectors, scales and circular strategies, thus, contributing to CE research in the region. Yet, it remains uncertain whether academic research addresses the critical questions most relevant for non-academic stakeholders—including policymakers, practitioners, and businesses—to support the CE transition in LAC. To assess this alignment, we compared 51 priority questions identified by 19 CE experts in LAC countries with the research objectives of 335 scientific publications on CE in the region. The analysis reveals the extent to which stakeholder priorities are reflected in academic literature and highlights persistent gaps where further research is needed. Our findings stress the importance of fostering more collaborative, transdisciplinary, and stakeholder-oriented research to accelerate a just and effective CE transition in LAC.
apa_citation: Aguilar-Hernandez, G. A., Guerrero, A. B., Vásquez-Ibarra, L., Rebolledo-Leiva, R., & Gallego-Schmid, A. (2026). Is academia addressing relevant questions to support a circularity transition in Latin America and the Caribbean? *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00038-8

title: Rewards-based crowdfunding as an institutional carrier: The cross-border backing of environmental projects
methods: Quantitative observational study using two-stage Heckman selection models (with robustness checks using OLS, linear probability, and coarsened exact matching) on Kickstarter campaign data merged with the country-level Environment Performance Index (EPI)
industry: Rewards-based crowdfunding (Kickstarter projects across all platform categories)
country: Campaigns from 153 countries/territories; backers from 226 countries/territories
units: Crowdfunding campaigns
n_sample: 290,126 campaigns with 29.87 million individual pledges
authors: Sewaid, A.; Vanacker, T.; Zahra, S. A.
theories: community logic; institutional carriers; institutional logics; institutional theory
keywords: Country institutions; Cross-border flows; Entrepreneurial finance; Environmental projects; Institutional carrier; Rewards-based crowdfunding
research_question: Do environmental projects have an advantage in attracting cross-border funds on rewards-based crowdfunding platforms, and in which direction do funds flow across borders — specifically, do pro-environmental community logics channel cross-border backing from countries with stronger environmental institutions toward environmental projects located in countries with weaker environmental support systems?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on institutional theory, the authors theorize that global rewards-based crowdfunding platforms act as cross-border institutional carriers infused with a pro-environmental community logic. Because backers on such platforms are motivated by non-pecuniary, prosocial and environmental values rather than market-based financial returns, projects with stronger environmental orientations become salient to backers in countries with stronger environmental protection institutions, who then transmit resources and pro-environmental values across borders. This mechanism is amplified when recipient projects sit in weak-institution contexts, because backers in strong-institution countries recognize the acute resource constraints and global interconnectedness of environmental problems and direct support accordingly. The result is a bottom-up diffusion of pro-environmental logics and resources that overturns the cross-border disadvantages predicted by market-logic-based international entrepreneurial finance research.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to institutional theory by conceptualizing global crowdfunding platforms as cross-border institutional carriers that, unlike prior top-down accounts centered on multinationals, returnee elites, or state-linked intermediaries, enable dispersed crowds to diffuse pro-environmental values and resources bottom-up across national borders. It contributes to international entrepreneurship and entrepreneurial finance by showing that pro-environmental community logics in rewards-based crowdfunding reverse the cross-border disadvantages documented for venture capital and angel investing, producing fundraising advantages for risky environmental ventures in weak-institutional contexts. It further extends crowdfunding research by highlighting how community logics — distinct from the market logics dominating traditional finance — reshape global resource flows toward entrepreneurs addressing sustainability challenges.
practical_implication: Rewards-based crowdfunding is a meaningful channel for raising cross-border funding for environmental sustainability projects, with environmentally oriented campaigns attracting a larger share of non-local backers (25.13% vs. 21.45%) than less environmental ones. Environmental entrepreneurs operating in countries with weak environmental support systems should strategically target and cultivate backers in countries with stronger environmental institutions, foregrounding the environmental impact and resource constraints of their ventures to activate prosocial motivations. Entrepreneurs should also invest in well-designed campaign content (video pitches, images, narratives, reward structures) because campaign quality strongly shapes cross-border fundraising success.
limitations: The authors do not directly observe individual-level motivations of backers, so results document cross-border funding patterns consistent with pro-environmental community logics rather than directly testing the mechanism. The study uses a single platform (Kickstarter) and a composite country-level Environment Performance Index, leaving open whether findings generalize to other crowdfunding types/platforms and to specific components of environmental institutions (e.g., regulations, subsidies, enforcement). The sample is restricted to 2009-2016 (pre-Paris Agreement), so post-2016 institutional shifts lie outside the analysis.
future_research: Future research should directly test the underlying community-logic mechanism using surveys, experiments, or qualitative analyses of backer motivations and project communications, and should examine backer heterogeneity and specific environmental policy dimensions rather than composite indices. Longitudinal studies are needed to document how crowdfunding platforms' role as cross-border institutional carriers evolves with technological, political, and social change, and to identify project- and country-level factors (including culture and language) that amplify or dampen this carrier role. Researchers should also investigate reverse flows — how innovations from weak-institution contexts diffuse to developed settings — and test whether the institutional-logic framework extends to funding of other social grand challenges beyond environmental issues.
abstract: institutional carriers that transmit backer support across borders guided by pro-environmental community logics. Using Kickstarter data spanning campaigns in 153 countries and backers from 226 countries and territories, we find a positive relationship between projects' environ­ mental orientation and their likelihood of attracting cross-border funding. Moreover, this crossborder support originates from countries with stronger environmental institutions and is espe­ cially directed toward projects in weaker institutional contexts. Our findings differ from pre­ vailing expectations from international entrepreneurial finance research focused on venture capitalists and angels, which emphasize cross-border disadvantages for risky, environmental projects in weak-institutional contexts, while we report cross-border fundraising advantages for these projects. Our study contributes to research on the nexus of institutional theory, interna­ tional entrepreneurship, and crowdfunding research by showing how international crowdfunding platforms can enable the bottom-up diffusion of pro-environmental values and resources across country borders.
apa_citation: Sewaid, A., Vanacker, T., & Zahra, S. A. (2026). Rewards-based crowdfunding as an institutional carrier: The cross-border backing of environmental projects. *Journal of Business Venturing*, 41, 106586. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2026.106586

title: Sharing is ethicizing: Examining the impact of sharing observed unethical behavior with family members on ethical voice at work.
methods: Multi-study mixed-design empirical package: two critical incident surveys (Studies 1 and 4), a laboratory experiment with behavioral outcome (Study 2), and a three-wave cross-lagged panel survey (Study 3); tested with OLS regression, bootstrapped mediation (N = 5,000 resamples), and cross-lagged structural equation modeling
industry: Multiple industries including education, health care, professional services, information technology, and manufacturing
country: United States (Studies 1, 2, 3, 4); additional Pilot Study 1 in China
units: Working adults (married, fully employed) recruited via Prolific Academic who had observed unethical behavior at work
n_sample: Study 1 N=245; Study 2 N=169; Study 3 N=239; Study 4 N=239
authors: Chen, A.; Mai, K. M.; Min Ye, Y.
theories: functional perspective of rumination; social sharing theory
keywords: behavioral ethics; ethical voice; family members; observed unethical behavior; reflective rumination; rumination; sharing; social sharing
research_question: How does the extent to which employees share observed workplace unethical behavior with family members — an extraorganizational factor — influence their subsequent ethical voice at work, and through what cognitive mechanism does this effect operate?
mechanism_summary: Integrating social sharing research (Rimé, 2009) and the functional perspective of rumination (Martin & Tesser, 1996), the authors argue that greater extent of sharing observed unethical behavior with a family member preserves unfiltered factual details and keeps the behavior salient in memory, thereby increasing reflective rumination — recurrent, problem-solving thoughts about the issue, its causes, and potential solutions. Because family members are uninvolved and trusted, conversations foreground factual details and broader implications rather than self-focused distress, fostering the adaptive reflective (rather than affective or risk-focused) form of rumination. Reflective rumination, in turn, mobilizes cognitive and motivational resources that propel the observer to engage in ethical voice — communicating ethical concerns or suggestions to those who can effect change. Study 4 shows that the indirect effect is uniquely driven by reflective rumination, not by risk, ethical, or affect-based rumination.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances behavioral ethics by offering a novel extraorganizational predictor (sharing with family members) of observer responses to unethical behavior, demonstrating unique variance beyond established personal and intraorganizational predictors and beyond sharing with coworkers. It extends the social sharing literature by differentiating sharing targets (family vs. coworkers) and by using a granular extent-based operationalization of sharing to show issue-specific downstream behavior rather than general well-being outcomes. It also contributes to the nascent literature on the beneficial effects of rumination by showing that reflective rumination — specifically, its problem-solving content — uniquely enables proactive ethical action, underscoring the importance of specifying the content of rumination rather than treating it as a unitary construct.
practical_implication: Employees who suspect wrongdoing at work can benefit from openly and extensively discussing the issue with uninvolved, trusted family members, as this fosters reflective rumination and motivates them to raise ethical concerns at work, potentially saving organizations from future reputational damage. Organizations and policymakers should carefully review the scope of nondisclosure and confidentiality agreements to ensure they do not impede external reflections on observed unethical behavior and inadvertently suppress ethical voice. More broadly, open and candid work-family communication emerges as a potential tool for bolstering ethical behavior within organizations.
limitations: In Studies 1 and 4, sharing extent was operationalized as the amount of time spent discussing the issue with a family member, which may not fully align with observers' subjective perceptions of how extensive the discussion was. The laboratory experiment (Study 2) used a constrained time frame, moderately intense manipulated unethical behavior, and an anonymous, lower-risk voice context, which may not elicit the highly persistent rumination or real-stakes voice behavior typical of field work settings. Only one study (Study 4) was conducted to rule out alternative forms of rumination as explanations, leaving the unique explanatory power of reflective rumination in need of further replication.
future_research: Future research should use measures that capture observers' subjective perceptions of sharing extensiveness, present more intense unethical behavior with longer intervals between sharing and rumination measurement, and replicate Study 4's findings using different methods to strengthen the unique explanatory power of reflective rumination. Scholars should examine the temporal dynamics and bidirectional relationships between sharing and rumination over time and across sharing targets, investigate how conversation partners' reactions shape rumination content, and compare observer versus victim sharing in eliciting different rumination forms and ethical voice outcomes. Additional avenues include extending the framework to nonmoral workplace issues and examining when sharing unethical behavior helps or hinders family relationships and work-family conflict.
abstract: prior research has primarily focused on personal and intraorganizational factors shaping observer responses to workplace unethical behavior, the potential inﬂuence of extraorganizational factors has been largely overlooked. Recognizing that employees often share workplace experiences with close others, such as their family members, we explore how the extent of sharing with family members inﬂuences employee responses to observed unethical behavior. Integrating the functional perspective of rumination and social sharing research, we theorize that the extent of sharing with family members increases observer reﬂective rumination about the unethical behavior and their tendency to engage in ethical voice at work. We found empirical evidence in two critical incident surveys (Studies 1 and 4), an experiment (Study 2), and a crosslagged panel survey (Study 3). Finally, we discuss the theoretical and practical implications of this research.
apa_citation: Chen, A., Mai, K. M., & Min Ye, Y. (2026). Sharing is ethicizing: Examining the impact of sharing observed unethical behavior with family members on ethical voice at work. *Journal of Applied Psychology*. https://doi.org/10.1037/apl0001356

title: Fair Market Value of Used Capacity Assets: Forecasts for Repurposed Electric Vehicle Batteries
methods: Analytical valuation model extended from prior replacement-value and user-cost-of-capital frameworks, combined with empirical calibration using battery cost and degradation data for LFP and NCX chemistries in the U.S. and China
industry: Lithium-ion battery and stationary energy storage
time_period: Forecasts over the coming decade (through approximately 2031)
units: Battery chemistries (LFP and NCX) and their fair market values for second-life applications
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Bach, A.; Onori, S.; Reichelstein, S.; Zhuang, J.
theories: no-arbitrage pricing; replacement value theory; user cost of capital
keywords: capacity assets; fair market valuation; recycling; repurposing; second life application; used EV batteries
research_question: How should the fair market value of used capacity assets be determined when owners face a choice between immediate recycling and repurposing for a second-life application, and what does this imply for the economic viability of repurposing retired lithium-ion EV batteries (LFP vs. NCX) in the U.S. and China over the coming decade?
mechanism_summary: The authors extend replacement-value and user-cost-of-capital models to assets that can be recycled or repurposed. Under no-arbitrage pricing for energy storage services, the fair market value of a used asset equals the discounted cash flows from its remaining capacity valued at the user cost of capital, with two new elements: a terminal recycling payoff and a repurposing cost. The effective FMV at the end of first life is the better of immediate recycling versus costly repurposing followed by second-life deployment and delayed recycling. Because LFP and NCX chemistries compete in the same capacity market, NCX values are bounded by the lower user cost of capital set by LFP, so NCX batteries with high raw-material recycling value are pushed toward immediate recycling while LFP batteries remain attractive for repurposing.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends prior accounting and economic studies on the replacement value of operating assets (Arrow 1964; Rogerson 2008; Rajan and Reichelstein 2009) by incorporating two new elements into the user-cost-of-capital framework: a terminal recycling cash flow and an explicit repurposing cost that gates entry into a second-life application. The authors develop a no-arbitrage valuation framework in which multiple competing asset technologies determine the user cost of capital for capacity services, yielding a general model of how technological progress, repurposing costs, and raw-material values jointly shape repurpose-versus-recycle decisions. The framework generalizes beyond EV batteries to other second-life assets such as consumer electronics, data-center hardware, industrial equipment, and renewable-power components.
practical_implication: For LFP batteries, repurposing into stationary energy storage is economically viable in both the U.S. and China over the coming decade, supporting business cases and investment in second-life deployment. For NCX batteries, owners should generally favor immediate recycling, especially in China and for batteries below roughly 80 percent state of health, with recycling becoming preferred in the U.S. even at 90 percent SOH by around 2031. Managers and investors can use the framework to guide corporate investment toward recycling versus repurposing infrastructure, and automakers may redesign vehicles (e.g., cell-to-body structural batteries for NCX, repurposing-friendly designs for LFP) and contractual arrangements (e.g., retained battery ownership rights) around these asset economics.
limitations: The model assumes competitive and frictionless markets for new and used assets and treats key parameters (such as degradation pathways and raw-material values) as known; introducing uncertainty, volatility, and real-option effects is acknowledged as a complication. The empirical application is restricted to two battery chemistries (LFP and NCX) and two markets (U.S. and China), and does not incorporate temporary recycling or repurposing capacity constraints or risk aversion on the part of decision-makers.
future_research: Future work could examine the sensitivity of fair market value estimates to market frictions such as limited recycling or repurposing capacity, and extend the framework to emerging battery chemistries such as sodium-ion and solid-state batteries, whose declining costs would further reduce the user cost of capital and the fair market value of incumbent chemistries. Researchers could also explore how vehicle owners will capture the residual value of used battery packs, including contractual models in which manufacturers retain ownership rights over the battery in exchange for lower upfront vehicle prices.
abstract: In response to growing economic and environmental concerns, companies in a range of industries seek to repurpose products (assets) that retain functional capacity beyond their initial first life. This paper examines a generic valuation model for used capacity assets that can either be recycled immediately or repurposed for a second life application. We apply our model framework to lithium-ion batteries retired from electric vehicles, as these assets typically retain substantial energy storage capacity at the end of their first life. Our analysis focuses on two battery chemistries: lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) and nickel-cobalt-X (NCX) based. We project their future fair market values in the United States and China. Our findings indicate that repurposing LFP batteries will be economically viable in both countries for the coming decade. In contrast, for most NCX batteries immediate recycling will soon be preferable due to their more valuable raw material content and shorter usable lives.
apa_citation: Bach, A., Onori, S., Reichelstein, S., & Zhuang, J. (2026). Fair market value of used capacity assets: Forecasts for repurposed electric vehicle batteries. *The Accounting Review*, 101(2), 33-55. https://doi.org/10.2308/TAR-2025-0024

title: Supporting repairability analysis through graph-based visualisation
methods: Three-stage methodology combining (I) input data structuring via an EN 45554-based Repair Matrix, (II) graph visualisation in Gephi using the ForceAtlas2 layout and (III) analytical output based on network metrics (degree, modularity, eccentricity, betweenness centrality) plus a novel graph-based Repair Index; demonstrated through an in-depth single-product case study.
industry: Small household appliances (capsule coffee machines)
units: Product (capsule coffee machine) modelled as a graph of components and connections
n_sample: 1 product case study
authors: Blanco-Espeleta, E.; Pérez-Belis, V.; Bovea, M. D.
theories: graph theory; network analysis
keywords: Circular economy; EN 45554; Graph-based modelling; Industrial ecology; Network metrics; Repair Index
research_question: The main objective of the study is to assess the potential of graph-based modelling and network metrics as tools for evaluating product repairability, including identifying the required input data, the most relevant graph tools and metrics, appropriate visualisation strategies, and a new graph-based metric to quantify repairability at the network level.
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that repairability is driven not only by component-level attributes but by the structural configuration of the product's architecture. By encoding EN 45554-based Repair Matrix scores as node and edge attributes in a graph and applying the ForceAtlas2 layout in Gephi, the methodology makes visible how connectivity, accessibility and sequencing shape repair effort. Network metrics such as degree, modularity, eccentricity and betweenness centrality then translate this structure into quantitative indicators of complexity, accessibility constraints and critical components, which are aggregated into a novel graph-based Repair Index that summarises system-level repairability.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances repairability assessment by integrating analysis, visualisation and parametrisation of repair-related information within a single graph-theoretic framework, addressing the gap left by conventional scoring matrices and static diagrams that do not preserve architectural logic. It introduces a novel graph-based Repair Index tailored to repairability, combining EN 45554-based parameters with network metrics so that accessibility, sequencing and part centrality are directly embedded in the assessment. In doing so, it contributes a conceptually scalable and interpretable methodology that links product architecture to circular-economy objectives of repairability and sustainability.
practical_implication: Designers, policymakers and manufacturers can use the graph-based methodology as a decision-support tool to identify high-priority parts that should be redesigned to reduce dependency, improve accessibility or enhance modularity. By combining macro-level graph insights with micro-level node analysis and visual ranking, the approach provides actionable guidance for design-for-repair strategies and supports compliance with ecodesign and Right-to-Repair regulations such as Regulation (EU) 2024/1781.
limitations: The work is an exploratory investigation tested through an in-depth case study on a single product configuration (a capsule coffee machine), so the immediate generalisation of the results is limited. The current implementation relies on expert-based scoring from the EN 45554-based Repair Matrix, whose weights may vary depending on the experts consulted, and Gephi is a general-purpose tool that does not interpret product structures or handle hierarchical assemblies, with modelling and data entry still performed manually.
future_research: Future work will apply the approach to additional product models and categories to assess its versatility and generalisability, and will conduct systematic sensitivity and uncertainty analyses to examine how alternative weighting schemes influence the indicators. Empirical validation in collaboration with repair centres and manufacturers is planned to compare model-derived indicators (disassembly paths, repair effort, graph-based RI) with real-world repair times, frequencies and outcomes. The authors also plan to automate data integration and graph generation by linking the EN 45554-based Repair Matrix and the graph-based model directly to CAD environments, using machine learning to assist in the automatic identification and classification of components and connections.
abstract: Repairability is a key aspect of circular economy strategies, contributing to product longevity and resource efficiency. However, existing assessment methods often provide limited insight into product architecture and rarely combine visual and quantitative analysis. This study proposes a graph-based modelling approach for repairability assessment that integrates network metrics with structural visualisation. Building on EN 45554, an EN 45554-based Repair Matrix (RM) is used to assign repair-related attributes to product components and their connections. These attributes are represented as nodes and edges within a network, enabling both visual exploration and quantitative analysis of product architecture. A three-stage methodology is developed: (i) input data structuring, (ii) graph visualisation using Gephi software and (iii) analytical output. In addition, a novel graph-based Repair Index (RI) is introduced to quantify repairability at the system level. The approach is demonstrated through an in-depth case study of a capsule coffee machine. The results show that graph metrics such as degree, modularity, eccentricity and betweenness centrality effectively reveal structural complexity, accessibility constraints and critical components. The proposed method offers a conceptually scalable and interpretable framework that supports designfor-repair strategies by highlighting component dependencies and optimising disassembly logic. Overall, this work contributes to the development of more repairable and sustainable product architectures, supporting circular economy objectives.
apa_citation: Blanco-Espeleta, E., Pérez-Belis, V., & Bovea, M. D. (2026). Supporting repairability analysis through graph-based visualisation. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00031-1

title: Are Social Media Platforms a Threat to Democracy? An Ecosystem Governance Perspective
methods: Conceptual 'Counterpoint' essay; develops an ecosystem-governance framework that reconceptualizes misinformation, fake news, and polarization on social media platforms as 'ecosystem failures' rather than political-space problems, and derives a four-mechanism reflexive governance architecture (participatory governance, adaptive control, integrity safeguards, intentional value redistribution) through theoretical synthesis of platform ecosystem and stakeholder-governance literatures, illustrated with secondary evidence (e.g., Cambridge Analytica, Zollo et al. 2017, Velichety & Shrivastava 2022).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Cennamo, C.; Karanovic, J.
theories: ecosystem failures (Jacobides, Cennamo & Gawer 2024); ecosystem theory / ecosystem governance; generativity tension (desired vs. undesired generativity); multi-sided platform / two-sided markets theory; stakeholder governance theory (Bacq & Aguilera 2021)
keywords: democracy; digital; ecosystem failures; ecosystem governance; multi-sided markets; social media platforms
research_question: Are social media platforms inherently threatening democracy, and if so, is the appropriate response to reimagine them as political/deliberative spaces — or is it instead to treat their harms (fake news, misinformation, polarization) as 'ecosystem failures' and redesign platform governance to internalize these negative externalities?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: SMP harms are not defects of commercial nature per se but endogenous 'ecosystem failures' — structural consequences of algorithmic curation, strong network effects with near-zero replication costs, and engagement-based monetization decoupled from editorial standards.
- Key constructs: ecosystem failure (value capture distortion / coordination failure / structural pathology); negative externalities of user attention markets; generativity tension between 'desired' and 'undesired' output; multidimensional value (short-term engagement vs. long-term trust, informational quality, ecosystem health).
- Diagnosis of three failures: (1) fake news as value-capture distortion (virality uncorrelated with truth); (2) misinformation as coordination failure (selective exposure and biased amplification); (3) polarization as structural ecosystem pathology (engagement-driven personalization endogenizing ideological segmentation).
- Proposed mechanisms (reflexive ecosystem governance): participatory governance, adaptive control, integrity safeguards, and intentional value redistribution — operating ex ante by re-pricing visibility and rewards rather than ex post content moderation.
- Boundary conditions / trade-offs: platform heterogeneity (not all SMPs face the same failures); advertiser brand-safety vs. user preferences for controversial content; risk of over- or under-moderation; political capture of moderation authority.

The authors argue that because SMPs are ecosystem architects whose governance design choices shape who sees what and why, misinformation and polarization cannot be corrected by reactive moderation, user education, or by reimagining platforms as public utilities. Instead, changing the structure of the payoff — embedding integrity costs into ranking, replication, and reward rules — realigns private incentives with ecosystem health, so that high-quality engagement dominates low-integrity virality as a design-time rather than police-time outcome.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a 'reflexive ecosystem governance' framework that reframes platform democratic harms as ecosystem failures rather than political-space failures, and operationalizes Jacobides, Cennamo & Gawer's (2024) ecosystem-failure typology for social media through four governance mechanisms adapted from Bacq and Aguilera's (2021) stakeholder governance theory. In doing so, it distinguishes SMP externalities from traditional-media externalities along three structural dimensions (algorithmic selectivity, near-zero replication cost, engagement–monetization decoupling), and reframes platform governance from incentive-control dyads toward multidimensional value management — what the authors call 'platform governance 2.0'.
practical_implication: Platforms should move from ex post content moderation toward ex ante governance design: risk-adjusted ranking, demotion of low-integrity content, bot-detection and political-ad transparency, participatory algorithm-review panels, intentional redistribution of visibility to public-interest journalists and educators, and algorithmic audits with trusted flaggers. Regulators (e.g., under the EU Digital Services Act) should focus on requiring platforms to identify, assess, and mitigate systemic risks rather than directly adjudicating content, and should resist solutions that effectively make platforms arbiters of truth.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further scholarly work on the future of platform governance and on designing more trustworthy digital ecosystems, including how to distinguish structural incentives from actual content effects across heterogeneous SMP models, how to operationalize reflexive governance mechanisms in practice, and how regulation (e.g., DSA-style systemic-risk reporting) can complement rather than substitute for platform-level ecosystem design.
abstract: Castello, Colleoni, Scherer, and Trittin contend that social media platforms threaten democratic processes by facilitating the spread of misinformation and fostering polarized debates – dynamics that ultimately serve to monetize user attention. We acknowledge the problem but challenge their proposed solution of conceptualizing social media as political spaces. Instead, drawing on ecosystem theory, we propose an 'ecosystem failure' analysis as a more robust analytical framework. Within this perspective, we see these information-related problems as negative externalities that weaken the entire platform ecosystem. These externalities are structurally distinct due to algorithmic curation that selectively amplifies content in ways that reinforce user biases, strong network effects with almost zero replication costs, and engagement-based monetization that decouples production from editorial standards. We therefore advance a reflexive ecosystem governance approach that accounts for broader ecosystem value, shifting the focus beyond short-term economic performance to encompass overall ecosystem health. This approach transforms the management of negative externalities into a core strategic interest for the platform and aligns the incentives of individual actors ex ante, by design, prioritizing risk-adjusted reach and demotion of low-integrity content over ex post moderation. We analyse the mechanisms that align private incentives with democratic resilience and highlight the inherent trade-offs involved.
apa_citation: Cennamo, C., & Karanovic, J. (2026). Are social media platforms a threat to democracy? An ecosystem governance perspective. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70044

title: Emancipatory entrepreneurship by a stigmatized social group
methods: Historical qualitative case study; grounded theory analysis of 30 years (1988-2018) of archival documents and 31 semi-structured interviews
industry: Gambling/casino industry
units: First Nations (Indigenous peoples) collective casino ventures
n_sample: 31 interviews (20 First Nations leaders, 11 government/industry informants); ~8000 pages of documents; 19 casinos established
authors: Lu, C.; Zhang, R.; Reay, T.
theories: contentious collective action; entrepreneurship as emancipation; stigma theory
keywords: Emancipation; Entrepreneurship; Interaction; Social group; Stigma
research_question: How does emancipatory entrepreneurship by a stigmatized social group unfold over time?
mechanism_summary: The authors theorize a three-phase process in which a stigmatized group first mobilizes internally by forging a shared collective entrepreneurial identity and narrative, then enters a first wave of contestations with stigmatizing audiences over collective entrepreneurial identity, autonomy, and resource eligibility, which resolves into an interim settlement enabling venture creation. A second wave of contestations centered on venture differentiation and governance then produces adjusted settlements. Drawing on contentious collective action theory, the authors argue that a mix of symbolic and material strategies, combined with partial acquiescence to powerful stigmatizers, enables the group to recalibrate power, legitimate its ventures, and transition stigmatizers into apparently supportive audiences, sustaining the precarious path of emancipatory entrepreneurship over time.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances the EE literature by developing a process model of collective emancipatory entrepreneurship by stigmatized groups, shifting the locus of EE theorizing from individual entrepreneurs to the dynamic interactions between group entrepreneurs and external audiences. It shows that partial acquiescence to stigmatizing audiences, rather than signaling emancipation failure, can be instrumental in forming interim settlements that sustain ventures and enable long-term progress toward emancipation. It further contributes to the entrepreneurship-stigma intersection by highlighting the importance of managing interdependence with stigmatizing audiences, demonstrating that stigmatizers also depend on the obedience of the stigmatized, which creates leverage that group entrepreneurs can exploit.
practical_implication: Stigmatized groups pursuing collective entrepreneurship should anticipate waves of contention with media, government, and public stakeholders and prepare by mobilizing internal unity through shared collective entrepreneurial identities and narratives. They should combine power-based tactics such as protests with actions designed to gain sympathy and support, and work with alternative resource providers like social procurement programs or inclusive business accelerators when mainstream sources are closed. They should also accept that negotiated partial concessions and interim settlements, though uncomfortable, can be critical stepping stones toward longer-term emancipation.
limitations: Not reported in paper.
future_research: The authors call for research in other contexts beyond First Nations casinos to validate and extend the process model of collective emancipatory entrepreneurship, particularly for other stigmatized groups such as women in highly patriarchal societies, undocumented immigrants, and people with histories of mental illness or incarceration. They also invite further study of the dynamics of contestations and settlements in emancipatory entrepreneurship and of how such processes can inform approaches to alleviating oppression via entrepreneurship.
abstract: In the world, marginalized groups such as women in highly patriarchal societies, displaced war victims, and oppressed ethnicities face structural constraints when attempting careers in established corporations. As a result, they may seek entrepreneurship as a way to break free from various constraints, despite stigma and lower power positions. In this study, we examine how a stigmatized social group can engage in emancipatory entrepreneurship through interactions with different stakeholders. We conducted a qualitative study of how First Nations in Canada collectively opened casinos to emancipate from post-colonial policies. By analyzing documents from 1988 to 2018 and 31 interviews with individuals who played major roles in casino creation or are knowledgeable about the process, we found that a stigmatized group will go through waves of contestations with stakeholders interspersed by interim settlements, once they are internally mobilized for collective entrepreneurship. We identified different types of contestations, including contestation around the stigmatized's collective entrepreneurial identity, autonomy, resource eligibility, venture differentiation and governance. Overall, we found that the stigmatized collective entrepreneur can use a mix of symbolic and material strategies to counter the resistance of stigmatizing stakeholders. In so doing, they can gain access to entrepreneurship and preserve the precarious path of emancipatory entrepreneurship.
apa_citation: Lu, C., Zhang, R., & Reay, T. (2026). Emancipatory entrepreneurship by a stigmatized social group. *Journal of Business Venturing*, 41, 106578. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2026.106578

title: The ESG-Innovation Disconnect: Evidence from Green Patenting
methods: Large-sample archival empirical analysis of U.S. patent data (USPTO Patent Citation and Patent Assignment databases, 1980-2020) merged with Sustainalytics and MSCI ESG ratings; OLS fixed-effects regressions at the industry-year and firm-year level with heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors clustered by industry, plus citation-based quality measures, first-mover tests, patent-thicket measures, and analyses of green product revenue, low-carbon hiring, and fund flows
industry: U.S. publicly traded firms across all sectors, with a focus on the traditional energy sector (SIC 10, 12, 13, 14, 29, 49, excluding alternative-energy NAICS codes) vs. other industries
time_period: 1980-2020 (full patent sample); 2008-2020 for tests involving ESG scores
units: firm-year and industry-year observations of public firms producing patents
n_sample: 11,397 public firms producing 3,032,611 patents (235,399 classified as green); 3,429 firm-year observations in the main ESG-score regressions
authors: Cohen, L. H.; Gurun, U. G.; Nguyen, Q. H.
theories: incumbent cannibalization / creative destruction; industrial organization
keywords: ESG investment; ESG ratings; capital flows; green patents; innovation; sustainable investing
research_question: Which firms actually contribute most significantly to green innovation in the United States, and do current ESG rating and capital allocation mechanisms effectively reward the entities best positioned to develop environmental technologies? The authors in particular ask whether traditional energy firms are systematically penalized by ESG ratings and divestment-oriented capital flows despite their role as producers of green patents.
mechanism_summary: The paper develops a conceptual framework grounded in industrial organization: when incumbent firms rationally anticipate the eventual technological displacement of their existing product lines, profit-maximizing behavior leads them to invest in the very innovations that could cannibalize their current business, aligning private incentives with environmental objectives without external ESG inducements. Empirically, traditional energy firms dedicate roughly 20.91% of their patenting to green innovation (vs. 9.2% for other green-patenting industries), produce patents with about 14% more citations and more top-5% "blockbuster" patents, enter green technology classes over 600,000 patent-sequence slots earlier and are roughly 80% more likely to produce pioneering patents, and accompany this with greater low-carbon product revenue and green hiring. Yet ESG rating providers systematically score them lower and penalize them further for each additional green patent, and environmentally focused funds underweight them, creating a disconnect between green innovation producers and where sustainable capital is directed.
theoretical_contribution: The paper documents, for the first time at large scale, the landscape of U.S. green patenting and shows that incumbent "brown" firms — specifically traditional energy producers — are first-mover, high-quality, foundational green innovators, contradicting the implicit premise of ESG exclusion frameworks. It offers a competitive-dynamics / cannibalization mechanism from industrial organization under which private, profit-maximizing incentives can drive environmental innovation absent preferential capital allocation, and speaks to broader questions about harnessing market competition to achieve societal objectives. It also provides evidence that ESG ratings fail to capture concrete green innovation, product, and labor inputs, offering a critique of current ESG measurement practice.
practical_implication: Investors, ESG rating providers, and policymakers should incorporate green innovation inputs and outputs (patenting, low-carbon products, green hiring, and the acquisition and citation of foundational green IP) when evaluating firms' environmental contributions, rather than relying on sector-level exclusion or disclosure-centric scoring. A more balanced evaluation framework — one that recognizes both the current environmental footprint of traditional energy firms and their demonstrated role in producing technologies critical for the green transition — could improve capital allocation toward firms actually advancing decarbonization. The authors report that, following circulation of their paper, at least one major ESG rating provider (MSCI) introduced patent-based "Environmental Opportunities" indicators reflecting clean tech, green building, and renewable energy.
limitations: The analysis is confined to U.S. publicly traded firms and USPTO patents, so private and non-U.S. green innovators are outside the sample. ESG score data only begin in 2008, limiting the ESG-specific regressions to 2008-2020. The authors also acknowledge that traditional energy firms remain substantial contributors to fossil-fuel extraction and emissions, so the findings do not imply that ESG investors should refrain from divestment — only that current evaluation frameworks may be incomplete.
future_research: Future work should develop ESG evaluation frameworks that explicitly incorporate green innovation inputs and outputs (patents, low-carbon products, green employment) and test how such richer measures affect capital allocation, prices, and real environmental outcomes. Researchers could extend the incumbent-cannibalization framework to other industries facing technological obsolescence, examine international patenting and non-U.S. energy firms, and directly model the interaction between divestment pressure and incumbent innovation incentives to understand when market competition alone suffices to drive the green transition versus when targeted capital allocation is needed.
abstract: Notes. This table reports the results of OLS regressions where the dependent variable is industry green patenting, which is the mean of total green patents divided by the total patents for all firms in each industry. Energy Sector is defined in Table 1. We identify green patents using OECD’s IPC classification; that is, green patents are the ones that contain one of the following environmental technologies: environmental management; water-related adaptation technologies; biodiversity protection; ecosystem health; climate change mitigation technologies related to energy generation, transmission, or distribution, transportation, buildings, wastewater treatment or waste management, and production or processing of goods; and capture, storage, sequestration, or disposal of greenhouse gases. The unit of observation is industry (two-digit SIC code) and year. The sample covers 1980 to 2020. Reported t-statistics in parentheses are heteroscedasticity-robust and clustered by industry. FE, fixed effect.
apa_citation: Cohen, L. H., Gurun, U. G., & Nguyen, Q. H. (2026). The ESG-innovation disconnect: Evidence from green patenting. *Management Science*. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2024.09124

title: Enhancing regional resilience for energy price shocks: efficient gas use and upstream decarbonization
methods: Quantitative modeling; a novel regional revealed cost competition (RCC) model integrating an input–output price model with a revealed competition measure, calibrated to a multi-regional world input–output table at the EU NUTS 2 level, used to derive price elasticities and simulate decarbonization scenarios
industry: Industrial sectors in European regions (including energy-intensive industries such as chemicals, basic metals, electricity generation, oil refinery and cokes production)
units: Region-sector combinations at the EU NUTS 2 level
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: den Nijs, S.; Thissen, M.
theories: input–output price modeling; regional resilience theory; revealed competition theory
keywords: Competitiveness; Decarbonization; Economic input–output analysis; Energy efficiency; Fossil fuels; Global value chains; Industrial energy use; Regional economy; Resilience
research_question: To what extent is the competitive position of industries in European regions sensitive to changes in fossil fuel prices, and whether (and how) reductions in gas use along the value chain — through efficiency improvements, electrification, and upstream decarbonization — may increase the resilience of regional industry to energy price shocks.
mechanism_summary: Fossil fuel price shocks translate into differential cost increases across region-sectors via an input–output price model that captures full pass-through along value chains. These cost changes are compared to cost changes faced by the region-sector's spatially differentiated competitors in the revealed competition framework of Thissen et al. (2013), yielding a revealed cost competitiveness elasticity. Decarbonization — modeled as marginal changes in technological coefficients reducing gas use or substituting electricity — shifts the baseline equilibrium and thereby alters this elasticity, so that efficiency gains or upstream decarbonization in the own region, country, or Europe reduce vulnerability, whereas equivalent improvements among competitors raise relative dependency and lower resilience.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a new regional revealed cost competition (RCC) model that fully integrates an input–output price model with the revealed competition measure of Thissen et al. (2013), providing a first-order method for analyzing how cost changes, technological changes, or policies affect the cost competitiveness of industries in specific regions without relying on unknown substitution elasticities. It extends the literature on regional energy resilience beyond supply-side shocks to study price-shock resilience of energy-using industrial sectors through competitive position, and offers a framework for linking industrial ecology arguments about efficiency and decarbonization to regional competitive dynamics in global value chains.
practical_implication: Policy makers aiming to enhance regional resilience to gas price shocks should prioritize efficiency improvements and electrification in industrial production and in upstream sectors such as electricity generation, oil refinery, and cokes production, because these measures reduce vulnerability to future gas price spikes. International cooperation within Europe on upstream decarbonization is warranted, since improvements elsewhere — especially outside Europe where competitors benefit — can instead erode the competitive position of European regions. Avoiding lag relative to competitors in efficiency gains is essential to limit negative effects of future shocks.
limitations: The analysis is a comparative static input–output exercise and does not capture the dynamics of transforming the production process toward a more decarbonized structure, nor first-mover advantages that a dynamic approach could reveal. There are limitations and constraints on the sectors in which technological adjustments can realistically be made. Consistent regional-level time-series data on energy use by type and interregional trade flows are lacking, which would considerably improve the reliability of the analysis and allow studying how resilience changes over time.
future_research: Future work should extend the framework to a dynamic setting that captures the transformation of production processes and first-mover advantages in decarbonization. When consistent time-series data become available, researchers should analyze how regional resilience to fossil fuel price shocks evolves over time. More consistent regional-level data on energy use by type and on interregional trade flows would enable broader applications of the RCC model to other shocks and policies.
abstract: Resilience and competitiveness in relation to fossil energy dependencies is of increasing concern to industries and policy makers. We investigate to what extent the competitive position of industries in European regions are sensitive to changes in fossil fuel prices, and whether reductions in gas use along the value chain may increase resilience of regional industry. A new spatial revealed cost competition model based on the input–output price model is used and calibrated to multi-regional world input–output tables on an EU NUTS 2 level. We obtain elasticities of fossil fuel prices on revealed cost competitiveness and analyse how resilience can be enhanced by increasing efficiency and electrification in production. We show that European regions are resilient to global coal price increases, but are vulnerable to gas price shocks. The transition towards using less gas in production, by efficiency improvements or electrification, can reduce these gas price vulnerabilities. However, when competitors become more efficient instead, the vulnerability to such shocks may increase. Decarbonizing upstream sectors like electricity generation in the own region, own country or in Europe, can increase resilience of downstream industrial sectors in most European regions.
apa_citation: den Nijs, S., & Thissen, M. (2026). Enhancing regional resilience for energy price shocks: Efficient gas use and upstream decarbonization. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00009-z

title: A Review of Systems Perspectives in Sustainability: How Systems Properties Convey Systems-Wide Dynamics
methods: Systematic literature review of 25 years (2000–2024) of empirical sustainability management research across 17 leading management journals; descriptive coding and synthesis of systems properties and systems-wide dynamics
industry: Cross-industry (sustainability management research)
units: Empirical sustainability management journal articles leveraging systems perspectives
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Dentoni, D.; Roglic, M.; Williams, A.; Bansal, P.
theories: complexity theory; panarchy; systems dynamics; systems perspectives
keywords: complexity theory; environmental responsibility; social responsibility; sustainability; systems thinking
research_question: How have sustainability management studies empirically leveraged systems perspectives over the past 25 years, and how can a unified property-based approach help scholars and managers interpret and interfere with systems-wide dynamics that underpin complex social-ecological sustainability problems beyond the organizational level?
mechanism_summary: The authors articulate a property-based approach in which four core systems properties—interrelatedness, nestedness, non-linearity, and emergence—jointly illuminate four systems-wide dynamics (equilibrium, disequilibrium, adaptation, organized systems change) that underpin sustainable or unsustainable outcomes. Interrelatedness and non-linearity surface feedback loops and thresholds/tipping points; nestedness relates organizational action to embedded social-ecological scales; and emergence captures self-organizing, path-breaking processes. Managers can interfere with these dynamics by supporting or contrasting non-linear feedback loops, cultivating/enabling/leveraging emergence, and anticipating unintended ripple effects, thereby linking organizational processes to broader social-ecological systems change.
theoretical_contribution: The review develops a "property-based approach" that synthesizes a fragmented plurality of systems perspectives (e.g., systems dynamics, panarchy, complex adaptive systems, polycentrism) into a unified framework relating four systems properties to four systems-wide dynamics. This framework establishes a basis for scholarly dialogue and theory advancement across multiple systems perspectives, and offers a complementary lens to mainstream management theories such as institutional theory and paradox theory for interpreting emergent, nested, and non-linear dynamics of social-ecological change. It reframes sustainability management away from single-level, cause–effect inquiry toward multi-level, cross-scale theorizing of systems-wide dynamics.
practical_implication: Managers should invest attentional resources beyond the organizational level to recognize whether to maintain equilibrium or accelerate disequilibrium in the face of reinforcing or balancing feedback loops. They should cultivate, enable, and leverage processes of emergence—by creating spaces for new relationships, dialogue, and experimentation across silos—to either restore equilibrium or trigger path-breaking change. Finally, managers should anticipate plausible unintended ripple effects of their interventions, using adaptive and decentralized (e.g., polycentric) approaches to mitigate the risk of making complex societal challenges worse.
limitations: Seeking to fully resolve semantic fragmentation and bring complete conceptual coherence between systems perspectives remained beyond the scope of this review; instead, the authors aimed to bring the plurality into sufficient dialogue for theory advancement. The review is also bounded by its focus on 17 leading management journals and empirical sustainability management studies between 2000 and 2024, and is therefore limited in the systems perspectives and disciplinary traditions it could cover.
future_research: Future empirical work should go beyond single qualitative case studies to include multi-case, quantitative, and mixed methods (e.g., panel data, systems dynamics, agent-based modeling, experiments, multi-level models, Bayesian approaches) to identify inter-organizational and industry-level drivers of systems change. Researchers should narrow geographical and temporal scales—especially at the underrepresented regional scale—to study how organizations interfere with local and regional systems-wide dynamics. Further research should empirically deepen notions of thresholds and tipping points, the organizational practices that cultivate and leverage emergence, and how organizations anticipate and deal with unintended ripple effects.
abstract: As seven of nine planetary boundaries are breached, management scholars face an urgent challenge: how can organizations address complex social-ecological crises that transcend traditional organizational boundaries and objectives? Responding to this need, researchers have leveraged a plurality of systems perspectives, yet current approaches remain nascent and fragmented. In this paper, we review 25 years (2000–2024) of empirical sustainability management research across 17 leading journals. We identify core systems properties—interrelatedness, nestedness, non-linearity, and emergence—that collectively illuminate four critical systemswide dynamics: equilibrium, disequilibrium, adaptation, and organized systems change. This systematic review offers scholars a unified framework for theorizing and addressing critical sustainability challenges facing organizations, society, and the planet.
apa_citation: Dentoni, D., Roglic, M., Williams, A., & Bansal, P. (2026). A review of systems perspectives in sustainability: How systems properties convey systems-wide dynamics. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063261417581
