title: Social entrepreneurship mechanisms for systems change: A structuration approach
methods: Conceptual theory-building paper that develops a structuration model of systems change through social entrepreneurship, using the evolution of microfinance as an in-depth illustrative case and additional illustrative cases of social entrepreneurship that have achieved or failed to achieve systems change
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Krlev, G.
theories: connected modalities (interpretative schemes, resources, norms); duality of agency and structure; structuration theory (Giddens)
keywords: Mechanisms; Social entrepreneurship; Structuration theory; Systems change
research_question: How do social entrepreneurs seek to advance systems change, and when do they succeed or fail to achieve it? More specifically, which mechanisms enable social entrepreneurial agency to translate into structural, systemic transformations in society?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Giddens' structuration theory, the paper argues that agency and structure are recursively constituted through modalities that actors mobilize in practice. Social entrepreneurs drive systems change when they actively combine new interpretative schemes, new resource constellations, and new norms into "connected modalities" rather than relying on a single lever. Such connected modalities simultaneously enable high integrativeness (triggering parallel change across issues and societal levels) and high inclusiveness (activating diverse stakeholders to co-engage), producing a reinforcing interplay between entrepreneurial agency and institutional structure that overcomes the dualism treating structure as a constraint on agency.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "connected modalities" as the central mechanism through which social entrepreneurship produces systems change, reframing the agency-structure relationship in social entrepreneurship as a productive duality rather than a dualism. By applying Giddens' structuration theory, the model bridges the micro-macro divide in social entrepreneurship research and explains why some social entrepreneurs achieve fundamental transformations while others do not. It further reconceptualizes core but poorly theorized features of social entrepreneurship — its capacity to enhance society's ability to act, its reliance on revolving innovation practices, and its characteristic vulnerability — as consequences of the need to continually generate and align connected modalities with evolving stakeholder constellations.
practical_implication: Support for social entrepreneurship should stop mimicking commercial incubation practices, which focus narrowly on viable business models and financial resource mobilization, because these are insufficient for systems change. Entrepreneurial strategy, incubation, and policy should instead train and support ventures to craft and orchestrate connected modalities — combining new interpretative schemes, resources, and norms — and to engage in political brokerage that aligns diverse stakeholders. Policy makers should favor instruments such as crowdsourcing around broadly defined sustainability challenges over standard pitch competitions and innovation prizes, which select for commercial viability rather than inclusiveness and integrativeness.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future work could investigate the relationships between different modalities and their self-perpetuating outcomes, including whether there are optimal combinations, sequences, or numbers of modalities to connect and whether particular "packages" of connected modalities characterize specific industries or issue areas. Researchers could also examine softer discursive and normative forms of bricolage as drivers of innovation, and explore the boundary conditions at which tuning connected modalities to stakeholder agendas, or extending integrativeness to additional societal domains, undermines rather than promotes systems change.
abstract: This article advocates for harnessing Giddens' structuration theory to improve our understanding. I develop a structuration model of systems change through social entrepreneurship, which shows that connected modalities, that is, intricate combinations of (1) interpretative schemes, (2) resources, and (3) norms, act as the key mechanisms that enable systemic effects.
apa_citation: Krlev, G. (2026). Social entrepreneurship mechanisms for systems change: A structuration approach. *Journal of Business Venturing*, 41, 106561. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2025.106561

title: Using Photo-Elicitation to Make Marginalised Voices Heard and Seen in Human Resource Management Research
methods: Methodological observational study presenting two qualitative exemplars of photo-elicitation (PE) in HRM research. Exemplar 1 (participant-driven PE): semi-structured photo-elicit interviews with six Vietnamese women academic leaders in higher education, analysed via Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA) using a seven-step process (transcription, coding, familiarisation, exploratory notes, experimental statements, personal experiential themes, group experiential themes). Exemplar 2 (researcher-driven PE): semi-structured online interviews with 22 trans and gender-diverse (TGD) individuals in Australia, followed by a photo-elicit open-ended questionnaire (n = 42) for HR/managerial personnel using six researcher-generated metaphorical images paired into three randomised sets with modified SHOWED-style questions; analysed via critical grounded theory (CGT) with first- and second-cycle coding, memo-writing, member-checking, and method triangulation.
industry: Higher education (Vietnam) and cross-industry workplaces with HR/diversity/leadership roles (Australia)
units: Individuals: women academic leaders; trans and gender-diverse employees; HR/diversity/leadership managers
n_sample: Exemplar 1: 6 women academics. Exemplar 2: 22 TGD interviewees plus 42 questionnaire respondents.
authors: Ladwig, R. C.; Phuong, J.
theories: Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA); critical HRM / critical HRD (humanism paradigm); critical grounded theory; critical theory; visual methods / photo-elicitation (Harper; Warren; Rose)
keywords: critical human resource development; marginalised groups; photo‐elicit interviews; research method; researcher guidance
research_question: How can photo-elicitation be used as a qualitative HRM research method to make marginalised voices heard and seen, and what practical guidance can researchers draw from participant-driven versus researcher-driven PE approaches? Two sub-questions structure the exemplars: "How do women in academic leadership make sense of their experience in a university in Vietnam?" and "How do trans and gender diverse identities influence individual work engagement and personal career development of TGD individuals in relation to the organisational awareness of gender diversity?"
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: Marginalised voices; material and physical practices of work; power dynamics between researcher and participant; embodied, emancipatory knowledge production.
- PE variants: Participant-driven PE (reflexive photography, auto-driving, photovoice — participants generate images) versus researcher-driven PE (researcher preselects or generates images for stimulated response).
- Process / phases: (1) research design aligned with critical theory and humanism; (2) purposive/snowball sampling of marginalised participants; (3) image generation (participant-produced or researcher-generated metaphorical images); (4) photo-elicit interview or open-ended questionnaire; (5) iterative analysis (IPA seven-step for exemplar 1; critical grounded theory with first- and second-cycle coding plus memo-writing and member-checking for exemplar 2); (6) reflexivity throughout.
- Practices: Member-checking; metaphorical image construction; SHOWED-style questioning adapted to questionnaire; method triangulation; positionality disclosure.
- Boundary conditions / challenges: Ethical issues of ownership, authorship, consent, and privacy; risk of tokenism; interpretive subjectivity; emotional labour; resource intensity; limited small samples.
- Emergent outcomes: "Powershift" from disempowered respondent to equitable research partner; surfacing of tacit, embodied, and symbolic knowledge (e.g., "swimming alone" metaphor for women's leadership journey; metaphorical images of dress code, toilet doors, and team inclusion for TGD experiences); authentic inclusion of marginalised voices in HRM research.

The central process is that PE, by introducing images as a "third party" in the research encounter, unlocks embodied and symbolic communication that verbal interviewing alone cannot access, thereby rebalancing power relations and enabling marginalised participants to co-construct meaning about their material and physical work practices. In the Vietnamese exemplar, participant-chosen photos (e.g., motorbikes, rivers, Don Quixote windmills) functioned as metaphorical anchors that prompted deep reflection on gendered leadership constraints; in the Australian exemplar, researcher-generated metaphorical images protected TGD participants' privacy while still eliciting HR/managerial personnel's taken-for-granted assumptions about cisnormativity. Across both, PE operationalises critical HRM/HRD humanism by foregrounding voices that verbal-centric, Global-North methods systematically under-represent.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers a conceptual contribution to qualitative research methods in HRM by revising and translating PE for HRM scholarship, clarifying the distinction between participant-driven and researcher-driven PE and their differential implications for power dynamics, ethics, and analytic depth. It re-envisions the role of PE in providing holistic insights into the material and physical practices of work, narrowing Kjellstrand and Vince's (2020) noted gap on how to use photos in the OMS research process. By linking PE to critical HRD's humanistic paradigm, the authors position visual methods as an emancipatory tool that challenges meta-narratives of marginalisation in HRM.
practical_implication: HRM researchers are encouraged to adopt PE to access tacit, embodied, and symbolic dimensions of workplace experience — especially for marginalised populations where verbal interviewing may reproduce suppression. The authors provide a practical research-design toolkit (Tables 1-3) covering participant selection, preparation, ethical considerations (consent, ownership, authorship, privacy), data collection structure, analytic frameworks, and reflexivity prompts. For organisations, the exemplars surface actionable insights: Vietnamese higher education institutions should address systemic underrepresentation of women in leadership (7.6% of rectors vs. 50.1% of academics) and the unpaid gendered labour of event organising; Australian employers should improve TGD inclusion through spatial (e.g., toilets, workstations), symbolic (dress code, documentation), and leadership practices.
limitations: Each exemplar faces interpretive challenges: the participant-driven approach raises complex ethical issues of image ownership, joint interpretation, and consent when others appear in photos (leading Jane to describe rather than publish images); the researcher-driven approach introduces researcher subjectivity in image generation, depends on respondents' willingness to engage with metaphors, and is resource-intensive. COVID-19 restrictions forced the Australian study to pivot from photovoice to researcher-generated images. Small sample sizes (6 and 22) limit generalisability, and the online asynchronous questionnaire format precluded follow-up probing.
future_research: The authors call for HRM scholars to experiment with PE and other alternative, non-verbal research methods beyond the Global North's verbal-linguistic norms, to further diversify research engagement and knowledge production. They encourage further work on addressing power dynamics between researcher and participant, refining ethical protocols for visual data (ownership, authorship, consent), extending PE to other marginalised communities, and developing quality indicators for mixed visual-verbal analysis. They also advocate interdisciplinary collaboration to continue narrowing the gap on how visual methods contribute to critical HRM/HRD scholarship.
abstract: Photo‐elicitation (PE) is a qualitative research method that utilises images to obtain a deeper understanding of the perspectives, and beliefs of the research participants. The PE approach can be particularly advantageous for marginalised voices (silenced or underrepresented groups with limited power) by exploring different world views, challenging commonly accepted information, and investigating the material work context. Additionally, it has the potential to impact power dynamics between the researcher and the participants, while promoting collaboration. Consequently, we contend that the use of PE can enhance the representation of marginalised perspectives in human resource management (HRM) and the effectiveness of sustainable transformations in the workplace. To support our assertion, we present our PE research endeavours from a participant‐driven and researcher‐driven PE approach. While there are other instances of PE in HRM studies, this observational study identifies and revises the practical translation of applied method and data analysis supporting researchers in their future PE applications.
apa_citation: Ladwig, R. C., & Phuong, J. (2026). Using photo-elicitation to make marginalised voices heard and seen in human resource management research. *Human Resource Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1748-8583.70033

title: Who Benefits from Alternative Data for Credit Scoring? Evidence from Peru
methods: Observational study using merged administrative and proprietary data (credit card applications, loyalty card transaction data, Peruvian administrative credit registry RCC, and Equifax utility payment score RP2). Machine-learning credit scoring algorithms trained separately for applicants with and without credit history; simulation of credit card approval decisions under alternative business objectives; counterfactual comparisons of approval rates with and without retail data.
industry: Consumer credit / retail (credit card issuer owned by a Peruvian multi-sector company)
time_period: January 2021-September 2023 (loyalty and credit registry); credit card applications from June 2022
units: Individual credit card applicants (with and without formal credit history)
n_sample: Applicants grouped into six cohorts (e.g., Group A: 24,899 approved applicants with credit history; Group C: 1,719; Group D: 1,016; Group F: 10,132 no-history rejected applicants also denied elsewhere)
authors: Lee, J. Y.; Yang, J.; Anderson, E. T.
theories: algorithmic decision-making and fairness; distributional consequences of data; information asymmetry in credit markets
keywords: alternative data; consumer finance; credit cards market; financial inclusion
research_question: Can retail transaction data be used as alternative data to construct credit scores that expand credit access for individuals lacking formal credit histories, and who benefits most from incorporating this alternative data into credit scoring decisions?
mechanism_summary: For applicants without a formal credit history, lenders face severe information asymmetry because conventional socioeconomic and utility-based predictors produce highly overlapping risk score distributions for defaulters and non-defaulters. Retail transaction data adds orthogonal behavioral signals (what and how consumers buy) that sharpen the ability to separate low- and high-risk individuals, which expands the set of applicants whose predicted risk falls below approval thresholds. For applicants already in the credit registry, registry data already captures most repayment-relevant information, so retail data produces only marginal reclassification and may even tighten approval when used to identify higher-risk borrowers inside the existing pool.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to four literatures: the distributional consequences of data and algorithmic decision-making in marketing, the use of alternative data for credit scoring (extending Lee, Yang, and Anderson 2025 beyond grocery to multi-domain retail), the emerging literature on nonfinancial firms entering consumer lending, and information-frictions research in credit markets. It reframes marketing data as a nonmarketing asset whose predictive power is concentrated precisely where traditional financial data is missing, and shows that the welfare effect of alternative data depends on which segment of the applicant pool currently suffers from information asymmetry rather than on data volume per se.
practical_implication: For lenders, retail transaction data is most valuable as a "second look" tool for evaluating applicants who would otherwise be rejected due to missing credit histories, and can nearly triple approval rates for no-history applicants (from 15.6% to as high as 47.8%) depending on risk appetite. For policy makers, the results suggest alternative data can advance financial inclusion goals for unbanked populations, but realizing these gains requires attention to long-term consumer welfare, procedural and distributive fairness, strategic manipulation risks, and privacy safeguards around cross-sector data sharing.
limitations: Selection bias remains because the algorithms cannot account for approved applicants who never activated their cards or for rejected applicants who do not appear in the credit registry. The sample is restricted to individuals who voluntarily enrolled in a single firm's loyalty program, limiting generalizability to the broader applicant pool and excluding people entirely invisible to traditional financial data. The analysis relies on simulated rather than randomized field-experimental approval decisions.
future_research: The authors suggest extending analyses to broader pools of potential borrowers beyond loyalty-program members, conducting field experiments that deploy alternative-data scoring algorithms in production to validate downstream behavioral effects, and investigating how alternative data reshapes organizational practices beyond credit scoring, including marketing targeting, customer acquisition strategies, and post-approval customer service and operations for newly included borrowers.
abstract: The World Bank estimates that 1.4 billion individuals worldwide are unbanked, lacking access to credit due to the absence of traditional credit scores. In this article, the authors demonstrate how retail transaction data can be used to construct an alternative credit score, potentially expanding credit access for these individuals. The study utilizes a unique dataset obtained through a partnership with a Peruvian company. The authors merge customer loyalty data and credit card repayment data with administrative records from the Peruvian ﬁnancial system that provide individuals’ detailed ﬁnancial histories. This comprehensive dataset allows the authors to construct credit scores for people both with and without a credit history. Through simulations of credit card approval decisions, they ﬁnd that incorporating retail data increases approval rates for individuals without a credit history, from 16% to between 31% and 48%. In contrast, for those with an established credit history, approval rates remain largely unchanged, at around 88%. The authors investigate why retail data particularly beneﬁts people without a credit history and discuss the broader implications of this credit scoring methodology for consumers, ﬁrms, and policy makers. The ﬁndings highlight the methodology’s potential to transform credit access for millions of previously unbanked individuals.
apa_citation: Lee, J. Y., Yang, J., & Anderson, E. T. (2026). Who benefits from alternative data for credit scoring? Evidence from Peru. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 63(1), 105-126. https://doi.org/10.1177/00222437251360996

title: Democratic Deviations: How Organizations Sustain Decentralization Commitments in the Face of Centralization Pressures
methods: Qualitative, multiple comparative case study (Yin, 2003; Eisenhardt, 1989, 2021) of four radically decentralized knowledge-intensive firms during the COVID-19 crisis; 64 semi-structured interviews (54 during pandemic, June 2020–January 2022; 10 post-pandemic, August–October 2023) with leaders and workers, triangulated with archival documents (bylaws, governance documents, slide decks, internal meeting notes); grounded, inductive coding with iterative within- and cross-case comparison to build a process model
industry: Knowledge-intensive firms (solar power, enterprise resource planning systems, organization consulting, biotechnology)
units: Four radically decentralized organizations (Energy, Enterprise, Evolve, Plant) with 15–200 employees that had adopted decentralized structures at least two years prior to the pandemic
n_sample: 4 organizations; 64 interviews (19 leaders, 45 workers)
authors: Lee, M. Y.; Young-Hyman, T.
theories: organization design theory; organizational democracy; relational contracts theory; threat rigidity thesis
keywords: crisis; decentralization; new organizational forms; organizational democracy; organizational theory; structure
research_question: How can organizations navigate pulls toward centralization in ways that uphold, rather than undermine, their commitments to decentralization?
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes that structurally similar acts of centralization can produce divergent effects on decentralization commitments depending on the process by which they unfold. Collective authorization by shared democratic rules constrains the scope and duration of centralization, subjects it to oversight, and instills in leaders a sense of accountability that inclines them toward open, transparent enactment; in turn, workers interpret the centralization as a legitimate, bounded, commitment-reinforcing act. By contrast, fiat authorization leaves centralization unbounded and leaders unchecked, weakening legitimacy and producing opaque enactments that workers interpret as a breach of the relational contract. These dynamics are enabled by structural features — multi-level decentralization (operational and collective choice), de facto constraints on owner/leader fiat, and formal democratic rules — that together make decentralization flexible and durable rather than fragile.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a process and structural theory of durable decentralization, showing that preserving commitments to decentralization does not require avoiding centralization but depends on how centralization is authorized and enacted; it identifies two distinct phases (authorization and enactment) and theorizes ‘‘democratic deviation’’ as a process by which organizations can uphold — and even reinforce — decentralization commitments while temporarily centralizing. Structurally, it distinguishes operational-level from collective-choice-level decentralization (drawing on Ostrom), argues that concentrated ownership is compatible with durable decentralization when de facto constraints on leader fiat are in place, and shows that formal democratic rules — rather than informality — make decentralization more flexible and durable. More broadly, it proposes that democratically executed deviations allow organizations to sustain foundational commitments, with implications for corporate purpose, relational contracts, and organizational democracy scholarship.
practical_implication: Leaders of decentralized organizations facing crisis-induced pressures to centralize should not assume they must choose between reclaiming authority and preserving commitments; instead, they can establish and follow formal democratic rules that authorize temporary, bounded centralization and enact that authority openly and transparently. Organizations can strengthen the durability of decentralization by decentralizing collective-choice authority (who decides how authority is distributed), not just operational authority, and by codifying democratic rules for deviation. Such practices help organizations respond flexibly to situational demands without undermining workers' trust, motivation, or legitimacy.
limitations: The study is based on four extreme cases of radically decentralized, knowledge-intensive U.S. firms navigating the singular context of the COVID-19 pandemic, which may have elicited unusual levels of goodwill and deference from workers due to the life-threatening nature of the crisis. The qualitative, inductive design constrains statistical generalizability, and findings may not extend straightforwardly to less-decentralized organizations, other industries, or other types of crises.
future_research: Future scholarship should examine whether the democratic-deviation model extends to less extreme contexts and to situations other than pandemic-induced crises, where workers may be less forgiving of leaders and where the processes of authorization and enactment may be even more consequential. Further work could explore how democratic rules emerge, are revised, and interact with ownership structures, and how democratic deviation applies to sustaining other foundational organizational commitments such as corporate purpose.
abstract: Organizations that have committed to decentralizing authority can face situations in which centralized decision making is useful or necessary. However, centralization risks undermining an organization’s perceived commitment to decentralization, which can threaten employees’ motivation and commitment, as well as organizational legitimacy. Leveraging a comparative case study of four organizations that had explicitly committed to decentralize authority and that subsequently centralized authority during the COVID-19 crisis, we identify a process by which organizations can deviate from decentralization commitments while keeping those commitments in place. This process, which we call ‘‘democratic deviation,’’ involves leaders and workers collectively authorizing centralization and then leaders enacting that authority with openness and transparency. In the three of our four cases that deviated democratically, decentralization commitments were upheld. Conversely, when centralization occurred implicitly through leader fiat and was then enacted opaquely by leaders, a process we label ‘‘monocratic deviation,’’ decentralization commitments were undermined. Through this study, we develop a theory of the structural antecedents and processes of durable commitments to decentralization, prompting re-evaluation of decentralization’s assumed fragility. More broadly, our theory suggests how organizations can uphold shared commitments even when they must deviate from them in the face of situational pressures.
apa_citation: Lee, M. Y., & Young-Hyman, T. (2026). Democratic deviations: How organizations sustain decentralization commitments in the face of centralization pressures. *Administrative Science Quarterly*. https://doi.org/10.1177/00018392261421927

title: Nudging Green-but-Slow Shipping Choices in Online Retail
methods: Two pre-registered, incentivized online experiments conducted on Qualtrics with MTurk participants (screened via CloudResearch). Study 1 used a 2 (logistical context: shipping mode vs. order consolidation) x 6 (information strategy: Baseline, Process, Green Label, Green Process, Green Outcome, Green Outcome-50) x 2 (financial incentive: yes/no) between-subjects design (24 treatments). Study 2 revisited the order consolidation context with 12 treatments and added a delivery-attribute activation measure. Analysis used logistic regression on the binary slow-shipping choice and contemporary causal mediation analysis (probit-based) to test stakeholder-perception mechanisms.
industry: Online retail / e-commerce (consumer electronics and personal-care products used as stimuli)
units: Adult online shoppers (MTurk subject pool)
n_sample: Approximately 3,800 participants across Studies 1 and 2 (Study 1 final n = 2,364)
authors: Lee, Y.; Donohue, K.
theories: operational transparency; salience / cue-driven decision making; signaling; stakeholder theory
keywords: behavioral operations; delivery speed; green shipping; online retail; sustainable logistics
research_question: Which information strategies (Process, Green Label, Green Process, Green Outcome) most effectively nudge online-retail customers to voluntarily choose green-but-slow shipping, and how does the most effective strategy depend on the logistical context (shipping mode vs. order consolidation) and the presence of financial incentives?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on a stakeholder model of customer perceptions (self, society, retailer), the paper argues that information strategies shift slow-shipping choices by raising baseline perceptions on whichever stakeholder dimension is initially low. In the shipping mode context, baseline perceptions of delivery convenience and environmental impact are both low, so an explicit green label is required to signal environmental merit, and adding outcome information (95% CO2 reduction) further amplifies perceived environmental impact. In the order consolidation context, baseline perceptions of self- and society-benefits are already moderate, so process information (fewer boxes, fewer trips) is as persuasive as a green label by activating the saliency of these already-recognized benefits rather than by raising perception levels. Financial incentives substitute for, rather than complement, strategies that already convey a clear green signal.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a stakeholder-based conceptual framework that explains why the relative effectiveness of informational nudges for sustainable shipping is contingent on the logistical context, bridging behavioral operations, operational transparency, and prosocial-behavior research. It is the first study to test process vs. green-label information in isolation and to examine green-but-slow nudging in an order consolidation context. Using contemporary causal mediation analysis, it identifies two distinct psychological mechanisms — perception enhancement (shipping mode) and saliency activation (order consolidation) — through which information strategies operate on customer choice.
practical_implication: Retailers should tailor nudging strategies to their logistical context: for shipping-mode choices, use a green label, ideally paired with outcome information when carbon savings are substantial; for order-consolidation choices, process information ("fewer boxes and fewer trips") is as effective as a green label and avoids potential greenwashing concerns. Financial incentives add little value once a clear green signal is present, but a small incentive combined with a simple green label can help recruit low-eco-consciousness customers into choosing green-but-slow shipping.
limitations: The controlled experimental design, while enabling causal identification and mechanism testing, may yield effect sizes that differ from real-world e-commerce settings; an auxiliary higher-prize-probability experiment showed that baseline slow-shipping preference is sensitive to purchase stakes. Only one small financial incentive level ($1) was tested, and only gain-framed messages were used. The sample is limited to U.S. MTurk respondents and two product categories (consumer electronics and toothbrushes).
future_research: Future work could run field experiments to estimate effect sizes and explore situational factors such as product price and urgency; test loss-framed messages, social-norm cues, and other nudging tactics beyond message content; investigate larger or differently structured financial incentives; and extend the stakeholder-perception framework to other customer-facing logistical decisions such as store pickup versus home delivery or delivery scheduling.
abstract: We first introduce a conceptual framework for selecting information strategies based on their influence on customer perceptions related to key stakeholders in each context: delivery convenience (self), environmental impact (society), and sustainability intention (retailer). Then, drawing on behavioral research and industry practices, we identify and test the effect of a comprehensive set of information strategies (i.e., Process, Green Label, Green Process, and Green Outcome) through controlled experiments involving 3,800 partici­ pants. Experimental results suggest that different logistical contexts require distinct nudg­ ing strategies, aligning with our conceptual framework. We find that including a green label is particularly effective for the shipping mode context, especially when combined with outcome information. In contrast, process information is more promising in the order consolidation context. Also, adding financial incentives can enhance the effectiveness of information strategies without a clear environmental signal, whereas pairing them with a simple green label may be effective for low eco-conscious individuals.
apa_citation: Lee, Y., & Donohue, K. (2026). Nudging green-but-slow shipping choices in online retail. *Management Science*. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2023.00239

title: Physical Disability in the Workplace: A Comprehensive Review and Unifying Framework
methods: Systematic literature review of 168 articles on physical disability in the workplace, drawn from FT50 and ABDC (FT50 publisher) journals using 11 disability-related keywords in Business Source Premier and PsycInfo, plus ancestry and citation searches of seven seminal disability reviews. Two authors independently coded each article across six dimensions (focus, mechanism/outcome, moderators, disability type, methodology, theoretical basis), reconciled disagreements, then synthesized conceptual patterns into a unifying framework building on Stone and Colella (1996).
industry: Cross-industry (management literature)
country: Primarily Europe and United States (>70% of empirical samples)
units: Peer-reviewed management articles on physical disability in the workplace
n_sample: 168 articles
authors: Lee, W. E.; Roccapriore, A. Y.; Lohrke, F. T.; Reutzel, C. R.
theories: Stone and Colella (1996) framework; identity theory; social exchange theory; theory of planned behavior
keywords: disability; diversity; diversity climate; equity; health and well-being; identity; inclusion; intra-organizational processes and dynamics; physical health
research_question: How can the fragmented management literature on physical disability in the workplace be synthesized into a unifying framework, and what constitutes a precise, shared definition of physical disability that addresses the lack of consensus and absence of an integrative framework across prior reviews?
mechanism_summary: Building on Stone and Colella's (1996) seminal framework, the authors integrate focal mechanisms with three categories of workplace outcomes linked to PWPDs, others in the organization, and the organization at large. The framework argues that how PWPDs are treated and how they navigate disclosure and accommodation is shaped by disability attributes and organizational/institutional boundary conditions, which in turn drive heterogeneous individual, interpersonal, and organizational outcomes. The review highlights that aggregation of disability types masks these mechanisms and calls for disaggregated theorization.
theoretical_contribution: The review offers a synthesized and precise definition of physical disability drawing on management, medical, legal, and sociological sources, anchored in the amended ADA (2008), and refines the conceptualization inherited from Stone and Colella (1996). It develops a unifying framework encompassing focal mechanisms, workplace outcomes, and boundary conditions that clarifies the theoretical and methodological fragmentation of PWPD research and identifies underused theoretical lenses (e.g., expectancy violation theory, agency theory) that could advance the field.
practical_implication: Organizations should adopt inclusive hiring practices (bias training, transparent disclosure processes), remove structural and procedural barriers (universally designed workspaces, accessible recruitment tools, streamlined accommodation procedures), and visibly include PWPDs across roles to normalize disability and enhance organizational image. Policymakers should expand support for disability entrepreneurship, incentivize private-sector inclusion, strengthen and extend accommodation mandates to non-standard workers, and subsidize assistive technologies to make accommodation feasible for organizations of all sizes.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future research should examine interpersonal outcomes (e.g., well-being, organizational commitment of coworkers without disabilities interacting with PWPDs), disaggregate disability types rather than aggregating them, and assess how specific disability attributes (severity, visibility, unpredictability, progression) shape outcomes. Scholars should also explore cross-national and cross-organizational differences — particularly outside Europe and the United States — and apply underused theoretical lenses such as expectancy violation theory and agency theory to questions of perception formation, accommodation monitoring, and performance evaluation for PWPDs.
abstract: In the United States, more than a quarter of the working-age population experiences physical disabilities that shape workplace participation and career outcomes. Drawing on 168 articles, we synthesize the management literature to develop a unifying framework of focal mechanisms, workplace outcomes, and boundary conditions. In doing so, we offer a clear definition of physical disability, long absent from management research. We also highlight the fragmented theoretical and methodological landscape, in which most studies aggregate disability types, overlooking critical heterogeneity. Our review charts a path forward by identifying key gaps, outlining directions for future scholarship, and emphasizing the need for stronger theoretical grounding and methodological rigor. Finally, we discuss implications for policy and practice, underscoring how greater inclusion of persons with physical disabilities can advance various workplace outcomes.
apa_citation: Lee, W. E., Roccapriore, A. Y., Lohrke, F. T., & Reutzel, C. R. (2026). Physical disability in the workplace: A comprehensive review and unifying framework. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251406535

title: CEO Activism and Corporate Reputation: How Authenticity and CSR Mitigate Stakeholder Misalignment
methods: Two-study mixed design: Study 1 is a nationally representative survey (n=1050) rating 525 real BLM statements from Fortune 500 companies and Certified B Corps, analysed via OLS regression and structural equation modelling with Sobel tests; Study 2 is a preregistered 2x2 between-subject randomized experiment (n=600) manipulating CEO activism and CSR initiative, analysed via OLS contrasts and mediation analysis.
industry: Fortune 500 companies and Certified B Corps (Study 1); fictitious retail chain 'Supermarket' stimulus (Study 2)
time_period: BLM statements issued largely in late May or June 2020
units: Participants (individual US adults) rating corporate statements
n_sample: Study 1: 1050 participants rating 525 BLM statements; Study 2: 600 participants (150 per experimental group)
authors: Li, L. Z.; Soule, S. A.
theories: cognitive perspective on reputation formation; signalling theory
keywords: CEO sociopolitical activism; corporate social responsibility; moral authenticity; organizational reputation; political ideology; signalling theory
research_question: Under what conditions and through what perceptual mechanisms does CEO activism improve corporate reputation across stakeholders with heterogeneous political ideologies, and how does CSR engagement shape these reputational effects?
mechanism_summary: CEO activism operates as a visible and politically costly signal that deviates from pure shareholder-value focus and risks alienating ideologically opposed stakeholders; this costliness makes the stance credible evidence of the organization's underlying values, which stakeholders interpret as moral authenticity — consistency between espoused values and actions. CSR engagement functions as a congruent, longer-horizon signal of organizational values that reinforces authenticity perceptions when combined with activism, amplifying reputational gains. Because moral authenticity is valued across the ideological spectrum, congruent activism-plus-CSR signals attenuate the reputational penalty that ideologically misaligned stakeholders would otherwise impose, producing a cognitive-interpretive buffer against partisan discounting.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends signalling theory by showing that multiple non-market signals (CEO activism and CSR) interact synergistically when congruent, and integrates the signalling perspective with a cognitive view of reputation formation by introducing stakeholders' political ideology as a previously underexplored interpretive dimension. It reframes CSR not only as a reputation premium but as a reputation buffer that overrides ideological disagreement through perceived moral authenticity, distinguishing this mechanism from Godfrey's moral-capital insurance view by locating the buffering effect in audience heterogeneity and cross-strategy spillovers rather than within-domain wrongdoing.
practical_implication: Leaders considering sociopolitical advocacy can mitigate the risk of alienating ideologically opposed stakeholders by pairing CEO activism with substantive, sustained CSR engagement (e.g., B Corp certification, philanthropic donations), because such congruent signals broaden reputational gains across the ideological spectrum. CSR therefore acts as a reputation buffer that makes activism more viable in ideologically diverse environments.
limitations: Study 1 is correlational and its mediation analysis does not establish causality or rule out alternative mechanisms; the authors did not control for perceptions of inauthenticity or hypocrisy potentially elicited by the Study 2 control; and the CSR initiative was operationalized in a politically neutral domain distinct from the activism stance, which may explain its null direct effect on authenticity. The authenticity mechanism may not generalize to product markets, and experimental vignettes capture only immediate responses from general-public samples rather than diverse organizational stakeholders.
future_research: Examine more contentious and politically costly forms of CSR (e.g., BLM-aligned CSR) to test whether CSR alone can trigger authenticity perceptions in charged domains. Employ non-intrusive methods such as sentiment analysis to trace longitudinal reputational effects in real-world settings, and investigate the antecedents of CEO activism — in particular, why B Corps were less likely than Fortune 500 firms to issue activist statements despite benefiting more from doing so.
abstract: Previous research suggests that the effect of CEO activism depends on its alignment with stakeholders' political ideology. We examine when and why CEO activism can enhance corporate reputation across the ideological spectrum. Integrating signalling theory with a cognitive perspective on reputation formation, we argue that the reputational impact of CEO activism depends on the firm's corporate social responsibility (CSR), which fosters perceptions of organizational authenticity. Using a national survey and an experiment, we find that CEO activism improves corporate reputation through perceived moral authenticity. This effect is stronger when firms hold B Corp certification, engage in philanthropic donations, or are perceived as more socially responsible. Although stakeholders rate firms as more reputable when their political ideology aligns with the CEO's activism, this relationship weakens when firms engage in CSR, thereby signalling authenticity. These findings demonstrate how non-­market strategies interact to shape corporate reputation and inform corporate sociopolitical advocacy among heterogeneous audiences.
apa_citation: Li, L. Z., & Soule, S. A. (2026). CEO activism and corporate reputation: How authenticity and CSR mitigate stakeholder misalignment. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70060

title: Female Equity Analysts and Corporate Environmental and Social Performance
methods: Archival empirical analysis combining hand-collected analyst gender data with E&S ratings; panel regressions with firm and year fixed effects; difference-in-differences around broker closures as a quasi-exogenous shock to female (male) analyst coverage; active-learning fine-tuning of FinBERT for E&S-topic classification on 2.4M analyst reports and 120K earnings call transcripts; Structural Topic Modeling (STM) and Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) for thematic, cognitive, and linguistic analysis; event-study tests of market reactions to analysts' E&S-related tone
industry: Multi-industry publicly listed firms with analyst coverage (Fama-French 12 industries)
units: Firm-year observations; analyst reports; earnings conference calls
n_sample: 20,423 firm-year observations covering 3,567 unique firms; more than 11,000 sell-side equity analysts; 2.4 million analyst reports; 120,000 earnings call transcripts
authors: Li, K.; Mai, F.; Wong, G.; Yang, C.; Zhang, T.
theories: analyst monitoring / governance role of analysts; gender differences in values and preferences (psychology/economics); information intermediary theory
keywords: FinBERT; RepRisk; analyst coverage; analyst monitoring; analyst reports; cognition; corporate environmental and social performance; earnings conference calls; female equity analysts; readability
research_question: Does female equity analyst coverage causally influence firms' environmental and social (E&S) performance, and through what mechanisms—differences in research activities, thematic focus, cognitive and linguistic approaches, and post-report actions—do female analysts exert this influence compared with their male counterparts?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on psychological and economic evidence that women place greater weight than men on the well-being of others, communities, and the environment, the authors argue that female equity analysts—who already constitute a selected, highly skilled group in a male-dominated profession—are more attentive to corporate E&S issues than male analysts. Through their research activities (writing reports, questioning managers on earnings calls), female analysts identify value-relevant E&S risks and communicate them with greater thematic breadth (regulatory compliance, stakeholder welfare, environment), more sophisticated cognitive processing, and greater clarity. These signals exert governance pressure on management because female analysts are also more willing to downgrade recommendations and target prices after negative E&S content, and investors react more strongly to their negative E&S tones. Together these information-production and monitoring channels lead firms with more female analyst coverage to improve their E&S performance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the gender-and-finance literature by showing that female equity analyst coverage causally improves corporate E&S performance, identifying gender diversity among sell-side analysts as an impetus for firms to adopt more environmentally and socially responsible policies. It extends the analyst-monitoring literature by taking a gender lens and uncovering the specific textual, cognitive, and action-based mechanisms through which female analysts influence firm outcomes, thereby explaining observed gender differences in analyst impact first documented by Kumar (2010). Methodologically, it advances the finance/accounting literature on computational linguistics by introducing a data-centric active-learning approach to fine-tune FinBERT on analyst reports and earnings-call transcripts for E&S-topic classification.
practical_implication: The findings imply that firms, brokerages, and regulators interested in strengthening corporate E&S performance should promote gender diversity in the equity analyst profession, since female analysts act as effective external monitors of E&S issues. Investors and asset managers can use female analysts' reports and negative tones as informative signals of firms' E&S risks and are already shown to respond more strongly to them. For corporate management, the evidence suggests that expanding coverage by female analysts increases the likelihood that E&S concerns will be raised publicly and priced into stock recommendations.
limitations: The analysis relies on hand-collected gender inferences and on E&S ratings (e.g., LSEG, ASSET4, KLD, Sustainalytics) that are known to contain measurement noise and inter-rater disagreement. The broker-closure design yields a small, clean treated-control sample, so the economic magnitude of the causal effect should be interpreted with care. The machine-learning pipeline, while extensively validated, still depends on the quality of human-labeled training data and on the coverage of the FinBERT backbone.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: stakeholders, and the environment, in both research reports and earnings conference calls. They also display distinct cognitive and linguistic patterns when discussing E&S issues. Fur­ thermore, female analysts are more likely to issue lower stock recommendations and target prices (lower stock recommendations) following negative E&S discussions in their reports (E&S incidents) than male analysts. Finally, investors respond more strongly to female analysts’ negative tones when discussing E&S issues. Overall, our findings suggest that gender diversity among analysts plays a significant role in shaping corporate E&S practices and provide new insights into the origins of gender differences in skills within the equity analyst profession.
apa_citation: Li, K., Mai, F., Wong, G., Yang, C., & Zhang, T. (2026). Female equity analysts and corporate environmental and social performance. *Management Science*. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2024.06429

title: Organizations as Stages: A Dramaturgical Theory of the Societal Integration of Stigmatized Groups
methods: Conceptual theory development drawing on Goffman's relational perspective on stigma and dramaturgical metaphors; derives a typology of staging forms (concealing and normalizing), identifies structural antecedents, and develops eight propositions
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Lodge, J.; Helms, W. S.; Roulet, T.; Tracey, P.
theories: Goffman's relational perspective on stigma; dramaturgical theory; stigma management
keywords: audiences; dramaturgy; organizations; societal integration; stigma
research_question: How can organizations, conceptualized as "stages," enable the societal integration of stigmatized groups by presenting them to diverse stigmatizing and supportive audiences, and what structural antecedents shape the forms of staging (concealing vs. normalizing) they adopt?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Goffman's relational view, the authors argue that stigma is constituted through audience evaluations rather than being inherent to attributes, so integration depends on how organizations manage audiences. Organizations function as stages that present stigmatized groups in ways that either conceal stigmatized attributes from hostile audiences (reducing sanctions) or normalize them before sympathetic audiences (eliciting acceptance). The likelihood of concealing is driven primarily by the salience of stigmatized attributes, while normalizing is driven by their institutionalization. Because environments are fragmented and contain both stigmatizing and supportive audiences, organizations frequently must engage in both forms of staging simultaneously, generating tensions when visibility from normalizing undermines concealment efforts.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions. First, it reconceptualizes the integration of stigmatized groups as an inherently audience-focused process, departing from prior work that treats integration as a consequence of managing group attributes. Second, it develops a structural model in which forms of staging are driven by contextual antecedents (attribute salience and institutionalization) rather than by managerial preferences alone. Third, it theorizes concealing and normalizing as discrete, potentially simultaneous approaches rather than opposite ends of a continuum, and elaborates the dynamics and tensions that arise when organizations engage in both at once.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that societal integration is not automatically achieved by openly celebrating stigmatized groups; in some contexts, deliberate concealment may be the most effective route to integration, an insight often overlooked in DEI debates that emphasize visibility. Integration outcomes depend on how different audiences evaluate stigmatized groups, so attribute-focused initiatives may fail if audience dynamics are ignored. Stigmatized groups can use the framework to anticipate trade-offs when selecting employers or advocacy partners, and organizations should involve stigmatized groups in co-creating staging approaches tailored to their needs.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future research could investigate the "dark side" of staging—how organizational actions may unintentionally exacerbate sanctions and deepen stigmatization when forms of staging and audiences are misaligned. Scholars could examine the relative weight of the structural antecedents, consider how organizations' own stigma interacts with that of the groups they support, and explore how technology and social-media-driven context collapse amplify or complicate concealing and normalizing activities.
abstract: This paper theorizes how organizations enable the societal integration of stigmatized groups. Drawing on Goffman’s relational perspective on stigma and his dramaturgical metaphors, we conceptualize organizations as “stages” on which stigmatized groups are presented to diverse audiences. We identify two fundamental forms of staging—concealing and normalizing— through which organizations can reduce sanctions from stigmatizing audiences and elicit acceptance among more supportive ones, thereby ultimately enabling societal integration. Our model specifies the structural antecedents that shape the likelihood of each form of staging, offering a nuanced understanding of when organizations adopt them as well as the tensions that may arise from doing so. Based on this, we extend research on organizations’ management of stigma by advancing a dramaturgical theory of the societal integration of stigmatized groups. Whereas prior work has largely assumed that managing the stigmatized attributes of groups is central (and often sufficient) for their broader integration, we demonstrate that integration ultimately depends on the management of audiences and must therefore be understood as an inherently audience-focused process.
apa_citation: Lodge, J., Helms, W. S., Roulet, T., & Tracey, P. (2026). Organizations as stages: A dramaturgical theory of the societal integration of stigmatized groups. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063261421506

title: How Gender Diversity and Equality in R&D Departments Jointly Affect Firm-Level Innovation Through Innovation Capabilities
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis of a balanced sample of corporate R&D departments from Spain's Technological Innovation Panel (TIP, based on the Community Innovation Survey framework) over 2005-2016; generalized structural equation modelling (GSEM, Stata) estimating all paths simultaneously with Probit (binary implementation quality), Poisson (counts for breadth and use of external knowledge), and Poisson with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors for the new-to-the-market sales share; pseudo-fixed effects via pre-sample (2004) values of dependent variables, plus industry, regional, and year dummies; robustness checks include Heckman-style sample-selection correction, negative binomial and fractional logit alternatives, lagged mediators, separate-equation mediation, and alternative gender-diversity indices (entropy, gender ratio)
industry: Manufacturing firms with in-house R&D departments across all sectors of the Spanish economy
units: R&D department-firm-year observations
n_sample: 552 R&D departments; 6,624 firm-year observations
authors: Lokshin, B.; Boone, C.; Post, C.
theories: diversity-as-variety vs. disparity (Harrison & Klein); gender inequality and status hierarchies; information/decision-making perspective on diversity; innovation capabilities / dynamic capabilities
keywords: diversity, equity, and inclusion; innovation and learning; technology and innovation management
research_question: How and when does gender diversity in R&D departments influence firm-level innovation—specifically, through which innovation capabilities (the "how"), and under what conditions of gender rank equality at different hierarchical levels of the R&D department (the "when") does this relationship hold?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the information/decision-making perspective on diversity combined with research on inequality, the authors argue that gender-diverse R&D departments can only convert demographic variety into innovation capabilities when men and women are proportionally represented across hierarchical ranks. Gender rank equality reduces the salience of gender and gender stereotypes, fostering a climate in which complementary knowledge, perspectives, networks, and resources can be voiced and integrated. Under these conditions, gender diversity strengthens external knowledge search, external knowledge use, and implementation-process quality, which in turn raise firm-level innovation. When gender rank inequality is high, by contrast, status-based hierarchies suppress communication and collaboration, attenuating or reversing the diversity-innovation link.
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper identifies firm-level innovation capabilities as the missing mechanism linking R&D department gender composition to firm-level innovation, responding to calls to move beyond upper-echelons explanations and to show how workforce diversity shapes organizational capabilities. Second, it brings inequality-as-disparity back into diversity research by demonstrating that numerical gender diversity cannot be understood without jointly considering the distribution of power and authority across hierarchical ranks, thereby helping to reconcile mixed prior evidence on gender diversity and innovation. Third, it extends theorizing on when gender diversity fosters a climate for constructive collaboration by showing that, in groups with meaningful internal hierarchies, gender rank equality is a necessary enabler of gender-based complementarities—advancing the micro-foundations of knowledge integration and dynamic capabilities.
practical_implication: Managers, HR executives, and diversity professionals should recognize that simply increasing the share of women in R&D departments is insufficient and may even hamper innovation when gender rank inequality persists. To realize the innovation benefits of gender diversity, firms must also ensure proportional representation of men and women at all hierarchical levels (researchers, technicians, and support staff) within R&D. These changes should be paired with holistic, firm-wide practices to translate improved R&D capabilities into new-to-the-market product sales.
limitations: The study measures macro-level innovation capabilities and cannot directly observe the micro-level group processes (e.g., psychological safety, relational conflict) through which gender diversity and rank equality shape capabilities. It captures levels rather than changes in gender rank equality, so it cannot disentangle short-run disruption effects from long-run benefits of structural change toward equality. The sales-based innovation outcome is distal and affected by many factors beyond R&D capabilities, and the secondary TIP data anonymize firms, preventing controls for variables such as TMT gender diversity. Results pertain to Spanish manufacturing R&D departments and may not generalize to other countries with different gender gaps or cultural norms.
future_research: Micro-level research should examine what actually happens inside R&D departments under different combinations of gender diversity and gender rank equality, including psychological safety and additional mediating mechanisms. Longitudinal designs capturing changes in gender rank equality over time could separate short-run adjustment costs from long-run benefits. Scholars should also explore more proximal innovation indicators (e.g., patent applications), test whether the framework generalizes beyond gender to other diffuse-status diversity dimensions (race/ethnicity, age, nationality, disability), and conduct cross-country comparative studies to see how societal gender norms condition the department-level findings.
abstract: Innovation research has long shown that firms depend on their R&D personnel to strengthen their innovation capabilities, sparking scholarly interest in how to best staff R&D departments for competitive advantage, with a focus on gender diversity. However, left unexamined are the mechanisms linking gender diversity in R&D departments and firm-level innovation. Further, despite growing interest in the conditions under which diversity may enhance innovation, researchers have not adequately addressed how department-level gender rank inequality—the disproportionate concentration of men and women at various hierarchical ranks—interacts with numerical diversity to affect innovation outcomes. We address both limitations by integrating insights from the information/decision-making perspective on diversity and research on inequality with research on innovation capabilities. Specifically, we develop and test a model explaining how R&D department gender diversity and gender rank equality jointly affect three firm-level innovation capabilities: breadth of external knowledge search, use of external knowledge, and quality of the innovation implementation process.
apa_citation: Lokshin, B., Boone, C., & Post, C. (2026). How gender diversity and equality in R&D departments jointly affect firm-level innovation through innovation capabilities. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251407611

title: In Defense of an “Ethics Judgment Rule”
methods: Conceptual/normative legal-philosophical argument; doctrinal analysis of Delaware corporate law and the Business Judgment Rule, followed by a normative proposal to incorporate an Ethics Judgment Rule
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Mejia, S.
theories: Business Judgment Rule doctrine; ethics-constrained shareholder value maximization (E-SVM); normative business ethics; shareholder value maximization (SVM)
keywords: Business Judgment Rule; Ethics Judgment Rule; corporate governance; corporate law; shareholder value maximization
research_question: Does the Business Judgment Rule, as commonly understood in Delaware corporate law, actually give corporate leaders the discretion to pursue ethically motivated decisions that are profit-sacrificing, and, if not, how should corporate law be reformed to properly accommodate managers' moral obligations?
mechanism_summary: Mejia argues that although the BJR nominally grants wide deference, its doctrinal tethering to SVM signals to practitioners that courts will only shield ethically motivated decisions when they can be recast as profit-enhancing. This pushes ethically minded leaders either to avoid profit-sacrificing ethical choices or to obfuscate their true motives, which in turn instrumentalizes ethics and corrupts public discourse about the role of morality in business. Incorporating an explicit Ethics Judgment Rule would replace SVM with E-SVM as the standard of review, authorizing good-faith, profit-sacrificing ethical decisions and thereby aligning legal doctrine with the genuine normative structure of corporate moral obligations.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the prevailing business-ethics consensus that the Business Judgment Rule already provides sufficient latitude for ethically motivated corporate conduct, and advances a novel doctrinal proposal—the Ethics Judgment Rule—that reframes the proper standard of managerial review from shareholder value maximization (SVM) to ethics-constrained shareholder value maximization (E-SVM). In doing so, it reconnects normative business ethics with corporate-law doctrine and reconceptualizes corporate success as requiring both profitability and moral integrity.
practical_implication: Adopting an Ethics Judgment Rule would give directors, executives, and managers explicit legal cover to make profit-sacrificing ethical decisions in good faith without fear of liability, freeing them from the pressure to dissemble by recasting moral commitments as profit-enhancing. It would clarify to shareholders, stakeholders, and the broader public that ethics is a non-instrumental constraint on corporate conduct, helping to repair moral discourse in the business community and reorienting conceptions of corporate success toward companies that are both profitable and display moral integrity.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The paper points to the need for further doctrinal work on how an Ethics Judgment Rule could be operationalized within Delaware corporate law, including how courts should evaluate good-faith ethical motivations and how E-SVM should be reconciled with existing fiduciary duties. It also invites continued normative inquiry into the substantive content of corporations' moral obligations and how legal institutions can better articulate the mandatory, non-voluntary dimension of business ethics.
abstract: According to the conventional wisdom among business ethicists, the “Business Judgment Rule” gives corporate leaders the discretion needed to abide by the firm’s moral obligations. In the first part of the paper, I challenge this view: managers have compelling reasons to believe that the Business Judgment Rule (and corporate law more generally) allows corporate leaders to pursue ethically motivated decisions only when these decisions are expected to be profit-enhancing. This is problematic because it instrumentalizes ethics, pushes ethically motivated corporate leaders to dissemble, and corrupts the quality of our public discourse. In response, I propose that corporate law should incorporate ethics into the Business Judgment Rule, explicitly giving managers discretion to make ethically motivated decisions that are profitsacrificing. After responding to concerns about implementing such a rule, I contend that such a rule would be an important step to put corporate ethics in its proper place.
apa_citation: Mejia, S. (2026). In defense of an “Ethics Judgment Rule”. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 1-32. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10102

title: Moral Distress in Bioethics and Business Ethics: Knowledge, Action, and Desire in Moral Conditions and Communities
methods: Conceptual and theoretical analysis; comparative review of bioethics and business ethics literatures; development of a taxonomy of moral conditions based on combinations of moral knowledge, agency, and desire.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Michaelson, C. W.; Jameton, A.; Liaschenko, J.
theories: moral community; moral distress theory; moral philosophy; moral psychology
keywords: bioethics; business ethics; decision-making; moral community; moral distress; nursing ethics
research_question: Why is moral distress — a central concept in bioethics — largely absent from business ethics scholarship, and how can importing and extending it illuminate important problems of business ethics and the role of moral communities in shaping moral action?
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes that ethical outcomes at work arise from the joint configuration of three elements — whether an agent knows the right thing to do, has the agency to act on it, and desires to do so. Different combinations yield a taxonomy of eight moral conditions, with moral distress defined as knowing and desiring the right action yet lacking agency due to institutional constraints. In business, the tension between profit maximization and other moral purposes makes moral distress an ever-present, existential condition that is often reframed as moral rationalization. Morally supportive communities — characterized by shared moral purpose, opportunities for voice, and loyalty — can align knowledge, action, and desire and mitigate distress across both medicine and business.
theoretical_contribution: The article bridges bioethics and business ethics by importing moral distress into business ethics discourse and reframing it as an existential condition of modern business rather than an exceptional event. It develops a novel taxonomy of eight moral conditions based on combinations of moral knowledge, agency, and desire, and theorizes morally supportive communities as a mechanism for cultivating right desires and aligning them with moral knowledge and action.
practical_implication: The authors suggest that organizations in healthcare, business, and beyond should cultivate morally supportive communities that articulate a common moral end beyond profit, remove institutional constraints that impede workers' ability to act on their moral convictions, and create conditions for voice and loyalty so that moral knowledge, agency, and desire can be aligned.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The paper invites further work applying moral distress to additional problems of business ethics, extending business ethics thinking on stakeholder relationships and moral agency back into bioethics, and exploring how different ethical theories apply to distinct relationships (e.g., patients and customers, employees and managers, providers and payers) across healthcare and business settings.
abstract: “Moral distress” was introduced in nursing ethics to describe the experience of having the moral conviction about the right thing to do while having limited agency to enact it. It exists at the intersection of moral philosophy, moral psychology, and moral communities that influence our desires to act. Although moral distress has significantly impacted bioethics scholarship, it has had almost no presence in business ethics scholarship. We argue that moral distress is useful for understanding important problems of business ethics. We claim it may be missing from business ethics discourse not because it is not present but rather because it is ever-present, an existential condition brought on by the tension between profit maximization and other moral purposes. We consider how the moral communities of medicine and business can be morally supportive or distressing and set forth a taxonomy of moral conditions involving the relationship between knowledge, action, and desire.
apa_citation: Michaelson, C. W., Jameton, A., & Liaschenko, J. (2026). Moral distress in bioethics and business ethics: Knowledge, action, and desire in moral conditions and communities. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 1–25. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10097

title: The effects of food insecurity on work outcomes.
methods: Multimethod design across three studies: (1) a preregistered online recall experiment on Prolific (N = 375) manipulating food insecurity; (2) a four-wave within-person diary study on Prolific (N = 567) modeling within- and between-person effects via multilevel path analysis; (3) a randomized field experiment in Lahore, Pakistan (N = 196) partnering with a local NGO to distribute food versus matched nonfood packages, analyzed with path analysis in Mplus and 10,000-draw bootstrap for indirect effects.
industry: Mixed employed adults (Studies 1-2: U.S. working adults recruited via Prolific, 31+ hrs/week); Study 3: low-income daily-wage workers served by a Pakistani NGO
units: Individual employees
n_sample: Study 1 N=375; Study 2 N=567; Study 3 N=196
authors: Moy, J. H.; Khan, U. A.; Ong, W. J.; Barnes, C. M.
theories: behavioral inhibition system; reinforcement sensitivity theory
keywords: anxiety; food insecurity; organizational citizenship behavior; task performance; work engagement
research_question: How does food insecurity—insufficient access to safe and nutritious foods—affect core workplace outcomes (task performance, work engagement, and organizational citizenship behaviors), and through what psychological mechanism? Can providing food aid remediate these effects for food-insecure employees?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on reinforcement sensitivity theory (Gray, 1978, 1982; Gray & McNaughton, 2003), the authors argue that food insecurity constitutes a diffuse, nonacute threat that activates the behavioral inhibition system and produces anxiety. Because cognitive capacity is limited, anxiety redirects attention toward monitoring and resolving the threat, leaving fewer attentional and cognitive resources available for work. This depleted bandwidth undermines task performance (which requires focused information processing), work engagement (which requires sustained mental immersion), and OCB (which requires noticing and responding to others' needs). Providing food aid is therefore expected to reduce anxiety and indirectly improve work outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends applied psychology into the grand challenge of food insecurity, challenging the field's implicit WEIRD-centric assumption that food is not a workplace-relevant stressor. It contributes to the food insecurity literature by moving beyond personal and physiological outcomes to show organizational consequences, building a business case for corporate engagement. It also enriches the workplace anxiety literature by identifying a stressor unique to economically disadvantaged workers struggling to meet basic physiological needs.
practical_implication: Organizations have an evidence-based rationale to address employee food insecurity because it improves task performance and engagement through reduced anxiety. Employers can provide free or discounted food, partner with local food banks, host on-site pantries or community gardens, offer transportation to food banks (especially in food deserts), and educate employees about public food-assistance programs such as SNAP. Investing in employees' basic needs may yield higher returns than programs targeting higher-order needs (e.g., meditation, employee resource groups).
limitations: In Study 3, only indirect (not direct) effects of food insecurity on work outcomes were significant, possibly because Pakistan's broader anxiety-inducing context diluted the food-specific signal and a single food package may have been insufficient to substantially reduce food insecurity. The studies rely heavily on self-reported measures, raising common-method concerns (though mitigated by the manipulated IV in Study 3 and within-person design in Study 2). The active nonfood control condition in Study 3 may have created expectancy effects. The research examined only three focal outcomes and did not capture other work-relevant consequences such as turnover or absenteeism.
future_research: Future work should probe boundary conditions of food insecurity's effect on OCB—e.g., whether food-insecure workers extend more citizenship toward similarly situated colleagues than toward others. Researchers could test alternative or additional mediating mechanisms beyond anxiety, such as negative affect or regulatory focus, to map a more complete nomological network. Field experiments should also explore dose-response effects by varying the number and size of food packages, and examine downstream outcomes like turnover and absenteeism.
abstract: Despite its prevalence and importance, the ﬁeld of applied psychology has ignored this problem with the implicit assumption that food insecurity is not an issue relevant to the workplace. Contrary to this view, we hypothesize and demonstrate that food insecurity is detrimental to the three core work outcomes: task performance, work engagement, and organizational citizenship behaviors. To test our hypotheses, we employed a multimethod design across three empirical studies: (a) an experimental recall study, (b) a within-person diary study, and (c) a ﬁeld experiment. Our ﬁndings consistently demonstrate that food insecurity leads to higher anxiety, which subsequently has a negative effect on task performance, work engagement, and organizational citizenship behavior. In our ﬁeld experiment, we further demonstrated that providing food to those experiencing food insecurity can improve work outcomes. This research highlights that food insecurity is a signiﬁcant issue with important implications for the workplace that requires greater attention from both researchers and business leaders.
apa_citation: Moy, J. H., Khan, U. A., Ong, W. J., & Barnes, C. M. (2026). The effects of food insecurity on work outcomes. *Journal of Applied Psychology*. Advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1037/apl0001361
