title: A CEO-Driven Process Model of Firm Responsiveness to Secondary Stakeholder Demands: The Roles of CEO Values and Passion
methods: Conceptual theory-building paper that develops a CEO-driven process model of firm responsiveness to secondary stakeholder demands, integrating stakeholder theory, strategic leadership/upper echelons research, and social-psychological work on values and passion, and illustrating the arguments with brief real-world vignettes (e.g., Trader Joe's vs. Costco/Publix, MEC/CamelBak, Starbucks, Chick-fil-A)
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Neville, F.; Rizzi, G. A.; Lovelace, J. B.
theories: Schwartz values theory; dualistic model of passion (harmonious vs. obsessive); stakeholder theory (salience); strategic leadership / executive cognition; upper echelons theory
keywords: CEO cognition; CEO passion; CEO values; secondary stakeholders; strategic leadership; upper echelons
research_question: What is the process by which a CEO's values and passion shape firm responsiveness to secondary stakeholder demands, and how do firms—and specifically CEOs—come to construct and enact divergent responses to similar, if not identical, secondary stakeholder demands?
mechanism_summary: The model argues that because secondary stakeholder demands arise in situationally weak, value-laden, and emotionally charged contexts, CEOs' personal values and passion — rather than purely rational stakeholder-salience calculations — drive firm responsiveness. Values determine which demands CEOs attend to and deem salient (what they engage with and why), while passion determines the intensity, persistence, and emotional investment with which CEOs engage (how). Harmonious passion tends to produce flexible, reflectively committed responses, whereas obsessive passion can yield rigid, defensive, or escalatory responses even when detrimental to the firm. Because audiences interpret responses through the lens of the leader, values- and passion-infused responses shape divergent social (dis)approval outcomes across otherwise similarly situated firms (multifinality).
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions. First, it enriches stakeholder theory by incorporating top-executive characteristics — specifically CEO values and passion — into the conceptualization of when and whether a secondary stakeholder demand is perceived as salient and acted upon, moving beyond frameworks developed primarily for primary stakeholders. Second, it advances strategic leadership and Upper Echelons Theory by disentangling values from cognitive bases and specifying the distinctive socio-cognitive and motivational processes through which values (what CEOs engage with) and passion (how intensely they engage) jointly shape executive choice. Third, it offers a micro-foundational lens on firm social (dis)approval by explaining how CEO values and passion shape both firm responses and how audiences interpret those responses, accounting for why similarly situated firms can elicit divergent social evaluations.
practical_implication: Strategic leaders should cultivate greater self-awareness of their natural tendencies to inject personal values and passion into decision-making about secondary stakeholder demands, because such reflexivity can help them evaluate these demands more effectively and avoid responses inconsistent with firm objectives or primary-stakeholder priorities. Boards and top management teams should monitor the extent to which a CEO's personal value commitments and obsessive versus harmonious passions are driving firm responses, and create processes for surfacing value-based assumptions before commitments become hard to reverse. Training and coaching interventions can help executives distinguish harmonious from obsessive passion so that value-driven responses remain flexible and compatible with broader firm strategy.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for empirical tests of the model using CEO primary data (e.g., Schwartz Value Survey; Vallerand et al.'s passion scale; Cardon et al.'s entrepreneurial passion measure adapted to CEOs), as well as AI-based measurement approaches that apply NLP, sentiment trajectory modeling, and high-arousal language detection to CEO speeches, interviews, earnings calls, and social media to infer values and passion at scale. They suggest verbal protocol ("think aloud") studies in which CEOs or proxies respond to realistic secondary-stakeholder scenarios, and laboratory experiments in which participants assuming stakeholder roles evaluate alternative response postures to trace social (dis)approval and competitive-dynamics consequences. Future work could also examine how CEOs' personal values form a basis for firms' dynamic political management capabilities and non-market competitive advantage.
abstract: Secondary stakeholder demands represent increasingly important strategic issues for firms. However, the principal drivers of firm response remain poorly understood. We extend research on firm responsiveness to secondary stakeholders by introducing a new theoretical perspective to detail the cognitive processes through which key CEO attributes—namely, personal values and passion—shape the trajectories of firm responsiveness. We first outline key aspects of secondary stakeholder demands to explain why CEO values and passion are important determinants of a firm’s response to secondary stakeholder demands. We then explain how a CEO’s values and passion influence the process of firm responsiveness. Finally, we extend our arguments to illustrate how values and passion-infused responses may influence social (dis)approval among key organizational stakeholders. By focusing on the cognitive processes of CEOs, we offer novel theoretical contributions to stakeholder theory, strategic leadership research, and the literature on firm social evaluations.
apa_citation: Neville, F., Rizzi, G. A., & Lovelace, J. B. (2026). A CEO-driven process model of firm responsiveness to secondary stakeholder demands: The roles of CEO values and passion. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063261416425

title: Leadership and the Virtue of Humanity: Conceptual Clarity, Systematic Review, and Future Research Agenda
methods: Conceptual theorization plus PRISMA 2020 systematic review of empirical leadership studies in Web of Science; title search conducted July 2024 yielding 60 included studies synthesized narratively across six virtuous dimensions.
industry: Not reported in paper
time_period: No year restriction; Web of Science title search conducted July 2024
units: Empirical academic journal articles (English-language) on leadership and a virtuous dimension of humanity
n_sample: 60 studies (from 183 records screened)
authors: Newstead, T.; Loghman, S.; Newman, A.; Riggio, R.
theories: Aristotelian virtue ethics; higher-order virtues framework; relational leadership / co-creation of leadership; virtue ethics
keywords: compassion; empathy; forgiveness; generosity; humanity; kindness; leadership; love; virtues
research_question: How should the higher-order virtue of humanity be conceptually defined, which lower-order virtues qualify as its virtuous dimensions, and what is empirically known about each of these dimensions in relation to leadership?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Humanity is a universal, higher-order virtue comprised of — but irreducible to — a set of lower-order virtuous dimensions that together enable meaningful human connection and sustain human community.
- Key constructs: empathy (primarily affective/cognitive), compassion, forgiveness, love (equally affective, cognitive, and behavioral), kindness, and generosity (primarily behavioral).
- Criteria (derived from Aristotelian virtue ethics) used to qualify a lower-order virtue: it must be inherently good, habituated, expressed in relation to others, and represent a golden mean between opposing vices.
- Processual model: leaders' and followers' expression of the six virtuous dimensions mutually shapes the relational co-creation of leadership, which in turn sustains human community (its telos).
- Boundary conditions: situations may demand other higher-order virtues (e.g., courage, justice); the effect of humanity on leader-follower relations is expected to vary with context.

The paper's processual argument is that when leaders and followers reciprocally enact empathy, compassion, forgiveness, love, kindness, and generosity, these dimensions operate in concert rather than isolation, producing the fullest expression of humanity as a higher-order virtue. Grounded in Aristotelian virtue ethics and Newstead's (2022) higher-order virtues framework, the authors argue that humanity — as the golden mean between cruelty and sycophantism — is habituated through practice and is uniquely suited to sustaining organizations through polycrisis because it is the virtue of meaningful human connection itself.
theoretical_contribution: The paper resolves conceptual ambiguity around the virtue of humanity by providing a robust definition, theorizing humanity as a higher-order (meta) virtue, and distilling virtue-ethics criteria for determining which lower-order virtues qualify as its virtuous dimensions. It advances a processual model of leader and follower humanity and, through a PRISMA 2020 systematic review of 60 empirical studies, consolidates what is known about each dimension in relation to leadership and articulates a structured future research agenda.
practical_implication: The authors argue that managers and HR practitioners should consider the virtuous dimensions of humanity in recruitment, selection, promotion, career development, training, and performance management — for example, by hiring, rewarding, and promoting individuals who demonstrate the capacity to lead in ways that express empathy, compassion, forgiveness, love, kindness, and generosity, because doing so promises to help sustain organizations through turbulent times and for the long term.
limitations: The authors identify three limitations in the body of reviewed research: a dearth of empirical work on several dimensions (love, kindness, and especially generosity, for which no empirical leadership studies were found), an overreliance on cross-sectional and self-report data prone to social desirability bias, and a pronounced leader-centrism that neglects follower perspectives and the relational co-creation of leadership. The review itself was restricted to published, English-language empirical articles indexed in Web of Science and relied on title-only search terms.
future_research: The authors propose exploring the outcomes and mechanisms (e.g., social exchange, reciprocity norms, emotional contagion, social identity, psychological safety climate) through which the less-studied dimensions — love, kindness, and generosity — influence workplace outcomes, and addressing leader-centrism by examining follower and relational expressions of humanity. They call for longitudinal, experience-sampling, and experimental designs to move beyond cross-sectional self-report, psychometric work to develop a valid higher-order measure of humanity, articulation of boundary conditions, and replication of the theorization exercise for the other higher-order virtues (courage, justice, prudence, temperance, transcendence, wisdom).
abstract: Humanity – the virtue enabling meaningful human connection – is vital to the leadership we need to survive our polycrisis context. As a prerequisite to sustainable human community, the virtue of humanity is considered universal. It has been claimed as a 'higher-order virtue', comprised of and enacted by – but irreducible to – a suite of 'lower-order virtues' such as compassion and kindness. This claim is evident in widely-adopted virtues catalogues, but we lack consensus on how humanity is defined, and which attributes qualify as its virtuous dimensions. To address this, we develop a robust definition of humanity and apply criteria distilled from virtue ethics to theorize the lower-order virtues that comprise its virtuous dimensions. We thus determine the virtuous dimensions of humanity include empathy, compassion, forgiveness, love, kindness, and generosity. Our processual model illustrates how, when expressed by leaders and followers, these virtuous dimensions represent the fullest expression of the higher-order virtue of humanity and sustain human community. Our systematic review details what is known empirically about each virtuous dimension of humanity in relation to leadership. Drawing on this, we propose a research agenda to advance understanding of how the virtue of humanity might enable the leadership needed today and into the future.
apa_citation: Newstead, T., Loghman, S., Newman, A., & Riggio, R. (2026). Leadership and the virtue of humanity: Conceptual clarity, systematic review, and future research agenda. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70064

title: Investments that make our homes greener: The role of regulation
methods: Archival empirical analysis using the near-universe of Energy Performance Certificates (EPCs) for residential properties in England and Wales merged with Rightmove rental listings; difference-in-differences (DiD) comparing pre/post-MEES regulation rents for below- vs. above-threshold properties with property and time fixed effects; instrumented difference-in-differences (IVDiD) using pre-regulation EPC status as instrument for post-regulation energy efficiency; characterization of retrofits (type, capex, lifespan, energy savings, internal rates of return); carbon-footprint analysis with updated fuel emission factors
industry: Residential real estate (private rental and owner-occupied sectors)
time_period: October 2008 through post-2015 MEES regulation period
units: Residential dwellings (Energy Performance Certificates) and property-level rental listings
n_sample: Approximately 14 million unique dwellings in the EPC dataset; hundreds of thousands of fuel-switching observations (e.g., 404,135 gas-to-gas, 113,879 electricity-to-electricity, 45,399 electricity-to-gas)
authors: Clara, N.; Cocco, J. F.; Naaraayanan, S. L.; Sharma, V.
theories: energy-efficiency gap / market-failure theory; regulatory risk in climate finance; split incentives (landlord-tenant agency) theory
keywords: Climate finance; Energy efficiency; Household finance; Real estate
research_question: What are the magnitude, nature, and financial returns of the investments triggered by a regulatory intervention requiring privately rented properties to meet minimum energy-efficiency standards, and to what extent are landlords compensated through rents for the capital expenditures they incur?
mechanism_summary: Building on the energy-efficiency gap and split-incentive literatures, the paper argues that in England and Wales utilities are paid directly by tenants, so landlords cannot internalize energy-bill savings from retrofits and — absent regulation — under-invest in energy efficiency. When mandated by MEES, landlords comply by selecting low-capex retrofits with the highest IRRs, but the DiD and IVDiD estimates show that they are unable to raise rents enough to recover these costs, confirming the friction. Because EPCs score properties on a cost-based metric that combines fuel quantities and prices, regulation incentivizes shifts toward cheaper rather than cleaner fuels, so energy-efficiency gains in the rental sector do not translate into commensurate reductions in carbon emissions, especially once outdated carbon factors are used.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides large-sample evidence on the types, capital costs, and internal rates of return of residential energy-efficiency retrofits undertaken by both homeowners and landlords, informing the climate-finance and energy-efficiency-gap literatures on barriers to private investment in decarbonization. It quantifies the cash-flow (rent) effects of a major energy-efficiency regulation and shows that landlords are not compensated for their capital expenditures, clarifying the nature of regulatory risks faced by real estate investors. It further demonstrates that cost-based energy-efficiency metrics can misalign private investment with decarbonization goals, distinguishing energy-efficiency from environmental-impact improvements.
practical_implication: If energy-performance certificates and regulations are to be used to tackle climate change, they should report and target carbon emissions directly rather than cost-based efficiency metrics, and carbon factors should be updated regularly to reflect the decarbonization of electricity generation. Policymakers should recognize that landlord-tenant split incentives limit the pass-through of energy savings into rents, so mandating energy-efficiency upgrades will likely impose uncompensated costs on landlords and bias their compliance toward low-capex retrofits. Interventions should also coordinate consumption-side efficiency requirements with production-side decarbonization policies.
limitations: The analysis is confined to England and Wales and to the specific MEES regulatory regime, so external validity to other jurisdictions and regulatory designs is uncertain. Carbon-footprint recalculations using updated emission factors are constrained by the EPC data, which do not allow the authors to split energy consumption by fuel type for multi-fuel properties. Estimated internal rates of return rely on the EPC-projected energy savings and lifespans, which may diverge from realized outcomes.
future_research: Further work could examine whether regulations that target carbon emissions directly, rather than cost-based energy-efficiency metrics, deliver larger decarbonization benefits in residential real estate. Researchers could also study the interaction between consumption-side building regulations and production-side decarbonization of electricity, and extend the rent pass-through analysis to other countries and tenure arrangements. Additional studies could assess how landlord responses evolve over longer horizons as compliance thresholds tighten.
abstract: We study the investments triggered by a regulatory intervention requiring rented properties to satisfy minimum energy efficiency standards.
apa_citation: Clara, N., Cocco, J. F., Naaraayanan, S. L., & Sharma, V. (2026). Investments that make our homes greener: The role of regulation. *Journal of Financial Economics*, 177, 104236. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfineco.2026.104236

title: Beyond Rugged Individualism?: Exploring the Resilience of Black Entrepreneurs to Chronic Racism
methods: Qualitative, decolonizing research design combining Group Model Building (GMB) workshops with semi-structured interviews. Data collection unfolded in four phases: (1) semi-structured Zoom interviews with 49 Black entrepreneurs (March-June 2023); (2) a first full-day GMB workshop in July 2023 with 12 entrepreneurs and 7 policymakers to co-construct causal loop diagrams of the ecosystem shaping resilience; (3) iterative refinement of the process model; and (4) a second validation workshop. Analysis integrated critical race methodology and critical race theory to foreground participants' lived experiences of racism-related stress and build an emergent process model of resilience to chronic racism.
industry: Diverse small businesses (retail, food service, salons, construction, professional services, creative industries, etc.)
time_period: March 2023 - July 2023 (data collection); reflections spanning COVID-19 pandemic and earlier business history
units: Self-identified Black entrepreneurs with no more than 40 employees who had experienced challenges such as gentrification, recessions, natural disasters, racial unrest, or COVID-19
n_sample: 49 Black entrepreneurs interviewed (10 immigrants from majority-Black countries in Africa and the Caribbean; remainder African American); 12 entrepreneurs plus 7 policymakers participated in the GMB workshop
authors: Nurse, S.; Dasent, K.; Rivera, A.; Ansah, J. P.; Black, J.
theories: critical race methodology; critical race theory; socio-ecological theory of resilience
keywords: black entrepreneur; chronic adversity; entrepreneurship; racism; resilience
research_question: How do individual capacities and systemic factors interact to shape the resilience of Black entrepreneurs in the context of chronic racism, and to what effect?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the socio-ecological theory of resilience and Critical Race Theory, the authors argue that Black entrepreneurs operate within systems built on White normative standards that provide lower foundational institutional support and, combined with in-group discrimination, limit external resources. Because racism is chronic rather than episodic, entrepreneurs cannot simply "bounce back" to a pre-adversity state; instead they must engage in continuous adaptation that repeatedly draws on internal coping mechanisms (self-reliance, hard work, faith). Over-reliance on these personal resources, triggered by the insufficiency of external support, leads to resource depletion, exhaustion, and fluctuating phases of well-being and duress. Strategic partnerships (co-ethnic and cross-racial) can replenish resources and enable periodic disengagement and re-entry, rendering resilience a cyclical, dynamic process whose outcomes are both enabling and costly.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the dominant "bounce-back" conception of resilience and theorizes resilience to chronic racism as a continuous, cyclical process of adaptation in which internal and external coping resources are gained and depleted over time. By integrating critical race theory and critical race methodology with the socio-ecological theory of resilience, it foregrounds racism-related stress as a core construct in entrepreneurship research and shows how insufficient institutional and social support compels over-reliance on individual coping, exposing a grimmer, compensatory face of resilience. In doing so, it advances efforts to decolonize entrepreneurship scholarship, moving beyond race-neutral framings and offering a process model that may transfer to other groups facing chronic, identity-linked adversity.
practical_implication: The authors advocate a dual-strategy policy approach that simultaneously strengthens internal capabilities and addresses systemic barriers. Internally, they recommend building Black-entrepreneur-focused business-to-business networks and cross-group mentorship and sponsorship programs, alongside financial vehicles such as credit unions, Community Development Financial Institutions (CDFIs), and cooperative economic practices (e.g., sousous). Systemically, they call for transparent, race-conscious policies, greater representation of Black individuals and allies in decision-making, disaggregated data on grants and loans by race, and streamlined MWBE certification and government contracting processes to remove biases; the research team also partnered with a CDFI to launch a networking and referral program with forgivable loans as a tangible benefit to participants.
limitations: The sample is drawn predominantly from the Northeast United States and over-represents women, which may constrain the regional and demographic generalizability of the findings. The emergent theory of resilience to chronic racism may be transferable to other marginalized groups facing chronic, identity-linked adversity, but its applicability should not be assumed without careful contextual consideration, as marginalized groups are heterogeneous. The study did not specifically theorize the experiences of Black immigrant entrepreneurs, whose relationship to racism and community support differs from that of African American participants.
future_research: Future work should conduct similar socio-ecological, decolonizing studies with other marginalized groups (e.g., Latinx, women, refugee, or low-income entrepreneurs) to test the transferability of the process model and identify group-specific dynamics. Researchers should examine Black immigrant entrepreneurs in greater depth, including how ethnic-enclave community support reduces reliance on individual coping, and explore other intersectional identities. Empirical testing of specific elements of the resilience process model — particularly the compensatory dynamics between internal and external coping resources — could refine the theory and inform more tailored interventions.
abstract: This study investigates how the resilience process unfolds for Black entrepreneurs in the context of chronic racism, employing a novel qualitative approach that combines Group Model Building (GMB) and semi-­structured interviews with 49 Black entrepreneurs. Drawing on the socio-­ecological theory of resilience and leveraging Critical Race Theory (CRT), the research finds that resilience, shaped by the persistent nature of racism, requires ongoing adaptation rather than a return to a pre-­adversity state. This continuous adaptation can lead to the depletion of coping resources. The study also illustrates how internal and external coping mechanisms interact, showing that over-­reliance on internal coping mechanisms arises due to insufficient institutional and social support. Our research contributes to the literature on Black entrepreneurship, resilience, and race in entrepreneurship, while offering a comprehensive policy approach to both support and empower Black entrepreneurs. We advocate for decolonizing research practices that not only study but actively benefit the communities involved, fostering engaged and transformative scholarship.
apa_citation: Nurse, S., Dasent, K., Rivera, A., Ansah, J. P., & Black, J. (2026). Beyond rugged individualism?: Exploring the resilience of Black entrepreneurs to chronic racism. *Journal of Management Studies*, 63(1), 162-194. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.13211

title: Carbon Populism and Representative Politics: On Why Fossil Fuel Firms Speaking for ‘The People’ Is a Bad Idea
methods: Qualitative case study of the Australian fossil fuel industry combining semi-structured interviews and document analysis. Data sources: 77 semi-structured interviews (2019-2023) with fossil fuel executives, lobbyists, industry association leaders, politicians (including two former Prime Ministers and a former State Premier), environmental NGO representatives, trade union officials, and industry analysts; 29 industry-association media releases; 57 ministerial press releases/speeches; 133 Hansard records; and 262 media items from The Australian, Sydney Morning Herald, ABC News, The Guardian Australia, and Sky News Australia (total 481 documents). Factiva/Hansard keyword searches on ‘climate change’ and ‘fossil fuel’ over 2009-2024 returned an additional 207 parliamentary documents and 311 media items. Three-stage coding in QSR NVivo: (1) open coding of actors, discourses, phenomena, practices; (2) coding of Saward's (2006, 2010) representative-claim components (maker, subject, object, audience); (3) alignment with political-theory constructs to identify six forms of political representation.
industry: Fossil fuel industries (mainly coal and gas), plus energy generation/retail, industry associations, politics, environmental NGOs, and trade unions
units: Individuals (interviewees) and texts (industry media releases, parliamentary speeches, Hansard records, newspaper articles)
n_sample: 77 interviews; 481 documents (29 industry media releases, 57 ministerial press releases, 133 Hansard records, 262 media items); plus 207 parliamentary documents and 311 media items from keyword searches
authors: Nyberg, D.; Wright, C.; Bowden, V.; Fleming, P.
theories: Corporate Political Activity (CPA); Political Corporate Social Responsibility (PCSR); political representation as claim-making (Saward 2006, 2010); populism theory (Laclau and Mouffe; Moffitt); public sphere / political sphere (Habermas); representative politics / constructivist turn (Saward; Disch; Castiglione and Pollak)
keywords: climate change; corporate political activity; fossil fuel industries; nationalism; populism; representative politics
research_question: How do industries seek to represent ‘the people’ and what are the implications for political decision-making? Specifically, how do fossil fuel industries in Australia construct and represent ‘the people’ in (a) the political sphere where collective and binding decisions are made, and (b) the public sphere where common concerns are deliberated?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: Carbon populism; representative claim-making (Saward's maker-subject-object-audience schema); political sphere vs. public sphere; national identity; artificial/hegemonic representation.
- Forms of representation in the public sphere: (1) Simulated representation — industry acts ‘as’ the people (e.g., Glencore-funded astroturfing campaigns like ‘Energy in Australia’); (2) Symbolic representation — industry acts ‘on behalf of’ the people through advertising, public relations blitzes, and regional jobs narratives (e.g., the anti-carbon-tax campaign); (3) Staged representation — sympathetic media identifies the industry as standing for the nation.
- Forms of representation in the political sphere: (4) Claims about what counts as legitimate expertise and knowledge; (5) Authorization of policies favourable to the industry; (6) Institutionalized revolving-door and lobbying practices that reinforce industry voice.
- Process / loop: These six representations form a self-reinforcing loop — public-sphere claims about national identity legitimize the industry's political-sphere activity, which authorizes public policy, which in turn enables continued representative claim-making.
- Outcomes: Construction of ‘who we are’ (identity), ‘what we know’ (knowledge), and ‘what we decide to do’ (policy) around carbon-intensive interests; exclusion of citizen participation and voice; hegemonic (rather than legitimate) industry dominance over climate politics.

The central process is performative: by making claims that equate the fossil fuel industry with ‘the people’ and the Australian nation, firms and their allies manufacture an aggregated ‘unity of the people’ that mirrors industry interests. This artificial representation derives from hegemony rather than democratic legitimacy (Laclau and Mouffe), because citizens are neither the formal authorizers of nor accountable audiences for industry's claims. Once industry interests are rendered equivalent with the national interest, the need for extensive overt lobbying diminishes — decarbonization is structurally obstructed without overt prohibition.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces the concept of carbon populism — a form of populism in which the future of the nation is discursively and materially bound to carbon-intensive industries — and advances three contributions to management scholarship: (i) it extends Political Corporate Social Responsibility (PCSR) by explaining why firms cannot act as legitimate democratic representatives, since their ‘representative’ claims exclude citizen participation and rest on hegemony rather than legitimacy; (ii) it advances Corporate Political Activity (CPA) research by surfacing how artificial representation across public and political spheres shapes national identity and policy expertise, producing negative democratic externalities; and (iii) it contributes to emerging debates on corporate populism and nationalism and to political-theory discussions of the ‘constructivist turn’ by foregrounding corporate actors as claim-makers largely absent from those literatures.
practical_implication: The authors recommend two measures to combat carbon populism and strengthen representative democracy for decarbonization. First, in the political sphere, expand citizen inclusion through public forums, citizen panels, and climate assemblies (e.g., the French Citizens' Convention for Climate), and devolve power to municipal councils, cooperatives, referenda, and recall elections. Second, in the public sphere, develop alternative digital platforms — not owned by private firms or single individuals — to support public consultation, online voting, real-time policy feedback, and ‘people's platforms’ for broad-based e-democracy. Together these measures would limit the industry's capacity to manufacture an ‘aggregated unity of the people’ that underwrites fossil fuel expansion.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors suggest extending the concept of carbon populism to other industries where industry prosperity is rendered equivalent to the national interest — including emerging ‘AI populism’ among US tech firms framing AI leadership as central to national identity amid US-China competition, as well as steel and other sectors facing foreign competition or legislative threats. They also call for further interrogation of the power relations underlying representative politics: who speaks for whom, why, and how social-media algorithms enable agents to reproduce representative claims from unwitting claim-makers.
abstract: Despite growing recognition that countries around the world must transition to a low-carbon economy, global greenhouse gas emissions continue to rise. One way that decarbonization has been obstructed, we argue, is by fossil fuel firms intentionally conflating their agenda with ‘the people’, evoking notions of national identity, security and prosperity. We develop the concept of carbon populism to explain why big oil, coal and gas firms make representative claims typically associated with democratically elected governments. This adds a significant layer of difficulty for stakeholders endeavouring to enact decarbonization. Drawing on an in-depth study of fossil fuel industries in Australia (mainly coal and gas), we demonstrate how different forms of political representation in the public and political spheres influence national identity, expert knowledge and public policy. These artificial forms of representation legitimize fossil fuel industries’ political activities, authorize public policies that benefit it, and enable the industry to enjoy national dominance. We conclude by arguing that decarbonization can be achieved by rejecting carbon populism in favour of inclusive and participatory democratic representation.
apa_citation: Nyberg, D., Wright, C., Bowden, V., & Fleming, P. (2026). Carbon populism and representative politics: On why fossil fuel firms speaking for ‘the people’ is a bad idea. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70096

title: A stakeholder theory perspective on digital product passport implementation
methods: Qualitative research design combining a systematic literature review of regulatory (ESPR, EBR) and academic sources with expert evaluation. Data coded using Gioia et al. (2013) qualitative coding to derive first-order concepts, second-order themes, and aggregated dimensions; findings evaluated by eight industry experts using a five-point Likert questionnaire adapted from Prat et al. (2015).
industry: Products subject to EU Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) and the European Battery Regulation (EBR); cross-industry scope with emphasis on batteries as the first mandated Digital Product Passport.
units: Regulatory texts, peer-reviewed articles, and DPP industry experts
n_sample: 985 codes drawn from the ESPR, EBR, and 41 academic papers; eight expert evaluators; 315 requirements mapped to 14 stakeholder groups
authors: Paeplow, J.; Jussen-Lengersdorf, I.; Schweihoff, J.; Schoormann, T.; Möller, F.
theories: Stakeholder theory
keywords: Digital collaboration; Digital product passport; Industrial ecology; Requirements; Stakeholder theory; Sustainability
research_question: What are the stakeholder-specific requirements to implement Digital Product Passports?
mechanism_summary:
- Data sources: EU regulatory texts (ESPR, EBR) and 41 academic DPP papers as the corpus from which candidate requirements are identified
- Gioia coding phases: 985 codes abstracted into first-order concepts (requirements), second-order themes (58 requirement categories), and aggregated dimensions
- Stakeholder classifications: 14 specified stakeholder groups grouped into three categories used by the paper — Regulatory Stakeholders (e.g., European Commission, National Authorities), Business Stakeholders (e.g., Economic Operator, Dealer, DPP Service Provider), and General Stakeholders (addressing all groups)
- Expert evaluation: eight DPP practitioners scored the interim mapping on a five-point Likert questionnaire adapted from Prat et al. (2015), leading to refinements of requirements and categories
- Emergent artifact: a requirement catalog and stakeholder–requirement map (visualized as a pie-chart wheel) that surfaces DPP-invisible groups (e.g., media, stake-watchers) where regulation is silent

Drawing on stakeholder theory, the authors read ESPR and EBR as a stakeholder-specific requirement system in which each interest group holds distinct responsibilities, capabilities, and risks for DPP rollout. By iteratively coding regulatory and literature passages into first-order concepts, consolidating them into requirement categories, and validating the structure with industry experts, the analysis produces 315 requirements assigned to 14 stakeholder groups across the three classifications. The resulting catalog operationalizes inter-organizational collaboration, shows where the Economic Operator bears the bulk of obligations, and marks gaps where the regulation currently leaves stakeholder requirements unspecified.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances DPP research by using stakeholder theory to systematically map a fragmented requirements space, showing that legal text (ESPR, EBR) can be read as a stakeholder-specific requirement system in which each group holds distinct responsibilities. It extends stakeholder theory applications to digital-sustainability technologies by specifying how stakeholders demand value in return for meeting regulatory requirements and by identifying DPP-invisible groups (e.g., media, stake-watchers) whose requirements are currently missing. The resulting requirement catalog provides a structured artifact that bridges stakeholder theory, information-systems requirements engineering, and circular-economy policy.
practical_implication: Policymakers can use the catalog to refine upcoming ESPR acts, clarify vague requirements (e.g., General Stakeholder obligations), and design incentives and enforcement tools, particularly for SMEs that lack resources to comply. Practitioners, especially Economic Operators, gain transparency over the DPP requirements and stakeholder map, helping them identify capability gaps, plan collaboration with dealers and service providers, and communicate obligations along the value chain. The catalog thereby supports compliant DPP design and guides investments in data-sharing infrastructure.
limitations: The requirements were derived through manual investigation and subjective interpretation of the ESPR, which text mining could complement to improve coverage and objectivity. The Gioia coding method introduces researcher subjectivity in forming categories, mitigated here by three-person cross-review. The study focuses on the already-published ESPR and EBR, so later sector-specific working plans and additional product regulations fall outside its snapshot, and the evaluation involved only eight experts.
future_research: Future work should broaden the evaluation with more experts and additional stakeholder interviews (e.g., consumers) to surface requirements for DPP-invisible groups such as media and stake-watchers. Researchers should consolidate existing DPP definitions, develop judicial and support tools (especially for SMEs), and investigate how emerging technologies like artificial intelligence and blockchain can support DPP data management. Examining upcoming delegated acts and sector working plans can extend the requirement catalog as the DPP regulation matures.
abstract: The Digital Product Passport (DPP) is expected to contribute to industry-wide sustainable development and digital transformation. However, implementing the DPP poses several risks, and non-compliance can lead to penalties and competitive disadvantages. This paper examines the requirements for DPP implementation from multiple perspectives by drawing on literature and an evaluation study. During the analysis, we assign stakeholders to DPP requirements through stakeholder mapping, identify necessary responsibilities, and facilitate collaboration to roll out DPPs. Following our qualitative analysis, we identified 315 requirements and mapped them to 14 specified stakeholder groups. The stakeholders and requirements can be found in our requirement catalog. Furthermore, we provide an overview of our requirement mapping to facilitate the usage of the requirement catalog. Overall, our study advances DPP research and practice by delivering nine explicit pathways to address uncertainties related to the DPP requirements. For instance, we elaborate on the need for DPP support for SMEs and the exploration of DPP definitions. The proposed requirement catalog informs about essential stakeholder groups and activities for implementing the DPP. It helps practitioners craft compliant DPPs and indicates the current boundaries of DPP regulation for policymakers.
apa_citation: Paeplow, J., Jussen-Lengersdorf, I., Schweihoff, J., Schoormann, T., & Möller, F. (2026). A stakeholder theory perspective on digital product passport implementation. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00029-9

title: Advancing Employee Voice Through Insights From Democratic Innovations
methods: Conceptual paper; cross-disciplinary theoretical synthesis that uses Elstub and Escobar's (2019a) framework of democratic innovations — seven dimensions and four families of practices — as an analytical lens to assess whether and how these ideas have been applied in employee voice research, identify understudied dimensions and families of practices, and articulate their implications for voice scholarship and practice.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pek, Simon; Busaan, Lorin
theories: deliberative democracy; democratic innovations framework (Elstub and Escobar 2019); participatory democracy; varieties of capitalism
keywords: deliberative democracy; democratic innovations; employee participation; employee voice; participatory democracy
research_question: Whether and how have the core dimensions and families of practices in Elstub and Escobar's (2019a) framework of democratic innovations been applied in employee voice research, and how could underused dimensions and families expand the field's repertoire of voice practices to address contemporary challenges like inclusion and employee polarization?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Employee voice and democratic innovations share an "ineliminable core" — improving opportunities for individuals to influence decision-making — so the political-science typology of democratic innovations can productively extend the voice field's conceptual repertoire.
- Key constructs (dimensions): Six existing/emerging voice dimensions (depth, level, scope, form, formalization, reach) are complemented by four understudied dimensions imported from democratic innovations — participant selection method, stage of the policy process, mode of participation, and mode of decision-making.
- Key constructs (families of practices): Collaborative governance already overlaps with voice; three understudied families — deliberative mini-publics, participatory budgeting, and referenda and citizen initiatives — are theorized as new or reimagined voice practices.
- Boundary conditions: Firms differ from polities in the legitimation of authority (ownership-based hierarchy vs. democratic rights), and adoption is shaped by managers' cost-benefit assessments, employees' perceptions of efficacy, and the institutional setting (CME vs. LME varieties of capitalism).

The paper's central process is conceptual translation: it maps Elstub and Escobar's seven-dimension framework onto the voice literature, identifies points of overlap and neglect, and argues that attending to the four understudied dimensions (selection, stage, participation mode, decision-making mode) reveals how the three understudied families could be adapted for workplaces. The authors illustrate applications — for example, using stratified random selection to improve "reach," using deliberative mini-publics to address complex or polarizing organizational issues, using participatory budgeting for benefits or philanthropic allocations, and using binding referenda and citizen initiatives for high-stakes decisions — while arguing that these adaptations jointly improve both epistemic quality of decisions and inclusion of marginalized voices.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a more comprehensive framework of employee voice by adding four understudied dimensions — participant selection method, stage of the policy process, mode of participation, and mode of decision-making — to the six dimensions already prominent in voice research, yielding an expanded ten-dimension framework that highlights both independent and interdependent design choices. It further introduces three families of democratic innovation practices (deliberative mini-publics, participatory budgeting, and referenda and citizen initiatives) as underused voice practices, and positions democratic innovations as a productive interdisciplinary resource for addressing voice failures, inclusion, and employee polarization.
practical_implication: Managers can use the expanded ten-dimension framework as a diagnostic tool to assess their current suite of voice practices and redesign them — for example, adding deliberative modes of participation and decision-making to town-hall meetings, or switching from self-selection to stratified random selection to improve reach across a diverse workforce. Policymakers can draw on the understudied families of practices when updating statutory voice provisions, using "hard" mandates (e.g., referenda for mergers and acquisitions) or "soft" incentives and funding supports to diffuse practices such as participatory budgeting and binding initiatives, particularly in coordinated market economies where public support for voice is stronger.
limitations: The paper is explicitly conceptual and illustrative rather than empirical, so the proposed applications of mini-publics, participatory budgeting, and referenda to employee voice are not tested. The authors note space constraints that forced them to foreground only particularly salient contemporary issues (inclusion and polarization) and acknowledge that important differences between firms and polities — especially around ownership-based authority and managerial discretion in non-statutory settings — mean democratic-innovation insights must be carefully translated rather than directly transplanted into workplace contexts.
future_research: The authors call for empirical research that investigates how different selection methods shape employees' perceptions of representatives' autonomy and legitimacy, and how shifts in mode of participation (e.g., from polling to deliberation) affect outcomes like organizational creativity and job satisfaction. They also encourage work that leverages the four understudied dimensions and three understudied families of practices to study relationships with longstanding voice outcomes, and that examines the institutional conditions (CME vs. LME, statutory regimes) under which these practices diffuse and achieve their intended effects.
abstract: Employee voice has long attracted interest from academics, policymakers, and practitioners in light of its myriad benefits. To date, employee voice researchers have identified a wide array of voice practices that are organized and understood through a core set of dimensions. However, there are growing calls for more interdisciplinary research to ensure the field's repertoire of practices is effective in terms of addressing employees' and firms' needs while also being attuned to the contemporary nature of work, which includes increasing concerns about inclusion and employee polarization. In this conceptual paper, we seek to expand the field's understanding of existing and potential voice practices by considering whether and how core dimensions and families of practices from democratic innovations scholarship have been applied in employee voice research. We identify four understudied dimensions and three understudied families of practices, illustrate how they could be applied in the context of employee voice, and articulate contributions to research and practice.
apa_citation: Pek, S., & Busaan, L. (2026). Advancing employee voice through insights from democratic innovations. *Human Resource Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1748-8583.70039

title: Are they really just words? Investigating the prevalence and impact of physical disability biased language in job advertisements.
methods: Three-study mixed design: Study 1 is a content analysis of 1,886 real job advertisements using a custom ableist-language dictionary and EEO/accommodation coding; Study 2 is a policy-capturing experiment manipulating ableist language and EEO and accommodation statements; Study 3 is a between-subjects experiment that adds a pay-level manipulation. Multilevel regression and linear models test effects on perceived person-organization fit and likelihood to apply, with physical disability status as a moderator.
industry: Cross-industry job advertisements (Study 1); general working-age adults recruited online (Studies 2-3)
units: Job advertisements (Study 1); individual job seekers with and without physical disabilities (Studies 2-3)
n_sample: 1,886 job ads (Study 1); 528 participants (Study 2); additional sample in Study 3
authors: Pike, M. D.; Powell, D. M.; Spence, J. R.; Bourdage, J. S.
theories: signaling theory
keywords: ableism; accommodations; bias in job ads; equal opportunity statements; recruitment
research_question: How prevalent is ableist (disability-biased) language in job advertisements, and how do ableist language and inclusive cues (EEO and accommodation statements) affect perceived person-organization fit and likelihood to apply among individuals with and without physical disabilities, including when job desirability (pay) varies?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in signaling theory (Spence, 1973), the authors argue that because job seekers work under imperfect information, the specific words used in job ads act as signals about the organization's culture, values, and the kinds of people who "fit." Ableist phrasing (e.g., "fast paced," "stand out in a crowd") signals that individuals with physical disabilities may not belong, lowering perceived P-O fit and intentions to apply, whereas EEO and accommodation statements signal inclusivity and raise both fit perceptions and application likelihood. These effects are expected to be amplified for individuals with disabilities, who are more attuned to such cues, and may be only partially offset by otherwise desirable job features such as higher pay.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides a seminal investigation of ableist language in recruitment materials, extending prior work on gendered and ageist job-ad wording to physical disability, and documents that ableist cues are both pervasive and consequential. It advances signaling theory by showing that subtle, likely unintentional signals of bias in everyday job-ad wording can carry as much weight as more overt signals of inclusivity (EEO and accommodation statements) and can rival the influence of pay on applicant reactions.
practical_implication: Recruiters and organizations should audit job advertisements for ableist wording and substitute more inclusive phrasing while still accurately describing job requirements, because such language deters both disabled and non-disabled applicants from applying. Adding EEO and accommodation statements, which remain rare in practice, is a low-cost way to raise perceived fit and intentions to apply. Higher pay does not fully compensate for the negative effects of ableist language, so pay-based strategies alone cannot offset biased wording.
limitations: The ableist word dictionary was developed with subject-matter experts with lived experience of physical disability but is not exhaustive and may overlap with age- or health-related bias; the ableist experimental conditions contained a large dose of ableist language, making it hard to test smaller, more realistic amounts. Pay manipulation checks were imperfect, the likelihood-to-apply and P-O fit measures may conceptually overlap, and the experimental context cannot capture real-world pressures such as the need to secure income. The authors also could not fully explain why participants without disabilities were affected by ableist cues.
future_research: Researchers should refine and extend the ableist-language dictionary to cover other disabilities and test thresholds for how much ableist language is needed to produce negative effects. Future studies could use more differentiated measures of P-O fit and likelihood to apply, stronger pay manipulations (e.g., explicit comparisons to median salary), and field or longitudinal designs that capture real job-search constraints. Further work is also needed to unpack the mechanisms by which ableist language affects applicants without disabilities.
abstract: language is used in recruitment, it could impact application rates of diverse and qualiﬁed applicants. This article examines the prevalence and impact of biased (ableist) and inclusive (equal employment opportunity [EEO] and accommodation statements) language on individuals with and without disabilities. In Study 1, a content analysis of 1886 job ads found that ableist language was present in 84.09% of the job ads, whereas EEO and accommodation statements were only present in 19.25% and 9.28% of job ads, respectively. Study 2 examined the effect of biased and inclusive language and found that individuals with and without disabilities were (a) negatively affected by ableist language and (b) positively affected by EEO and accommodation statements. Ableist language was associated with lower perceptions of personorganization ﬁt and intentions to apply. In contrast, EEO and accommodation statements were associated with higher intentions to apply. Accommodation statements were also associated with increased ﬁt perceptions. These effects were more pronounced for individuals with disabilities. Study 3 sought to replicate these results while manipulating the desirability of jobs through pay levels. Ableist language continued to have a negative impact on ﬁt and likelihood to apply, and EEO and accommodation statements maintained a positive impact. However, unlike Study 2, these effects were not stronger for those with physical disabilities. This research demonstrates the negative effects of ableist language in recruitment materials and the positive effect of EEO and accommodation statements.
apa_citation: Pike, M. D., Powell, D. M., Spence, J. R., & Bourdage, J. S. (2026). Are they really just words? Investigating the prevalence and impact of physical disability biased language in job advertisements. *Journal of Applied Psychology*. Advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1037/apl0001365

title: When Opposites Don’t Attract: The New Information Environment, Polarization, and What Happens to Social Evaluations When ‘The World Goes to Hell in a Handbasket’
methods: Conceptual essay. The authors develop theoretical propositions through synthesis of prior social evaluations research, arguing how the new information environment and polarization alter the value and durability of five social evaluation types.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pollock, T. G.; Rindova, V. P.
theories: institutional legitimacy (Scott's three pillars); polarization and echo chambers; social evaluations theory; social identity / values theory
keywords: celebrity; legitimacy; new media; polarization; reputation; social evaluations; status; stigma
research_question: How do shifts in the new information environment and increased polarization alter the value and durability of five types of social evaluations — legitimacy, reputation, status, celebrity/infamy, and stigma/esteem — and what does this mean for future theorizing and empirical study of social evaluations?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The new information environment (multi-directional, many-to-many, without editorial gatekeepers) plus algorithm-driven polarization fragment the shared sociocognitive content on which social evaluations depend.
- Key constructs: new information environment; polarization / echo chambers; value and durability of social evaluations; five social evaluation types (legitimacy, reputation, status, celebrity/infamy, stigma/esteem).
- Boundary conditions: effects vary by social-evaluation type because each draws on different sociocognitive content (cognitive-normative-regulative for legitimacy; rational assessments for reputation and status; emotions for celebrity/infamy; moral judgments for stigma/esteem).
- Emergent outcomes: societal-level value and durability of rational, consensus-based evaluations (legitimacy, reputation, status) erode while subgroup-level value and durability may intensify; emotion- and morality-based evaluations (celebrity/infamy, stigma/esteem) amplify within polarized in-groups and intensify against out-groups.

The process the authors articulate is that consensus-dependent evaluations collapse when audiences no longer share information sources, facts, or values, so aggregate evaluations give way to simultaneous, opposing subgroup evaluations. Because celebrity, infamy, stigma, and esteem are grounded in emotion and moral judgment rather than analytical assessment, polarization's constant emotional supply makes them more durable and more extreme within polarized communities, while the tribal nature of polarization also means the same actor can simultaneously attract celebrity from one group and infamy from another.
theoretical_contribution: The essay contributes to social evaluations research by foregrounding how the traditional and new information environments differ, and by articulating how the resulting polarization reshapes what is — and is not — different in theorizing and empirically studying social evaluations. It calls for moving legitimacy scholarship from a consensus to a conflict model, for rethinking whether "generalized" favourability in reputation remains possible, and for developing esteem as a positive moral analogue to stigma.
practical_implication: The authors signal that organizations can no longer be managed as if audiences were an undifferentiated "general public"; instead, managers and scholars must recognize that actors can simultaneously hold opposing social evaluations with different polarized audiences, and that attention-grabbing and emotional intensification reward the loudest and most extreme voices. Navigating polarized and fragmented social landscapes requires attending to specific audiences rather than averaged assessments.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors propose three priorities: (1) broaden social evaluations' theoretical foundations by integrating values psychology (e.g., Schwartz value framework) and individual/group identity research; (2) revise sample construction and measurement by abandoning central-tendency aggregation, adopting spectrum or stratified sampling, and capturing evaluations within specific audiences and time periods; and (3) attend to context, language, and generalization through ethnographic and digital-ethnographic methods that illuminate how vocabularies and frames shape social evaluations in different polarized communities.
abstract: Organizations today operate in a new information environment, where changing technologies have enabled fundamentally different forms and pathways of information creation and dissemination while also generating unintended consequences – chief among them increased polarization. In combination, the new information environment and polarization have profoundly altered the social processes underpinning social evaluations. To animate social evaluations research on these issues, we consider how information environment shifts and increased polarization affect the value and durability of five social evaluation types – legitimacy, reputation, status, celebrity/infamy, and stigma/esteem. We highlight the implications for both theorizing about and empirically studying social evaluations in an ever more polarized world.
apa_citation: Pollock, T. G., & Rindova, V. P. (2026). When opposites don’t attract: The new information environment, polarization, and what happens to social evaluations when ‘the world goes to hell in a handbasket’. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70067

title: Reframing perceptions on the informal and formal waste sectors: an analysis of the social and systemic hotspots of the plastic waste management system of Chennai, India
methods: Exploratory qualitative case study using 18 semi-structured stakeholder interviews (experts from NGOs, private service providers, citizen initiatives, and journalists; itinerant waste buyers; waste pickers; conservancy workers; small and large aggregators) with chain-referral sampling; interview transcripts analyzed via inductive qualitative content analysis (Hsieh & Shannon, 2005) using three-level hierarchical coding in Atlas.ti to identify social and systemic hotspots in the formal and informal plastic waste management sectors
industry: Municipal solid waste management, plastic waste value chain (collection, aggregation, recycling)
units: Stakeholders in Chennai's formal and informal plastic waste management system (waste pickers, itinerant waste buyers, conservancy workers, scrap shop operators, large aggregators, NGOs, private service providers, citizen initiatives, journalists)
n_sample: 18 stakeholder interviews
authors: Ravisandiran, S. M.; Navarre, N.; Mogollón, J. M.; Cucurachi, S.
theories: informal-formal sector integration; participatory formalization; social hotspots analysis
keywords: Formalization; Industrial ecology; Informal waste sector (IWS); Plastic recycling; Plastic waste management (PWM); Qualitative content analysis
research_question: What are the social and systemic (institutional) hotspots in the formal and informal plastic waste management systems of Chennai, India, and how do the lived conditions of workers in each sector compare in ways that can inform more inclusive and effective formalization policies?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that because the formal sector in Chennai has been privatized and is organized hierarchically, frontline formal workers face pay cuts, longer hours, reduced job security, and caste- and gender-based discrimination from supervisors, producing social hotspots comparable to or worse than those in the IWS. The IWS, by contrast, is decentralized and autonomous, which buffers its workers from hierarchical abuses and offers flexible hours and regular cash flow, generating a preference among the most vulnerable workers to remain informal. At the systemic level, the formal system's lack of transparency, infrastructure, and citizen trust interact with the IWS's system-hiding and absence of bookkeeping so that neither sector alone can deliver efficient, socially just plastic waste management, motivating participatory rather than top-down integration.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers the first side-by-side qualitative comparison of social and institutional hotspots in the formal and informal plastic waste management sectors of a single Indian megacity, challenging the dominant "savior" framing of formalization by showing that the formal sector does not automatically provide better working conditions. It extends work on social life cycle assessment and informal-sector integration by contextualizing social dynamics in both sectors simultaneously and theorizing how privatization, hierarchy, and autonomy jointly shape worker outcomes. It reframes formalization as a participatory, bottom-up process that must preserve the autonomy and knowledge of informal workers while extending legal protections and social security.
practical_implication: Policymakers in Chennai and comparable Indian cities should move beyond top-down absorption of informal workers into the formal system and instead pursue participatory integration models such as the SWaCH cooperative in Pune and waste picker cooperatives in São Paulo, giving workers a voice in decision-making while preserving their autonomy. Municipal authorities should provide proper protective equipment, fair compensation, and transparent reporting for formal sector workers; address infrastructure gaps; engage self-organized waste worker associations to improve data collection on the IWS; and use monetary incentives to mobilize IWS capacity for collecting non-profitable plastic polymers that currently escape both systems.
limitations: The study is exploratory: chain-referral sampling introduces selection bias, and time constraints plus informal workers' reluctance to participate meant the sample of 18 stakeholders is not fully representative of the broader population. Interviewer-interviewee hierarchy and subjective interpretation during transcription and translation may further bias the data. Findings on downstream recycling are specific to plastics and may not generalize to other recyclable streams, and the evidence base is local to Chennai.
future_research: Further research should engage self-organized associations of informal workers to overcome data collection barriers and build more representative pictures of the IWS across Indian cities. Comparative work is needed on participatory, bottom-up integration models (e.g., SWaCH Pune, São Paulo cooperatives) applied to other Indian megacities, and on how transparent reporting and independent worker-satisfaction surveys can be institutionalized within privatized SWM systems. Studies should also explore how monetary incentives can mobilize IWS capacity for collecting non-profitable plastic polymers and reduce environmental leakage.
abstract: Waste management systems in lowmiddle-income countries are strained due to rapid urbanization and population growth. Inefficiencies in formal waste management systems have led to the emergence of the informal waste sector (IWS). Despite benefits like higher collection efficiency and the provision of livelihoods for workers, IWS creates exposure to occupational hazards and social discrimination. Although previous studies have researched the role of IWS workers, their occupational conditions, and barriers to formalization, there is limited research on contextualization of social dynamics in both the informal and formal waste sectors. In this study, we provide for the first time a comparison of the social and institutional hotspots in the formal and informal plastic waste management systems of Chennai, India, through interviews with stakeholders from both sectors. We show that social hotspots can be just as bad in the formal sector for waste collectors, resulting in a preference among the most vulnerable workers to remain informal. This is also in part due to power imbalances in the formal sector, which have worsened since the privatization of the solid waste management system, and in contrast, the autonomy offered by the informal system. At the institutional level, the formal system faces the barriers of lack of transparency, waste generator mistrust on the system, and lack of infrastructure, while the informal system exhibits strong barriers to data collection due to system-hiding and lack of organization. We argue that narratives around formalization must adopt a participatory approach to improve social outcomes for waste workers.
apa_citation: Ravisandiran, S. M., Navarre, N., Mogollón, J. M., & Cucurachi, S. (2026). Reframing perceptions on the informal and formal waste sectors: An analysis of the social and systemic hotspots of the plastic waste management system of Chennai, India. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00012-4

title: Kalpita Bhar Paul. Ecophenomenology and the Environmental Crisis in the Sundarbans: Towards a Community-Based Ethic
methods: Book review essay; no empirical design.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Reinecke, J.
theories: ecophenomenology; phenomenology (Heideggerian); relational ontology
keywords: Sundarbans; community-based ethic; ecophenomenology; environmental crisis; more-than-human; nature's agency; relational ontology; sustainability paradigm
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper
practical_implication: Reinecke argues that Paul’s ecophenomenology offers managers and organizations a vocabulary for moving beyond treating nature as capital, risk, or a set of ecosystem services to be optimized. It invites a shift from managing over nature to managing with it—engaging ecological systems as partners in value creation—illustrated by examples such as Patagonia making Earth its sole shareholder and Faith in Nature’s "Nature on the Board" initiative that grants nature formal representation in corporate governance.
limitations: Reinecke notes that Paul’s call to "let go" and adopt an "ethos of letting be" may appear too contemplative and insufficient amid the accelerating crises of climate change, biodiversity loss, and social inequality, potentially limiting its traction as a prescriptive framework for urgent organizational action.
future_research: The review suggests that organization scholars could build on ecophenomenology as a methodological provocation—studying organizations and institutions as part of, rather than apart from, the living environments that sustain them—and develop leadership and governance approaches for the Anthropocene that cultivate sensitivity to how ecosystems co-create resilience and align strategy with natural limits and rhythms.
abstract: Kalpita Bhar Paul’s Ecophenomenology and the Environmental Crisis in the Sundarbans is a powerful and unconventional contribution to the study of organizations, environment, and ethics. Although written in the language of philosophy, the book has implications that resonate deeply with management and organization scholars confronting the limitations of the contemporary sustainability paradigm. Paul’s central claim is that the environmental crisis is not only a biophysical condition but also a crisis of meaning and imagination: a breakdown in how humans understand their relationship with the more-than-human world.

title: Is Gen Z willing to pay for sustainability? An advanced market segmentation analysis
methods: Quantitative survey-based segmentation across two longitudinal studies using Finite Mixture Modeling (latent class analysis) with AIC and BIC for model selection; maximization of the log-likelihood for estimation; willingness to pay a premium and perceived financial well-being measured with single-item scales; SDGs and societal issues captured via ranking questions
industry: Consumer marketing (general; no specific industry)
units: Gen Z individuals aged 18-26 recruited via Qualtrics third-party consumer panel, representative of the U.S. population in terms of gender, race, and geographic location
n_sample: Study 1 N=1,223 usable responses (from 3,438 screened); Study 2 N=266 usable observations
authors: Rohm, A.; Namin, A.; Stefl, M.
theories: cohort theory; self-efficacy theory; value-belief-norm (VBN) theory
keywords: Gen Z; Latent class analysis; Market segmentation; Societal issues; Sustainability; United Nations Sustainable Development Goals
research_question: What are the characteristics of differentiated segments of Gen Z individuals, based on demographic and psychographic factors, related to their willingness to pay a premium for brands that make the world a better place through their products or services, their stand on social justice, and support of their employees? Further, given the nature of the politically sensitive issues investigated, to what extent do recent changes in public sentiment related to sustainability and societal issues alter the characteristics of these segments?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on cohort theory, the authors argue that Gen Z — having come of age during climate activism, Black Lives Matter, and #MeToo in an always-on media environment — shares values and beliefs that shape sustainability perceptions and purchase motives. Finite Mixture Modeling uncovers two latent classes in each study whose willingness to pay a premium is differentially driven by specific SDGs and societal issues. The authors invoke self-efficacy theory (Bandura, 1977) to explain why Gen Z may prefer more personal and controllable issues (e.g., LGBTQ rights) over abstract global issues (e.g., affordable clean energy). Because socio-political sentiment shifted between studies, segment composition and the mix of issues driving willingness to pay changed, challenging the stability and generalizability of segmentation models over time.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes four contributions to the marketing sustainability literature: it focuses on Gen Z as a consumer segment; incorporates an extended set of issues spanning the UN SDGs and Gen Z-relevant societal issues; reveals distinct segments of Gen Z consumers based on willingness to pay a premium for sustainable products; and assesses the impact of recent socio-political shifts in public sentiment on the generalizability and stability of the initial segmentation model. By doing so, it advances understanding of when and why segmentation models may need to be reassessed or adapted in dynamic cultural, social, and political environments.
practical_implication: Marketers should recognize that Gen Z is not a one-size-fits-all segment and that the sustainability issues driving their willingness to pay a premium are sensitive to socio-political shifts. Brands should craft narrower, more personal, and locally actionable initiatives — e.g., circular-fashion messaging around climate action, size-inclusive products, accessibility features, or neighborhood-level LGBTQ and racial-justice partnerships — rather than abstract global SDG commitments. Brands targeting Gen Z should regularly reassess segmentation models and pair sustainability claims with measurable, credible commitments (e.g., supplier wage upgrades, low-carbon operations, women's advancement programs).
limitations: One limitation is that the use of ranking data for the SDGs and related societal issues could limit the depth of the findings, as ranking does not capture the varying magnitudes of importance respondents attach to each issue. Study 2's usable sample (N=266) is substantially smaller than Study 1's (N=1,223), which constrains comparisons of segment composition across studies. The study relies on single-item measures for willingness to pay and financial well-being, and was conducted exclusively with U.S.-based Gen Z respondents, limiting cross-cultural generalizability.
future_research: Future research could employ rating scales (rather than rankings) to capture varying levels of importance for SDGs and societal issues, and further explore the impact of socially, politically, and culturally sensitive topics on the stability of segmentation models during times of rapid change. Researchers could investigate the underlying mechanisms or mindsets driving Gen Z segment perceptions, compare attitudes across cohorts (Millennials, Baby Boomers), and replicate the study in other countries using different social-media platforms such as Instagram and TikTok. Finally, applying social cognition theory — specifically self-efficacy — could illuminate the role of specific motivations influencing sustainable consumption across groups defined by age, gender, race, and other background variables.
abstract: Purpose – The purpose of this study is to develop a segmentation model that uncovers distinct segments of Gen Z respondents based on their perceptions related to sustainability issues, with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) as a framework, along with willingness to pay a premium for sustainable products. Design/methodology/approach – Drawing from two national samples of Gen Z individuals and utilizing state-of-the-art econometrics techniques – Finite Mixture Modeling – the authors apply perceptions of the importance of SDGs and related societal issues, along with demographic and psychographic factors including willingness to pay a premium for a brand that makes the world a better place, across two longitudinal studies to determine differentiated segments of Gen Z individuals. Findings – These findings explore Gen Z’s perspectives related to sustainability with implications for managers and policy makers. Results reveal segmentation models with two segments of Gen Z consumers with differing model characteristics across the two studies. Findings highlight how changes in public sentiment can substantially alter the characteristics of consumer segments over relatively short periods of time, posing challenges for traditional assumptions about segmentation model generalizability and stability. Originality/value – As demand for sustainable goods and services grows, brands continue to incorporate sustainability and social responsibility within their marketing practices. Existing research supports the rationale (market-, policyor ethics-based reasons) for firms to take a leadership role in addressing important environmental and societal issues. However, there remains a gap in research that explores perceptions related to sustainability and social justice initiatives from a segmentation perspective related to Gen Z consumers. This study reveals the impact of recent socio-political shifts in sentiment on the generalizability and stability of segmentation models. It contributes to existing research by advancing the understanding of when and why segmentation models may need to be reassessed or adapted in dynamic cultural, social and political environments.
apa_citation: Rohm, A., Namin, A., & Stefl, M. (2026). Is Gen Z willing to pay for sustainability? An advanced market segmentation analysis. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*, 43(2), 180-194. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-08-2024-7090
