title: The Gendered Complexity of Sponsorship: How Male and Female Sponsors’ Goals Shape Their Social Network Strategies
methods: Multi-study quantitative design with four studies: Studies 1a and 1b are survey-based goal elicitation studies with exploratory content analysis of free-response goal statements; Study 2 is a confirmatory survey measuring sponsors’ perceptions of sponsorship as self- vs. protege-beneficial; Study 3 is a randomized experiment manipulating protege-centric, sponsor-centric, and dual-goal conditions and measuring cognitive network activation (density/closure) using real contact initials. Analyses include regression, ANOVA, content coding, and mediation tests.
industry: Mixed; online panel participants from a range of industries and occupations
units: Working adults in supervisory positions with self-reported prior sponsorship experience (male and female sponsors)
n_sample: Four studies using online panels of supervisors with sponsorship experience; exact sample sizes per study reported in the paper
authors: Campbell, Elizabeth Lauren; Shea, Catherine T.
theories: cognitive network activation; gender role theory; goal pursuit theory; social capital theory; social network theory
keywords: career support; cognitive network activation; gender differences; goal complexity; network closure; protege-centric goals; social capital; social networks; sparse networks; sponsor-centric goals; sponsorship
research_question: How do male and female sponsors’ goals and goal complexity differ when providing sponsorship, and how do these gendered differences in goal pursuit shape sponsors’ cognitive social network activation strategies for mobilizing resources on behalf of proteges?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on psychological theories of goal pursuit and social capital, the authors argue that gendered workplace expectations, stereotypes, and masculine defaults lead women to experience greater goal complexity than men when considering sponsorship. Men generate fewer, more self-beneficial goals, while women balance protege- and sponsor-centric goals, creating goal conflict. Because goals shape how individuals cognitively activate their social networks, this goal complexity differential produces distinct network activation strategies: women balancing dual goals activate denser, higher-closure networks (conducive to cooperation and social support for relational goals), while men activate sparse, low-density networks (offering informational advantages and self-advancement opportunities) regardless of goal framing.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a sponsor-centric approach to sponsorship research, disentangling sponsorship from other forms of career support and revealing gender as a key differentiator in how leaders engage their social capital for others. It contributes novel theoretical and empirical insight into a micro-level psychological mechanism—gendered goal complexity—that shapes how male and female sponsors activate networks, and it suggests sponsorship may inadvertently perpetuate workplace inequality by differentially burdening female sponsors and differentially rewarding male sponsors.
practical_implication: Organizations should reconsider broad calls for leaders to provide more sponsorship, since such calls may result in male leaders, but not female leaders, benefiting personally while expending social capital. To bolster equitable sponsorship, firms could design goal-based interventions that make sponsorship normative, clearly articulate its purpose, and differentiate its goals from those of mentorship to provide clearer cognitive guidance for leaders of both genders.
limitations: The research focuses exclusively on sponsors’ cognitive processes after a high-quality protege has been selected, leaving selection effects and actual network mobilization unexamined. It relies on experimental and survey methods with online panels, which sidestep field-setting confounds but raise external-validity concerns. Results may vary across organizational cultures, occupational contexts, and intersecting identities (e.g., race, disability) not examined here.
future_research: Future work should examine how sponsor and protege characteristics (race, ethnicity, disability status) intersect with gender to shape sponsorship goals and network strategies, study gender differences in actual network mobilization (not just cognitive activation), and investigate how organizational culture, occupational norms, and selection into sponsorship shape sponsors’ decision-making. Scholars should also empirically test how different cognitively activated network configurations relate to sponsor effectiveness over time.
abstract: Providing sponsorship, when senior colleagues (sponsors) mobilize resources in their social network on junior colleagues’ (prot eg es) behalf, is consequential to the effective functioning of organizations. Yet, the psychological processes underlying male and female sponsors’ goals, priorities, and strategies for activating resources in their networks for proteges are not well understood. We conduct four studies to examine male and female sponsors’ goals and the resulting impact on their approach to activating resources in their social network. Studies 1a and 1b provide converging, initial evidence that men approach sponsorship with less complexity than women, by setting fewer goals that focus more on benefiting themselves. Study 2 provides additional evidence: male sponsors are more likely than female sponsors to perceive sponsorship as an opportunity to benefit themselves while female sponsors perceive sponsorship as an opportunity to benefit their proteges. Study 3 experimentally links the differences in goal pursuit to sponsors’ social network strategies: when asked to balance prot eg e-centric and sponsorcentric goals, women cognitively activate networks higher in closure, but men activate sparse networks regardless of their goals. The implications of these network activation configurations for understanding the sponsorship process are discussed.
apa_citation: Campbell, E. L., & Shea, C. T. (2025). The gendered complexity of sponsorship: How male and female sponsors’ goals shape their social network strategies. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1055-1083. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1110

title: Catalyzing Categories: Category Contrast and the Creation of Groundbreaking Inventions
methods: Large-scale quantitative analysis of USPTO utility patents (1975-2013) using patent fixed-effects regressions predicting the consolidation-disruption (CD) index of a focal patent's technological impact; instrumental variable estimation leveraging exogenous shifts in category contrast from retrospective USPTO reclassification orders; mediation analyses testing idea-creation (novelty, atypicality, originality, boundary-spanning of descendant inventions) and idea-positioning (patent text features, claim scope, examiner narrowing) mechanisms; supplementary analyses linking patents to market-based economic value (Kogan et al., 2017) and to long-term technological impact.
industry: All technology classes covered by USPTO utility patents
units: USPTO utility patents (focal patents and their descendant inventions)
n_sample: 3,127,467 patents
authors: Carnabuci, Gianluca; Kovács, Balázs
theories: categorical imperative; classification systems; knowledge recombination; sociology of categories
keywords: CD index; USPTO; catalyzing categories; category contrast; groundbreaking inventions; idea-creation; idea-positioning; knowledge recombination; patent classification; technological change
research_question: Where do groundbreaking inventions come from, and specifically, how does the category contrast of the institutionalized knowledge classification in which a focal invention is placed shape its likelihood of spurring groundbreaking rather than consolidating descendant inventions?
mechanism_summary: Institutionalized classification schemes shape cognition and creative recombination. Low-contrast categories are internally heterogeneous and weakly differentiated from neighbors, which during the idea-creation stage exposes would-be followers to a more diverse knowledge base and increases the probability that descendant inventions will draw on novel, atypical, original, and boundary-spanning inputs. During the idea-positioning stage, ambiguous evaluative standards in low-contrast categories give patent applicants greater interpretive leeway to frame descendants as distinct from the focal invention's lineage; examiners in such categories are less able to enforce narrow claims or strict prior-art linkages. Together, these two mechanisms make inventions in low-contrast categories more likely to catalyze technological paths that diverge from existing trajectories, yielding descendants with higher economic value and more enduring impact.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces the concept of "catalyzing categories" and advances a novel theoretical framework rooted in the sociology of categories to explain the genesis of groundbreaking inventions. It challenges the prevailing view that high-contrast categories are unambiguously beneficial by showing that categorical fuzziness can actually foster breakthrough innovation through idea-creation and idea-positioning mechanisms. The study thereby extends research on classification systems, knowledge recombination, and the disruptiveness of inventions by treating classification not merely as a mechanism for efficient information search but as a cognitive and institutional force that shapes the inventive process itself.
practical_implication: For policymakers and patent-office administrators, the findings caution that efforts to digitize and sharpen patent classification schemes—while enhancing information retrieval—may inadvertently suppress the emergence of groundbreaking inventions by raising category contrast. For inventors and firms concerned with IP strategy, the results suggest that inventions positioned within low-contrast categories may afford greater strategic leeway to broaden claims, signal novelty, and generate higher-value descendant technologies.
limitations: As with all observational research, isolating the theorized idea-creation and idea-positioning mechanisms is difficult; direct data on search behavior are unavailable and the idea-positioning channel relies on indirect proxies, while the main outcome (the CD index) is itself an indirect though widely validated measure. The design cannot directly compare the magnitudes of the idea-creation and idea-positioning channels, and endogeneity concerns remain because inventions cannot be randomly assigned to categories. The empirical test is confined to USPTO patents, which have known biases, and the paper does not investigate whether inventions may simultaneously consolidate and break ground across distinct technological streams.
future_research: Future work could use interviews, experiments, or case studies to measure the idea-creation and idea-positioning mechanisms more directly, and should explore whether similar dynamics operate in other classification domains such as academic disciplines or scientific fields. Researchers could examine how technological cycles drive shifts in category contrast over time, whether low-contrast categories offer a more favorable environment for strategic patent scope broadening and for patent assertion entities, and how category contrast differentially attracts "outsider" inventors from other domains. The authors also encourage work on how category contrast affects different groups of inventors and on inventions that simultaneously consolidate one technological stream while breaking ground in another.
abstract: We hypothesize that “low-contrast categories” (those lacking sharp differentiation from adjacent categories) catalyze the creation of groundbreaking inventions by influencing two key stages in the life of an invention: (1) idea-creation and (2) idea-positioning. During “idea-creation,” low-contrast categories increase the likelihood that descendant inventions will combine the focal invention with more (a) boundary-spanning, (b) novel, (c) original, and (d) atypical knowledge inputs. During “idea-positioning,” they allow greater leeway in articulating how descendant inventions depart from the focal invention’s lineage and chart new technological directions. We find robust support for our hypothesis using data from the United States Patent and Trademark Office’s classification system spanning nearly four decades. Further analyses demonstrate that the catalyzing effect of low-contrast categories has important material consequences: inventions classified in low-contrast categories spur descendant inventions that generate substantially higher economic value and exert more enduring technological impact than those in high-contrast categories. By introducing the concept of catalyzing categories, this study offers a novel theoretical perspective on the genesis of groundbreaking inventions and the role of categorical structures in the inventive process.
apa_citation: Carnabuci, G., & Kovács, B. (2025). Catalyzing categories: Category contrast and the creation of groundbreaking inventions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1084-1107. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1010

title: Cross-Sector Relational Realignments after an Institutional Crisis: Checking the Instrumentalization of Corporate-Nonprofit Partnerships
methods: Quantitative panel analysis of firm-nonprofit dyads using logistic regression with firm and nonprofit fixed effects, complemented by 54 semi-structured interviews for illustrative qualitative evidence. Difference-in-differences-style comparison of pre- and post-Operation Carwash periods (2010-2013 vs. 2014-2017). Natural language processing in Python to extract partnership mentions from 3,776 annual reports and 324,150 Factiva press articles; board political ties coded from 39,452 resumes. Robustness checks include alternative partnership definitions, LPM comparison, and interaction specifications.
industry: 20 industries (excluding financial firms); publicly listed firms on the Sao Paulo Stock Exchange (B3) and registered Brazilian nonprofits across trade associations, education and research, social services, health, environment, culture, human rights, and other missions
time_period: 2010-2017 (four years before and after Operation Carwash, launched March 2014)
units: Firm-nonprofit dyads (annual observations); partnership coded from corporate annual reports
n_sample: ~440 publicly listed firms per year; 6,067 nonprofits identified across annual reports, cross-checked against IPEA database of 820,000 registered nonprofits; 3,524 corporate annual reports and 252 foundation reports analyzed; 39,452 board member resumes; 54 interviews
authors: Gama, M. A. B.; Gatignon, A.
theories: corporate political activity; institutional theory; legitimacy theory; nonmarket strategy; stakeholder theory
keywords: Operation Carwash; co-optation; corporate political activity; corporate-nonprofit partnerships; government corruption scandals; institutional crisis; instrumentalization; nonmarket strategy; political capital; trade associations
research_question: How do revelations of government corruption scandals influence corporate-nonprofit partnerships by altering the institutional context and shifting the benefits and risks for both parties?
mechanism_summary: A highly mediatized government corruption scandal reshapes the institutional context by eroding trust in government and shifting legitimacy toward nonprofits, amplifying the joint benefits of corporate-nonprofit collaboration on socioeconomic development (H1). At the same time, firms tainted directly or indirectly by the scandal become risky partners because association with them threatens nonprofits' legitimacy (H2). Nonprofits that could serve as vehicles for corporate political influence—trade associations, education and research organizations, and those with political capital—become especially cautious because partnering heightens their exposure to accusations of co-optation and astroturfing (H3, H4). Thus, scandals trigger a realignment of cross-sector ties driven by the coevolution of benefits and risks perceived by both parties.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances a dynamic perspective on nonmarket strategy by showing that institutional changes—specifically government corruption scandals—trigger a realignment of corporate partnerships with nonmarket actors that spans political and nonprofit arenas rather than operating within each in isolation. It illuminates how nonprofits actively resist instrumentalization for corporate reputational repair and political influence, integrating corporate social responsibility with corporate political activity research. By theorizing and measuring firm- and nonprofit-level heterogeneity in post-scandal partnering, the paper reveals limits to using nonprofits as vehicles for stakeholder support and political goodwill even for firms not directly implicated in the scandal.
practical_implication: The findings can help firms and nonprofits adapt more quickly when corruption scandals or similar institutional crises occur, or become more proactive and intentional in crafting partnership strategies in advance. Managers gain a clearer view of how the benefits and risks of nonmarket ties shift from different perspectives after such events. Policymakers and civil society gain insight into the sometimes opaque implications of firm-nonprofit relationships for government and public trust.
limitations: The theoretical rationale is based on the combined net benefits and risks to firms and nonprofits, but the authors cannot directly observe which partnerships were declined or the reasons behind such decisions, nor can they measure the specific benefit and risk combinations that led to each collaboration. Operation Carwash is a rare large-scale scandal, limiting generalizability to smaller or structurally different corruption events. The arguments assume nonprofits are credited with disinterested pursuit of social welfare, so results could change if nonprofits themselves face allegations of misconduct.
future_research: Future work could extend the theory using configurational approaches and examine generalizability to other settings and types of institutional change that introduce political uncertainty, such as the Sunflower Movement in Taiwan. Researchers could assess the longer-term sustainability of the observed realignment, including whether effects persist if public concern about corporate political influence recedes. Cross-country analyses could explore how rising anti-government and anti-corporate sentiments shape firm-nonprofit dynamics, and investigators could study how nonprofit financial misconduct or government opposition alters these patterns.
abstract: We examine how government corruption scandals influence corporate-nonprofit partnerships by altering the institutional context in ways that reshape the value of nonmarket relationships. We analyze these dynamics following Operation Carwash (2014) in Brazil, using comprehensive data on all publicly listed firms and registered nonprofits from 2010 to 2017. We find that partnerships became more common after the scandal’s disclosure, as firms and nonprofits sought to collaborate on socioeconomic development. However, firms seen as directly or indirectly implicated in the scandal struggled to form partnerships due to risks to nonprofits’ legitimacy. Moreover, nonprofits perceived as closely aligned with firms (i.e., trade associations) or the state (i.e., education and research nonprofits or those with political capital) were especially cautious about entering partnerships to avoid appearing complicit in advancing corporate political influence. Our findings thus “check” the instrumentalization of corporate-nonprofit partnerships in two ways: by examining how it unfolds after an institutional crisis and by revealing how nonprofits resist being used as vehicles for corporate reputational repair or political gain. We show that such crises can trigger a broader realignment of corporate partnerships with nonmarket actors, highlighting the need to study shifts across nonmarket sectors, and not just within them.
apa_citation: Gama, M. A. B., & Gatignon, A. (2025). Cross-sector relational realignments after an institutional crisis: Checking the instrumentalization of corporate-nonprofit partnerships. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1031-1054. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0677

title: Special Research Forum—Our Transformational Era: Implications for Management and Organizations
methods: Editorial introduction to a Special Research Forum; narrative synthesis of 10 included studies organized around three thematic clusters.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gruber, M.; Langley, A.; Roberson, Q.; von Krogh, G.
theories: collective action theory; identity theory; institutional theory; knowledge-based view; strategic learning theory
keywords: algorithmic knowledge work; identity and inclusion; organizational transformation; researching forward; stakeholder governance; transformational era
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: As an editorial introducing the Special Research Forum, the piece frames today as a "transformational era" in which new technologies (AI, algorithms, digital trace data, platforms) and urgent societal challenges (climate change, inequity, public health, migration) jointly destabilize foundational assumptions about what firms are, for whom they exist, and how scholars should study them. The editors organize the SRF's 10 studies into three thematic clusters—(1) technology and the changing nature of knowledge and work, (2) identity, inclusion, and human flourishing in the workplace, and (3) governance, stakeholders, and the reconfiguration of institutional relationships—and argue that bridging micro and macro lenses and adopting "researching forward" designs (real-time studies, field experiments, prospective case studies, racing designs) is needed to keep theory relevant to rapidly unfolding phenomena.
practical_implication: The editors urge scholars to move beyond retrospective theorizing and to articulate practical and policy implications of their work more clearly to broad audiences, and they call on organizations and leaders to build inclusive, adaptive, intersectoral forms of organizing capable of engaging diverse stakeholders and addressing grand challenges that no single entity can solve alone.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors highlight opportunities to study the nonlinear, sometimes reversing trajectories of institutional change and public expectations; to investigate links between work arrangements (e.g., virtual work), mental illness, and individual/organizational strategies for managing engagement; and to advance "researching forward" approaches—real-time studies of unfolding issues, collaborative inquiry, field experiments, prospective case studies, and racing designs—that remain empirically open to the future and engage more directly with practice.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS SPECIAL RESEARCH FORUM OUR TRANSFORMATIONAL ERA: IMPLICATIONS FOR MANAGEMENT AND ORGANIZATIONS
apa_citation: Gruber, M., Langley, A., Roberson, Q., & von Krogh, G. (2025). Special research forum—Our transformational era: Implications for management and organizations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 875–880. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2025.4005

title: Interlacing Situated and Algorithmic Modes of Knowledge Work: A Workplace Jurisdiction Perspective
methods: Three-year ethnographic case study (November 2019-September 2022) of an image processing lab (IPL) within the radiology department of an academic medical center in the Netherlands. Data sources: 167.5 hours of observation of IPL members across 37 non-consecutive days plus 25 hours observing the broader radiology workflow; 34 semi-structured interviews with IPL members, radiologists, referring physicians, managers, and IT specialists; and 544 documents (9 public, 535 internal including protocols, annual reports, user manuals). Grounded, inductive analysis in four steps (immersion, defining analytical focus, conceptualizing interlacing practices, operationalizing outcomes) following Lok and de Rond (2013) and Miles and Huberman (1994).
industry: Healthcare; academic medical center radiology
units: One image processing lab (IPL) embedded in the radiology department of a tertiary university medical center (pseudonym: Southern)
n_sample: 1 organization; 9 IPL members, 34 interviews, 167.5 hours of IPL observation plus 25 hours of workflow observation, 544 documents
authors: Kim, Bomi; Rezazade Mehrizi, Mohammad Hosein; Bailey, Diane E.
theories: practice theory; situated cognition; sociology of professions; sociomateriality; workplace jurisdictions
keywords: algorithmic mode of knowledge work; algorithmic technologies; ethnography; inter-occupational coordination; interlacing practices; knowledge claims; professional knowledge work; radiology; situated mode of knowledge work; workplace artifacts; workplace jurisdictions
research_question: "How does the algorithmic mode of knowledge work become integrated with the situated mode of knowledge work across the flow of work to create new workplace jurisdictions?"
mechanism_summary:
- Key tension: Two incommensurable modes of knowledge work — the "situated mode" (context-embedded claims via experts' embodied skills and intuition) and the "algorithmic mode" (context-transcending, systematic claims produced via predefined procedures on digital data) — must be integrated across a multi-occupational workflow.
- Reorganizing interlacing practices: (1) Redefining tasks and interactions — IPL members, using Vitrea and ISP, defined rigorous image-processing measurement and visualization methods relevant to radiologists and referring physicians, codified in detailed protocols. (2) Reorchestrating workplace artifacts through technology — IPL members, with an IT specialist, reprogrammed the PACS workflow engine (if-then rules, structured report templates) to automatically route images and results among occupations.
- Enacting interlacing practices: (3) Systematically using algorithmic technologies — IPL members adhered to disciplined, consistent use of Vitrea and ISP to produce accurate, reproducible results across patient cases. (4) Embedding algorithmic knowledge claims into expert knowledge claims — IPL members inserted their algorithmic outputs (numbers, structured reports, 3D reconstructions) into radiologists' and referring physicians' existing knowledge-claim practices without disrupting workflow predictability.
- Emergent outcomes: Unique combinatory expertise developed within IPL; distinct value added to the flow of work; a new task area created; a new workplace jurisdiction whose boundaries both demarcate (IPL's task area) and connect (coordinate with upstream radiographers and downstream radiologists/referring physicians).
- Triggering conditions (four, enabling jurisdiction creation): (1) temporally constrained work, (2) performed interdependently across occupations, (3) where experts' situated mode led to limited use of algorithmic technologies and dormancy of digital data, and (4) whose idiosyncratic expert knowledge claims spawned growing demands for standardization and replicability.
- Boundary conditions (scope conditions discussed by the authors): algorithmic technologies that enrich rather than substitute for expert knowledge claims; nonexpert group with sufficient knowledge overlap and openness to exploring algorithmic capabilities; knowledge work involving digital data rather than unmediated empirical phenomena; settings where experts make pragmatic concessions under time pressure (less likely in slow, careful domains like R&D); professional-accountability regimes.

Interlacing unfolds as a two-part process that prevents the situated mode from overriding the algorithmic mode within the workflow. Through the two reorganizing practices, IPL established a new structure of coordination by renegotiating task boundaries with other occupations and reconfiguring the larger technological infrastructure (PACS). Through the two enacting practices, IPL sustained that new structure by using the algorithmic technologies systematically and weaving algorithmic knowledge claims into radiologists' and referring physicians' expert claims. Together, these practices simultaneously altered the occupation's non-relational aspects (unique expertise with algorithmic technologies) and relational aspects (coordination with upstream and downstream occupations), yielding a jurisdiction whose boundaries demarcate and connect at once, legitimating algorithmic knowledge claims across the full flow of work.
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper introduces interlacing practices as a trans-group mechanism for integrating algorithmic technologies into the flow of knowledge work across multiple occupations, departing from prior literature's emphasis on local interfacing between algorithmic tools and a single focal user group (e.g., questioning, validating, interrogating, translating by algorithmic brokers). Second, it advances workplace jurisdiction theory by showing that new jurisdictions around novel technologies can emerge through the simultaneous and proactive alteration of both an occupation's relational and non-relational aspects, producing boundaries that both demarcate and connect, and by foregrounding the reconfiguration of the broader technological infrastructure (workflow technology) as a central jurisdiction-creating move.
practical_implication: Managers of professional knowledge work should adopt algorithmic technologies holistically by comprehensively reorganizing the workflow before or alongside deployment, rather than applying local quick fixes. They should support cross-occupational knowledge sharing, creatively reallocate tasks to an upstream nonexpert group with sufficient knowledge overlap (e.g., technicians rather than administrators), and collaborate with IT specialists to reprogram workflow infrastructure so that algorithmic knowledge claims flow seamlessly into expert work - thereby upskilling rather than weakening professional occupations.
limitations: The study is based on a single ethnographic case within one radiology department in one Dutch academic medical center, so the specific sociotechnical and organizational setting may shape theorization. Referring physicians were housed in the same organization as IPL members and radiologists, leaving unexplored how interlacing unfolds when occupations in a shared workflow span organizational boundaries. The authors also did not assess whether IPL's jurisdiction will be formalized in legal or public realms, or test boundary conditions in settings where professional accountability is less central.
future_research: Future research could trace whether workplace jurisdictions created through interlacing (like IPL) are formalized in legal and public realms and evolve into full occupations. Scholars might study interlacing across organizational boundaries, in non-healthcare settings, in non-professional knowledge work contexts (where the algorithmic mode may substitute for rather than complement the situated mode), and where algorithmic technologies substitute for rather than enrich expert knowledge claims. Studies could tease out conditions under which interlacing is most and least likely to create jurisdictions, including settings lacking strong professional accountability or where slow, careful work is normative (e.g., R&D).
abstract: Algorithmic technologies are challenging to integrate into knowledge work; they produce knowledge claims profoundly differently from the situated mode in which experts work. Because knowledge work is often accomplished through the flow or sequential progression of work across multiple occupations, knowledge claims must be legitimized and accepted across those occupations and not by just a single focal user group. Yet, we know little about the deeper changes that organizations must make regarding who has the legitimate expertise to produce knowledge claims and what counts as valuable knowledge when the algorithmic mode of knowledge work enters the workplace. To explore this, we draw on workplace jurisdictions as the foundation for dividing and coordinating work among occupations to study how the algorithmic mode of knowledge work becomes integrated with the situated flow of work, creating a new workplace jurisdiction. Our three-year ethnographic study of the radiology workflow in a medical center shows that interlacing practices weave together the two distinct modes of knowledge work by reorganizing existing relations among tasks, workplace artifacts, and technologies and enacting those reorganized relations. Contrary to the literature’s focus on the interface between algorithmic knowledge claims and a single focal user group, our study proposes a trans-group focus that refines scholarly understanding of how organizations can integrate algorithmic technologies with situated practices of professionals and their knowledge claims, as well as how new workplace jurisdictions emerge around technologies.
apa_citation: Kim, B., Rezazade Mehrizi, M. H., & Bailey, D. E. (2025). Interlacing situated and algorithmic modes of knowledge work: A workplace jurisdiction perspective. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 907-938. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1286

title: Vicarious Experimentation: Do Innovators Learn by Being Imitated?
methods: Quantitative analysis of 438 dyads of original and sequel PlayStation 2 video games (2000-2013); OLS regressions of sequel critics' rating on a vicarious-experimentation index (angular separation of IGDB keyword vectors between near-clone imitations and the original, aggregated across imitators), with genre, publisher, and release-year fixed effects; moderators for imitator prominence (market share, entropy, award status, sales) and tacit knowledge disruption (change in game development unit); robustness checks including Oster (2019) bounds, alternative samples, cross-platform imitation, and alternative specifications.
industry: Console video games (PlayStation 2 platform, with cross-platform checks using Wii, Xbox, etc.)
country: Global (games released commercially on the PS2 platform)
units: Dyads of original PS2 games and their PS2 sequels, with pools of near-clone imitator games released between the original and the sequel
n_sample: 438 original-sequel dyads (drawn from 3,051 PS2 games plus 5,449 cross-platform games)
authors: Kim, S.; Posen, H. E.; Ganco, M.
theories: absorptive capacity; causal ambiguity; organizational learning theory; resource-based view; strategic experimentation; vicarious learning
keywords: causal ambiguity; imitation; knowledge spillovers; learning by being imitated; near-clone; strategic experimentation; tacit knowledge; vicarious experimentation; vicarious learning; video game industry
research_question: How and what can innovators learn from being imitated? Specifically, when and under what conditions do near-clonal imitations enable an innovator to improve the quality of its next-generation product through "vicarious experimentation"?
mechanism_summary: Because near-clones preserve the innovator's core features while varying peripheral features, each imitator's product functions as a quasi-experimental treatment against the innovator's original (the control), enabling the innovator to draw causal inferences about which feature variations improve performance. A larger and more varied pool of near-clones supplies more parallel "experiments" than any single firm could run internally, reducing causal ambiguity and allowing the innovator to selectively incorporate successful modifications into its sequel. This learning is stronger when imitators are prominent (richer, more reliable observational data) and weaker when the innovator's development unit changes (erosion of tacit, product-specific absorptive capacity needed to interpret observed variations).
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "vicarious experimentation" as a novel mechanism situated at the intersection of vicarious learning and strategic experimentation, reframing being imitated from a purely competitive hazard into a source of targeted, causally actionable learning for innovators. By distinguishing near-clonal imitation from both perfect clones and recombinatory knowledge spillovers, it specifies when imitation overcomes rather than reproduces causal ambiguity, and it identifies informational (imitator prominence) and absorptive (tacit-knowledge retention) boundary conditions that govern the effect.
practical_implication: Managers should reframe imitation: rather than dismissing copycats or focusing exclusively on forestalling imitation, innovator firms should systematically study near-clones of their products as quasi-experiments that reveal which peripheral feature variations improve performance. Firms should also protect the continuity of the original development unit, since organizational changes (team turnover, studio reassignment, M&A-driven reorganization) erode the tacit product-specific knowledge needed to interpret and integrate insights from imitators' variations.
limitations: The analysis cannot fully disentangle learning effects from competitive dynamics, and the results do not establish strict causality—the authors rely on extensive controls, Oster (2019) unobservables bounds, and alternative specifications rather than instruments or natural experiments. The study focuses narrowly on vicarious experimentation and does not jointly model other channels through which imitation shapes innovator outcomes (e.g., customer perceptions, competitive responses, recombinatory spillovers). It also does not resolve whether innovators or imitators gain more from the process, nor does it empirically test disruptive-innovation boundary conditions.
future_research: Future work should disentangle the interaction between competition and imitation, develop a comprehensive framework integrating vicarious experimentation with shifts in customer perceptions, rival responses, and recombinatory knowledge spillovers, and examine when being imitated is better for the innovator versus the imitator under different industry and product-category conditions. Scholars should also pursue stronger causal identification in imitation research and explore how vicarious experimentation interacts with disruptive innovation, Red Queen competition, and convergence/divergence in product features.
abstract: Strategy scholars conventionally view imitation as a one-way knowledge transfer from an innovating firm to an imitating firm. We propose that being imitated also serves as a source of learning for the innovator. An innovator learns by being imitated—observing the imitator’s choices and outcomes and comparing them to its own. A key challenge in such learning, and in vicarious learning more broadly, is causal ambiguity in identifying the factors that drive observed performance outcomes. We theorize that an innovator’s learning by being imitated is particularly effective in overcoming causal ambiguity when it takes the form of vicarious experimentation, where an imitator’s near-clone of the innovator’s product functions as a quasi-experimental treatment from which the innovator can learn. This concept extends the logic of strategic experimentation by highlighting how firms can learn from experiments they do not control. We test our theory in the video game industry, demonstrating that vicarious experimentation enhances the quality of the innovator’s next-generation product and offering support for the theorized boundary conditions under which this effect holds.
apa_citation: Kim, S., Posen, H. E., & Ganco, M. (2025). Vicarious experimentation: Do innovators learn by being imitated? *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1108–1129. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1122

title: Would Archimedes Shout “Eureka” with Algorithms? The Hidden Hand of Algorithmic Design in Idea Generation, the Creation of Ideation Bubbles, and How Experts Can Burst Them
methods: Two complementary experimental studies using a purpose-built exploration-based modification (XYZ) of Google Search, built via Sentence-BERT semantic clustering and (in Study 2) furthest-point semantic sampling. Pilot Study 1 is a between-subjects laboratory experiment randomly assigning participants to Google Search vs. XYZ and analyzing idea creativity with independent-samples t-tests and OLS regression. Study 2 is a preregistered field experiment via a global ideation challenge on Freelancer using between-subjects random assignment, OLS regression, two-way ANOVA with simple-slope analysis to test the algorithm-by-expertise interaction, and mediation analyses for recombinant innovation. Ideas are rated by blind expert panels; diversity of ideas is assessed with Sentence-BERT embeddings, TSNE dimensionality reduction, and DBSCAN density-based semantic clustering to identify ideation bubbles.
industry: Sustainability / food industry (food waste in households); overconsumption domain in Pilot 1
country: Study 1: undergraduate sample (university lab); Study 2: creators based in more than 40 countries worldwide (Freelancer platform)
units: Individual creators generating new ideas (undergraduates in Pilot 1; freelance creators in Study 2)
n_sample: Pilot Study 1: 104 participants; Study 2: 245 creators (from an initial 291, after 46 quality exclusions)
authors: Lazar, Moran; Lifshitz, Hila; Ayoubi, Charles; Emuna, Hen
theories: algorithmic design logic; confirmation bias; expertise in knowledge work; exploration-exploitation; filter bubbles; recombinant innovation
keywords: Google Search; algorithmic design; confirmation bias; creativity; domain expertise; exploration vs exploitation; idea generation; ideation bubbles; recombinant innovation; sustainability
research_question: How do algorithmic design logic (exploration vs. exploitation) and domain expertise interact to shape idea generation—specifically, the creativity of ideas at the individual level and the diversity of ideas (including the formation and bursting of "ideation bubbles") at the aggregate level?
mechanism_summary: Exploitation-based search algorithms return popular, relevant, and similar information, which—when used by domain experts—reinforces familiar knowledge and triggers algorithmic confirmation bias, locking experts into a bounded ideation space and limiting recombinant innovation. In contrast, exploration-based algorithms present a wide, semantically diverse information set that bypasses experts' familiar cues and exposes them to unexpected concepts. Experts' deep domain knowledge then allows them to identify valuable associations across distant topics and synthesize them into creative recombinations that novices cannot easily execute. At the aggregate level, experts using exploration-based tools burst dominant "ideation bubbles"—bounded clusters of similar ideas—and seed new, less conventional bubbles, thereby expanding cognitive diversity and idea variety for the collective problem space.
theoretical_contribution: The paper theorizes algorithmic design logic as a "hidden hand" that shapes idea generation and reveals a critical interplay between algorithmic design and domain expertise. Against claims that algorithms make expertise obsolete in creative work, the authors show that experts are precisely the actors best positioned to reap creative benefits from exploration-based algorithms because of their capacity for recombinant innovation, while exploitation-based algorithms trigger algorithmic confirmation bias and entrench experts in familiar frames. The study also introduces the concept of "ideation bubbles"—output-side counterparts to filter bubbles—and demonstrates how expert-by-algorithmic-design interactions shape cognitive diversity beyond the individual level, and it operationalizes recombinant innovation in an algorithmic context.
practical_implication: When using digital tools in early-stage ideation, creators, managers, and organizations should favor exploration-based designs (e.g., adjusted parameters or temperature settings in GenAI) rather than standard exploitation-based tools to surface diverse information. Organizations should strategically pair domain experts with exploration-based algorithms—rather than treating algorithms as replacements for expertise—to maximize recombinant innovation and to burst dominant thought patterns, especially when tackling complex societal challenges that require heterogeneity of thought.
limitations: The empirical test focuses on a single class of algorithm (a search engine modeled on Google Search) rather than the full spectrum of algorithmic tools such as LLMs and interactive collaborative algorithms. The studies document only the short-term effects of algorithm use on idea generation and do not track dynamics as users interact with algorithms repeatedly over time. The work centers on domain expertise and on the idea generation stage of the creative process, so it does not speak directly to other forms of expertise (e.g., process, algorithmic, functional) or to later stages such as idea evaluation, validation, championing, and implementation.
future_research: Future work can broaden the range of algorithmic tools studied—particularly LLMs and collaborative AI—and examine how prompting strategies, personas, and interface features shape knowledge work in specific domains. Longitudinal designs should test whether repeated algorithm exposure amplifies or attenuates effects on creativity, whether different types of expertise generate different biases, and how algorithm familiarity and search habits condition the impact of exploration- versus exploitation-based designs. Researchers could also extend the framework to teams and collaborative creativity, to later stages of the innovation process where exploitation-based algorithms may be preferable, and to long-term consequences of ideation bubbles for homogeneity of thought at the organizational and societal levels.
abstract: Does “eureka” still ring true in the algorithmic age? This paper investigates algorithmic design and its impact on idea generation. The use of algorithms transforms knowledge work processes, redefining experts’ roles across industries. While experts are typically viewed as the cornerstone of knowledge work, an emerging debate is underway about whether expertise is still necessary in the idea generation process for creativity and innovation. Integrating creativity, expertise in knowledge work, and algorithmic design literatures, we theorize on the interplay between expertise and algorithmic design in idea generation. We suggest that the prevailing algorithmic design, focusing on information exploitation, is misaligned with the recombinant innovation processes needed for idea generation, especially for experts. We design an exploration-based modification to the prevalent exploitation-based algorithm—Google Search. We hypothesize that when using exploration-based algorithms, experts can overcome confirmation bias and generate more creative ideas through recombinant innovation. Moving beyond the individual level, experts are central to bursting “ideation bubbles”—clusters of similar ideas—thus enhancing idea diversity. We test our theory in a laboratory study and a field experiment through a global ideation challenge in sustainability launched on Freelancer. Findings offer insights into designing and using algorithms to augment human expertise for innovation.
apa_citation: Lazar, M., Lifshitz, H., Ayoubi, C., & Emuna, H. (2025). Would Archimedes shout “eureka” with algorithms? The hidden hand of algorithmic design in idea generation, the creation of ideation bubbles, and how experts can burst them. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 881-906. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1307

title: Navigating Mental Illness at Work Using Disengagement and Engagement Pathways
methods: Inductive qualitative study using grounded theory. Two-phase design: (1) a sensitizing phase analyzing anonymous blog posts from 171 individuals and 1,119 Reddit posts from 781 unique users to refine the interview protocol, and (2) a main phase of 59 semi-structured interviews with 57 participants diagnosed with mood and/or anxiety disorders. Interviews (43-84 minutes; average 61 minutes) were transcribed verbatim and analyzed inductively via open coding, axial coding, and theoretical integration to build a process model of (dis)engagement types, mechanisms, and outcomes.
industry: Diverse occupations including bus driver, chemical engineer, CTO, consultant, graphic designer, IT professional, lawyer, manager, professor, real estate manager, restaurant server, salesperson, teacher, manufacturing tester, touring musician
units: Employed adults diagnosed with mood disorders, anxiety disorders, or both prior to March 2020
n_sample: 59 interviews with 57 participants (two interviewed twice); ~37% women; average age 41.5 (SD 11; range 22-66)
authors: Rosado-Solomon, Emily H.; Thatcher, Sherry M. B.; Strizver, Sam D.
theories: embodiment theory; inductive grounded theory; stigma and disclosure theory; work engagement and disengagement theory
keywords: anxiety disorders; distraction; embodiment; image preservation; mental illness at work; mood disorders; physical disengagement; social disengagement; suffering reduction; symptom management; task disengagement; wellness outcomes; work disengagement; work engagement
research_question: How do employees with mental illness navigate their symptoms vis-a-vis work? Specifically, how do employees manage the symptoms of mood and anxiety disorders alongside their responsibilities at work in ways that support both wellness and work-related outcomes?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Onset of aversive mental illness symptoms at work (e.g., low mood, irritability, panic, lethargy, trouble concentrating) that create tension with job demands.
- (Dis)engagement actions: Temporary shifts in work involvement, grouped into three disengagement types (physical, task, social) and three engagement types (physical, task, social). Participants often enacted multiple types simultaneously.
- Linking mechanisms: Three mechanisms connect (dis)engagement actions to outcomes — (1) suffering reduction (curtailing the aversive experience of symptoms), (2) image preservation (maintaining how others perceive them to avoid stigma), and (3) distraction (diverting attention from symptoms).
- Emergent outcomes: Wellness outcomes (survival; subjective well-being) and work-related outcomes (acceptable task performance; retention).
- Boundary conditions (not formally tested): Whether a given (dis)engagement type is feasible depends on contextual constraints such as remote vs. in-person work, job autonomy, and workplace norms around disclosure.

When symptoms arise, employees with mental illness agentically enact temporary shifts in work involvement rather than passively suffering or withdrawing permanently, pulling back through physical, task, or social disengagement or pushing in through increased physical, task, or social engagement. These actions operate through three mechanisms — reducing the suffering of aversive symptoms, preserving a professional image to avoid stigma, and distracting attention away from symptoms — which in turn produce wellness outcomes (survival and subjective well-being) and work-related outcomes (acceptable task performance and retention). The model frames disengagement not as disaffection or burnout but as a deliberate, responsible, short-duration strategy that lets employees navigate both wellness and work demands.
theoretical_contribution: The study augments the literature on mental illness at work — previously criticized for limited theoretical grounding — by articulating a process model in which employees with mental illness are agentic actors who use temporary (dis)engagement to navigate symptoms, rather than passive recipients of stigma or poor performers. Second, it extends embodiment theory by explicitly linking somatic phenomena with the cognitive and affective symptoms of mental illness, bridging a literature that has emphasized the body with one that has emphasized the mind. Third, it extends the (dis)engagement literature by distinguishing physical, task, and social forms and by showing that disengagement can function through suffering reduction and image preservation, not merely as resource replenishment breaks.
practical_implication: Managers should trust that temporary employee disengagement is often an intentional, pro-organizational act by employees with mental illness trying to preserve performance and wellness, and one-size-fits-all accommodation programs are unlikely to work. Managers can support employees by recognizing that informal social engagement (e.g., small talk) may serve as symptom management rather than distraction from work, and by reconsidering blanket employment restrictions on those with mental illness histories, which deter help-seeking and diagnosis.
limitations: The sample is overwhelmingly Caucasian (~80%), limiting the ability to examine how intersectional racial or ethnic backgrounds shape mental illness experiences at work. The authors did not explicitly investigate temporal dynamics, so they cannot determine how (dis)engagement behaviors might differ across short- versus long-term symptom trajectories. They also could not assess whether simultaneous combinations of (dis)engagement types are distinctly beneficial for particular outcomes.
future_research: Future work should take an intersectional perspective on race, ethnicity, culture, and gender in shaping mental illness experiences at work, and examine gender-specific (e.g., male) samples given masculine stereotypes around emotional suppression. Quantitative techniques such as latent profile analysis and longitudinal designs could test how combinations of (dis)engagement types relate to outcomes and how employees learn effective (dis)engagement strategies. The model's mechanisms (image preservation, suffering reduction) should be tested in adjacent populations such as those with chronic physical illness or those experiencing episodic life crises like divorce.
abstract: Based on 59 interviews with participants that have diagnosed mood and anxiety disorders, our findings elucidate that employees temporarily alter their work engagement—either by pulling back through various types of disengagement (physical, task, social) or by increasing their engagement in various elements of their work. Our findings show that disengagement is crucial in enabling employees with mental illness to balance symptom management and stable job performance. Our theoretical model specifies types of disengagement and engagement and the mechanisms that explain their relationship to wellness and work outcomes, including suffering reduction, image preservation, and distraction. These findings provide a nuanced view of the ways employees with mental illness navigate their symptoms, with implications for better understanding how mental illness influences affective, cognitive, and embodied experiences at work.
apa_citation: Rosado-Solomon, E. H., Thatcher, S. M. B., & Strizver, S. D. (2025). Navigating mental illness at work using disengagement and engagement pathways. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 971-999. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0430

title: Subordinating Humanism: How Colliding Beliefs about a Living Wage Shape Personal Fulfillment and “Professional-Class” Identities in Working-Class Jobs
methods: Qualitative, inductive field study based on 77 semi-structured interviews with 64 participants across two sites, plus site-visit observations and archival documents; analyzed via inductive coding (Charmaz, 2014).
industry: Financial technology services
units: Workers and managers at WageCo (pseudonym), a small private-sector U.S. firm that introduced a living wage; two sites (parent company and acquired subsidiary)
n_sample: 64 participants (51 workers, 13 managers); 77 interviews total
authors: Seegars, Lumumba; Lee, Serenity S.; Reid, Erin M.; Ramarajan, Lakshmi
theories: humanistic management; market-based ideology; psychological contracts; social class theory; social identity theory; work role identity
keywords: humanistic management; identity work; living wage; market-based ideology; pay-for-performance; professional-class; psychological contracts; social class identity; work role identity; working-class jobs
research_question: How does the introduction of a humanistic practice (a living wage) in a market-based context affect managers' and workers' identities?
mechanism_summary:
- Boundary condition: The U.S. setting, where market-based pay practices are taken for granted and professional-class identities are valorized; the authors note that the dynamics they observe may differ in countries with stronger social welfare infrastructure where market-based ideology is less dominant.

The living wage initially delivered its intended humanistic benefits (financial security and personal fulfillment), but the deeply entrenched market-based ideology linking pay to performance generated tension by framing the higher wage as undeserved. To restore a sense of deservingness, managers raised role expectations toward professional-class traits—autonomy and overwork—and workers internalized these demands, undertaking a partial class-based identity transition within the same working-class jobs. In parallel, managers reaffirmed their own role identities as performance shapers despite losing control over wages. The result is a multilevel process whereby identity work subordinates the humanistic practice to the market-based ideology it was meant to transform.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances research on humanistic management by showing how a transformative humanistic practice (a living wage) can be subordinated to market-based ideology through identity work at individual and interpersonal levels. It offers novel insights into class and work identity by demonstrating that changes in pay-setting can trigger class-based work role identity transitions even when tasks remain unchanged, and it argues that scholars of work identity must take wages seriously as a constitutive element of what it means "to be" a type of worker.
practical_implication: Leaders introducing humanistic practices such as living wages must explicitly communicate and protect their humanistic intent, train managers in humanistic management practices (hiring, performance reviews), and pair wage change with systemic transformation of governance, power distributions, and formal/informal practices. Piecemeal humanistic interventions embedded in a market-based society risk being subordinated, so leaders should also be externally facing advocates for broader humanistic management.
limitations: The study is a single-organization qualitative case based primarily on interview data, which carries risks of self-enhancement and retrospective sensemaking, and it cannot directly ascertain the CEO's true motivations. It also cannot adjudicate whether the opposite shift (humanistic to market-based pay) would produce symmetric effects, and its U.S. context with taken-for-granted market-based pay practices may not generalize to settings with stronger social welfare infrastructure.
future_research: Future work should use quantitative data to examine how living wages alter labor supply, hours, turnover, and workforce composition over time, and should track whether professional-class identity enactment tapers. Scholars should further unpack the meaning of autonomy in working-class jobs, study cases where worker and manager views of deservingness diverge, and adopt systems-psychodynamic perspectives to explore the underlying anxieties and defenses that sustain market-based identities during humanistic transitions.
abstract: In a society dominated by market-based ideology and management practices that prioritize financial considerations, some organizations are shifting toward humanistic ideology and practices that emphasize human welfare. To examine this transformation in pay-setting, we studied a U.S. company that introduced a living wage for its low-wage workers. Interviews with 64 participants across two sites revealed both intended and unintended effects. Motivated by humanistic aims, the living wage was designed to reduce financial insecurity; indeed, workers felt more financially secure and fulfilled in their personal lives. However, its humanistic intent conflicted with the dominant market-based ideology linking wages to performance, raising concerns about whether these workers deserved higher pay. To resolve this tension, managers and workers altered expectations for workers to reflect two aspects of professional-class roles: autonomy and overwork. As workers internalized these expectations, they adopted elements of professional-class identities while remaining in working-class jobs. Simultaneously, managers reaffirmed their own role identities as shapers of performance despite their weakened control over wages. These findings inform a multilevel model conceptualizing how a transformative humanistic practice can be subordinated to market-based ideology through identity work. We contribute to research on humanistic management and the interconnections between wage, class, work roles, and identity.
apa_citation: Seegars, L., Lee, S. S., Reid, E. M., & Ramarajan, L. (2025). Subordinating humanism: How colliding beliefs about a living wage shape personal fulfillment and "professional-class" identities in working-class jobs. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 939-970. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1289

title: Distributed Knowledge and the Creation of Public Value: Community-Based Organizing and Wildfire Management in Northern California
methods: Longitudinal quantitative design using an original community-year panel of fire prevention plans (FPPs) and fire histories in seven Northern California counties (1998-2018). Identification combines two-way fixed effects (TWFE) difference-in-differences, propensity-score matching, and two-stage least squares (2SLS) instrumental-variable DiD; stakeholder and collaboration variables coded from FPP documents; principal component analysis (PCA) used to classify cross-level collaboration types; robustness checks include alternative stakeholder measures, negative binomial models, and validation with insurance-claims data.
industry: Public-sector and community-based wildfire management (local, state, and federal fire agencies collaborating with self-organizing communities)
units: Community-year observations, where each observation is a yearly community-fire dyad in Del Norte, Humboldt, Siskiyou, Trinity, Shasta, Modoc, and Lassen counties
n_sample: 6,741 community-year observations across 321 communities; 103 unique fire prevention plan observations
authors: Stein, Devin; Minniti, Maria
theories: Hayekian distributed knowledge; Ostrom collective action theory; new institutional economics; new stakeholder theory; polycentric governance; public value theory
keywords: collective action; cross-level collaboration; distributed knowledge; fire prevention plans; local knowledge; noncontractual governance; public value; public-private collaborations; self-organizing communities; stakeholder participation; wildfire management
research_question: How and why do self-organizing communities, and their cross-level collaborations with higher-level public organizations, contribute to the creation of public value beyond what is achievable by public organizations alone? Specifically, how do stakeholder participation and the intensity of cross-level public–private collaborations shape public value outcomes such as wildfire-related property losses?
mechanism_summary: Building on Ostrom's governing principles for collective action and Hayek's notion of distributed local knowledge, the authors argue that self-organizing communities of vested and mutually vulnerable stakeholders can internalize coordination costs because private and public value overlap. Communities accumulate embedded local knowledge through trial-and-error learning, and cross-level collaborations with higher-level public organizations enable that local knowledge to be combined with "global" knowledge (e.g., scientific best practices and economies of scale), producing more contextualized and effective solutions to public problems. Broad stakeholder participation and extensive, active cross-level orchestration reinforce the benefits, though with diminishing returns once coordination costs outpace added knowledge.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances Ostrom-style collective action theory by explaining how and why self-organizing communities create public value alongside higher-level public organizations, rather than merely as substitutes for public governance. It reframes self-organizing communities as a distinct organizational form that functions as a repository of distributed local knowledge, and posits noncontractual cross-level collaborations as a governance mechanism that reduces knowledge (not just resource) constraints. In doing so, it enriches new stakeholder theory and the public–private collaborations literature by showing that the effectiveness of community-level public–private arrangements is contingent on the type and intensity of cross-level engagement.
practical_implication: For policy, the results support distributed, polycentric governance: local self-organizing with active orchestration of higher-level public agencies tends to outperform top-down application of homogenous best practices, with implications for wildfire management and for public education, water, sewage, roads, waste, healthcare, and public safety. For practice, managers and public officials can leverage noncontractual, relational governance mechanisms to mobilize community-based organizing, even when formal contracts are incomplete or infeasible due to high coordination costs.
limitations: The quasi-experimental and 2SLS-DiD design does not fully rule out endogeneity from unobservable reasons some communities face higher fire risk, though the likely direction of bias runs against the findings. Property losses are a limited proxy for public value since they combine public and private structures and do not capture all wildfire impacts. The authors cannot directly measure knowledge or knowledge flows and therefore cannot rule out alternative mechanisms such as improved coordination, incentive alignment, or richer resource environments.
future_research: Future work should test the boundary conditions of the theory in other contexts (e.g., education, water, sanitation, healthcare) and where community engagement or information asymmetries are less pronounced. Scholars could unpack the relative importance and interplay of stakeholder engagement, cross-level collaborations, and subsequent decision-making and resource use, and examine the specific content and mechanisms of knowledge dissemination within self-organizing communities. Finally, researchers could dig into communities' histories and leadership structures to explain the origins and evolution of self-organizing.
abstract: We build on extant theories of collective action and public–private collaborations to unpack the distinctive role self-organizing communities may play alongside other organizations in the creation of public value. We suggest that, when communities selforganize to address a common goal and, in particular, when they collaborate with higher-level public organizations, they discover more and better opportunities to reduce market frictions. Using an original longitudinal dataset of community-level fire prevention plans in Northern California from 1998 to 2018, we find that communities taking local actions and coordinating them with state and federal organizations manage wildfires better and experience fewer property losses than communities that do not organize. The number of stakeholders participating in these collaborations and the extent of the collaborations across hierarchical levels reinforce this effect. We conjecture that one possible mechanism explaining why self-organizing and cross-level collaborations tend to yield positive outcomes may be because they allow stakeholders to benefit from otherwise idle local knowledge. Unpacking the role played by self-organizing communities and their cross-level collaborations is important because it may enable contextualized solutions and prevent the potentially ineffective application of homogenous practices to heterogeneous problems, even in the absence of formal contracts.
apa_citation: Stein, D., & Minniti, M. (2025). Distributed knowledge and the creation of public value: Community-based organizing and wildfire management in Northern California. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1000-1030. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1331

title: Temporary Regulations and Institutional Change: Insights from the Brazilian World Cup Experience
methods: Qualitative historical case study with abductive analysis; 52 semi-structured interviews (professionals and fans), 611 newspaper articles (1995-2023), 66 legal/governmental documents, industry reports, FIFA documents, and participant observation at eight matches; open, axial, and abductive coding following Gioia methodology and Langley process approaches to build a process model of institutional change.
industry: Professional football/soccer and beer industries
units: Institutional actors surrounding beer regulation in stadiums (beer producers, stadium security, public administrators, journalists, academics, activists, football fans, legislators)
n_sample: 52 interviews (31 professionals, 21 fans); 611 newspaper articles; 66 legal documents; 8 observed matches
authors: Bitektine, Alex; Dolbec, Pierre-Yann; Martins, Michele; Kruidenier, Stijn
theories: institutional learning; institutional theory; institutional work; practice theory
keywords: counter-normative practice; institutional change; institutional learning; institutional maintenance; institutional work; practice variation; process model; temporary regulations
research_question: How and why can temporary regulatory changes—regulations adopted for a limited time and not expected to continue beyond an established end date—lead to lasting institutional change, even when their initial introduction conflicts with prevailing societal understandings and actors involved have no intent to transform institutions?
mechanism_summary: A temporary regulation creates a bounded time window in which a counter-normative practice can be performed and observed at scale. Because actors expect the practice to revert when the regulation lapses, they tolerate it and engage in institutional maintenance work (training, standardizing, gentrifying participants, discursively framing) to contain anticipated negative outcomes. Successfully containing these outcomes unintendedly generates new societal understandings about the practice's risks and viability—a form of institutional learning distinct from socialization to conformity. Interested actors then leverage these new understandings to challenge the old regulation and push for permanent change, resolving three sequential tensions between regulations, understandings, and practices.
theoretical_contribution: The paper identifies a previously unexplored path of institutional change through temporary regulations, developing a process model in which maintenance work—typically a force for stability—unintendedly drives change. It extends institutional work scholarship by showing how practice deviations can occur even in highly visible, tightly controlled settings when temporary regulations create bounded time periods shielded from maintenance pressures. It also reframes institutional learning as learning about "how things can be done differently," distinct from socialization-based learning, positioning it as a critical precursor to institutional change.
practical_implication: Temporary regulations can be a valuable policy tool for reducing uncertainty, enabling institutional learning, and complementing experimentalist governance approaches in domains facing polycrisis conditions such as climate change, AI, and pandemics. Policymakers can pilot counter-normative arrangements (e.g., emission standards, work-from-home policies) under time-limited rules, observe outcomes, and use accumulated learning to design durable solutions. However, temporary regulations can also be misused to establish states of exception and erode democratic institutions, so safeguards are essential.
limitations: The study builds theory from a single context (beer regulations in Brazilian stadiums around the 2014 World Cup), so transferability requires care. The authors identify five boundary conditions (resistance must be overcome, broad observability, favorable perceived outcomes, generation of novel understandings, and legal/political opportunity for renegotiation) whose generalizability requires empirical validation. The qualitative historical design limits causal inference and depends on retrospective accounts and media sources of varying credibility.
future_research: Future work should explore how temporariness shapes actors' willingness to tolerate counter-normative arrangements, empirically validate the boundary conditions and examine varying durations and visibility levels of temporary regulations, and investigate how "success" is socially constructed when actors lack shared interests, coordination, or power. Researchers should also further develop the underexplored role of institutional learning—beyond maintenance-oriented socialization—in driving institutional change.
abstract: While institutional theory has extensively examined how durable regulations contribute to institutional change, little is known about how temporary regulations—pervasive in modern societies—can transform institutions. This study examines how and why temporary regulations can lead to lasting institutional change by analyzing Brazil’s temporary legalization of beer sales and consumption in stadiums during the 2014 FIFA World Cup. Using extensive qualitative data, we develop a process model explaining how the introduction of a temporary regulation creates an opportunity for society to experience a counter-normative practice and learn from it. During this period, multiple institutional actors—while pursuing objectives far removed from considerations of institutional change—perform various types of institutional work to contain the practice’s expected negative outcomes. This institutional work unintendedly generates new societal understandings about the practice’s risks and consequences, which interested actors then leverage to achieve lasting institutional change. We advance institutional theory by identifying an unexplored path of institutional change through temporary regulations, demonstrating how temporary regulations can enable practice variations and institutional learning, and revealing how institutional maintenance work can drive change. Our insights suggest that temporary regulations can be valuable policy tools for reducing uncertainty and enabling learning about alternative institutional arrangements.
apa_citation: Bitektine, A., Dolbec, P.-Y., Martins, M., & Kruidenier, S. (2025). Temporary regulations and institutional change: Insights from the Brazilian World Cup experience. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1168-1206. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1135
