title: When Dirty Work Inspires: The Joint Effect of Work Dirtiness and Perceived Stigma on Pro-Organizational Behavior
methods: Multi-study quantitative design combining three field surveys and one scenario-based experiment. Study 1: cross-sectional survey of 179 Chinese at-home care nurses from 27 hospitals, analyzed with path modeling accounting for hospital-level clustering. Study 2: three-wave time-lagged online survey of 302 U.S. employees across diverse occupations/industries with temporally separated predictors and outcomes. Study 3: scenario-based experiment manipulating high vs. low work dirtiness in a fitness-trainer ('Iron House') context with U.S. full-time workers, testing interaction with measured occupational stigma. Study 4: two-week experience-sampling study with daily surveys of 194 Chinese employees (1,798 person-day observations) analyzed with multilevel structural equation modeling. All four studies used Johnson-Neyman analyses to probe the interaction between work dirtiness and perceived occupational stigma on OCB, with moderated-mediation tests via organizational identification.
industry: Study 1: healthcare (at-home nursing). Study 2: mixed (technology, manufacturing, public sector, healthcare, retail, service). Study 3: fitness training (experimental scenario) with full-time U.S. workers. Study 4: mixed (information/software/technology, manufacturing, finance, hospitality, public hygiene and social services, retail, construction, real estate, transportation, public administration, entertainment, others).
country: Study 1: China; Study 2: United States; Study 3: United States; Study 4: China
units: Individual employees; Study 4 also uses daily (person-day) observations
n_sample: Study 1: 179 nurses from 27 hospitals; Study 2: 302 employees (three-wave); Study 3: U.S. full-time workers in a scenario experiment; Study 4: 194 employees with 1,798 daily observations; plus two supplementary studies (168 restaurant servers and 128 U.S. security guards) testing identity accessibility
authors: Peng, A. C.; Kim, D.; Schaubroeck, J. M.; Zeng, W.
theories: self-categorization theory; social identity theory
keywords: dirty work; normative fit; organizational citizenship behavior; organizational identification; perceived occupational stigma; pro-organizational behavior; self-categorization theory; social identity salience; work dirtiness
research_question: Why do many individuals performing dirty work engage positively with their jobs and organizations whereas others become disengaged? Specifically, how do the experience of performing dirty tasks (work dirtiness) and the perception of being stigmatized by the public (perceived occupational stigma) jointly shape pro-organizational behavior through processes of social-identity salience.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on self-categorization theory, the authors argue that perceived occupational stigma renders a stigmatized work-related social category cognitively accessible, and that engaging in dirty tasks supplies the "normative fit" that activates this social identity by matching behavior to societal stereotypes about the job. When both work dirtiness and perceived stigma are high, the work-related social identity becomes salient, heightening organizational identification and motivating employees to support coworkers, customers, and the organization through OCBs. When perceived stigma is low, high work dirtiness no longer creates normative fit; instead, dirty tasks are appraised as a threat to personal identity, prompting employees to psychologically distance themselves from the organization and reducing pro-organizational behavior.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends dirty-work research by providing a theoretical account of why individuals engaged in dirty work develop a salient work-related social identity rather than merely describing the tactics dirty workers use once that identity is presumably already salient. It conceptually and empirically disentangles "work dirtiness" (the direct experience of degrading tasks) from "perceived occupational stigma" (beliefs about societal views of the occupation), showing that the two are distinct constructs that operate jointly via normative fit to determine identity salience and pro-organizational behavior. In doing so, the authors reconcile contradictory prior findings showing both positive and negative responses to dirty work and broaden attention from occupational identity to organizational identity as a locus of social categorization.
practical_implication: Managers of jobs involving dirty tasks should recognize that employees who do not perceive strong occupational stigma are more likely to interpret work dirtiness as a personal-identity threat and disengage, so these employees — not only those who already hold strong stigma beliefs — warrant proactive communication framing dirty tasks as meaningful and professionally skilled work. Symbolic management efforts (stories, rituals, language, status symbols) are more effective when they explicitly acknowledge the unfavorable societal stereotypes about the job rather than ignoring them, because highlighting those stereotypes reinforces normative fit and strengthens the work-related social identity that drives pro-organizational behavior.
limitations: Three of the four studies relied on self-reports, which may introduce common method bias, though the authors mitigated this through temporal separation, experimental manipulation, and daily repeated measures, and note that interaction effects cannot be explained by common method bias. Data come only from the United States and China, limiting generalizability to other cultural contexts, and occupational stigma was conceptualized and measured at the individual level rather than as a shared group perception. The theory and tests focused on between-person processes; the authors examined but did not find support for a within-person daily process linking fluctuations in work dirtiness to identity salience and OCB.
future_research: Future work should test the theory using externally rated pro-organizational behavior, examine whether group-level shared perceptions of stigma add explanatory power beyond individual perceptions, and explore "incongruent" cases such as workers in tainted occupations performing ordinary tasks or workers in low-stigma occupations performing highly dirty tasks. Researchers could also investigate pivotal or crucial moments (e.g., a police officer using force) that temporarily heighten work dirtiness and activate social identity, and consider how comparative fit (alongside normative fit) and other forms of societal stigma (e.g., discrimination, social exclusion) guide identity processes and workplace behavior.
abstract: Research reveals that many employees derive esteem and pride from their work despite indignities attributed to their occupations. Whereas studies have identified what these “dirty workers” think and do to maintain a positive work identity, it remains unclear why they do so whereas others tend to disengage from work. We draw from selfcategorization theory to develop a model that conceptualizes how the salience of one’s social identity is determined jointly by engaging in dirty tasks (i.e., work dirtiness) and perceived occupational stigma. When perceiving that others in society stigmatize one’s occupation, high work dirtiness creates a match between one’s behavior and societal expectations linked to one’s job. Based on this mechanism of normative fit, work-related social identity is salient, encouraging pro-organizational behavior. Conversely, we argue that when stigma is perceived as low, individuals experiencing high dirtiness at work become more concerned about their personal identity, reducing engagement with their organization. Results from four studies using survey and experimental designs largely support our hypotheses. This research reconciles seemingly contradictory results concerning employee responses toward dirty work and provides a theoretical explanation for why people with dirty jobs engage positively with their work and organizations.
apa_citation: Peng, A. C., Kim, D., Schaubroeck, J. M., & Zeng, W. (2025). When dirty work inspires: The joint effect of work dirtiness and perceived stigma on pro-organizational behavior. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 149-175. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0108

title: On the Move: The Impact of Internal Mobility and Internal Comobility on Unit- and Organization-Level Outcomes
methods: Longitudinal panel study using monthly store-level data from a Fortune 500 U.S. retail firm (2016-2018); two-way fixed-effects regressions (store and month fixed effects) for established stores and separate cross-sectional OLS for new stores; rolling six-month measures of transfers in/out decomposed into comobile, multisource, and multi-destination categories; focal DV is monthly retail sales
industry: Retail (single Fortune 500 U.S. retail firm)
units: Retail stores (establishment-months); 884 established stores and 259 new stores
n_sample: 26,910 store-month observations for established stores; 259 new stores for the new-unit comparison
authors: Ray, C.; Nyberg, A. J.; Maltarich, M. A.; Reilly, G.
theories: human capital resources (HCR) theory; human capital theory; social capital theory
keywords: comobile transfers; firm-specific human capital; human capital resources; internal comobility; internal mobility; multisource transfers; social capital; transfers in; transfers out; unit performance
research_question: Whether, when, and why does internal mobility (transfers into and out of a unit) affect unit- and organization-level performance, and how do the attributes of transfers—specifically comobile versus multisource transfers in, and comobile versus multi-destination transfers out—shape those effects?
mechanism_summary: The authors integrate human capital resources (HCR) theory with the internal mobility literature to argue that internal transfers reconfigure a unit's HCR in two distinct ways: by importing/exporting firm-specific human capital (knowledge, skills, and abilities honed to the organization) and by moving intact social structures when employees travel together. Transfers in should raise receiving-unit performance because firm-specific human capital aligns decisions with organizational routines and raises productivity; multisource transfers in add breadth of unit-specific knowledge while retaining enough firm-wide overlap to communicate, yielding larger gains than comobile transfers in (whose value rests on pre-existing social ties that are redundant with firm-specific familiarity). Transfers out should depress supplying-unit performance by removing firm-specific HCR, and comobile transfers out should amplify that loss by dissolving intact social structures. These amplified effects should be stronger in new units, where path-dependent history and established routines do not yet buffer HCR change.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes internal mobility from an individual-level phenomenon into a collective-level mechanism for HCR reallocation across units, introducing new constructs (comobile vs. multisource transfers in; comobile vs. multi-destination transfers out) and showing that their effects on unit and organizational outcomes diverge. By theorizing and testing the net organizational effect—gains in receiving units net of losses in supplying units—it answers Dalton's (1997) long-standing "domino effect" question and shows that reallocating firm-specific HCR is value-creating for the firm overall only when transfers in are broadly sourced. It also contributes to the macro resource-reallocation literature by treating human capital as a strategically allocated, nonfinancial resource distinct from financial capital.
practical_implication: Managers should actively reallocate internal talent rather than minimize disruption, but they should source transfers from as many different units as possible rather than moving intact groups. In practical terms, sourcing one transfer each from several supplying units yields substantially larger net gains than moving a comobile cluster from a single unit; transferring more than one worker from any given supplying unit brings diminishing benefit and may be outweighed by the cost to that unit. The benefits are largest when populating new units or stores, where firm-specific knowledge from multisource movers has the strongest effect on early performance.
limitations: The study uses data from a single U.S. Fortune 500 retail firm, so generalizability to other industries, countries, and organizational forms is untested. HCR is theorized as the underlying mechanism but is not measured directly — the authors do not observe the quality of transferred human capital, the content of relationships, or diversity of knowledge structures within units. All transferred employees are treated as equivalent even though they occupied different roles (e.g., managers vs. frontline workers), and the analyses cannot separate managerial from non-managerial transfer effects.
future_research: Future research should test boundary conditions across industries and settings, examine the duration and decay of transfer effects on HCR over longer horizons, and directly measure the quality of transferred human capital, the content of pre-existing relationships, and within-unit knowledge diversity to test the proposed mechanism more cleanly. Scholars should also model the causes of transfer decisions (e.g., employee self-selection into higher-performing units), explore more complex HCR configurations such as fault lines and subgroup dynamics among transfers, and untangle role-specific effects (e.g., manager versus frontline transfers) on unit outcomes.
abstract: Internal mobility is commonly used to reallocate talent within organizations. Despite its prevalence and potential implications for organizational performance, most prior work has focused on the individual-level outcomes associated with internal mobility. That is, there has been relatively limited theoretical and empirical attention to the mechanisms that connect internal mobility to unitand organization-level outcomes, making it difficult to predict when or why internal mobility is beneficial to organizations. To address this oversight, we draw from human and social capital literatures to build theory to predict when and why internal mobility will have unit-level implications. We test this by examining a period of growth in a Fortune 500 company and evaluate gains and losses associated with moving employees within firms, including distinguishing between two types of internal movers—those who come from different units in the organization (solo movers) and those who come from the same unit in the organization (comobile movers). In doing so, we contribute to both internal mobility and strategic human capital resource literatures and provide actionable insights regarding when to source internal movers, and where from.
apa_citation: Ray, C., Nyberg, A. J., Maltarich, M. A., & Reilly, G. (2025). On the move: The impact of internal mobility and internal comobility on unit- and organization-level outcomes. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 76-98. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0311

title: Anticipatory Impression Management for Potential Adverse Events: Positive Framing in the Wake of Short Seller Attacks on a Competitor
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis of 11,824 firm-quarter observations (1,989 firms with a direct competitor attacked by a short seller, 2007-2022), using content analysis of CEO earnings-call transcripts with Loughran-McDonald sentiment dictionaries; fixed-effects regression with firm and year controls; TNIC-3 text-based competitor identification; robustness checks including alternative estimation windows and unscaled dependent variable.
industry: Publicly traded firms across multiple industries listed on NASDAQ or NYSE
units: firm-quarters (CEO earnings conference calls at firms whose TNIC-3 competitors were attacked by short sellers)
n_sample: 1,989 firms; 344 unique short attacks; 11,824 firm-quarter observations
authors: Song, R.; Connelly, B. L.; Ketchen, D. J.; Shi, W.
theories: anticipatory impression management; awareness-motivation-capability (AMC) perspective; impression management theory
keywords: CEO communication; anticipatory impression management; awareness-motivation-capability; competitor spillover; earnings conference calls; positive framing; short seller attacks; theory elaboration
research_question: Under what conditions do firms engage in anticipatory impression management (AIM) — specifically, CEO positive framing in earnings conference calls — following a short seller attack on a competitor, and how do awareness, motivation, and capability shape this response?
mechanism_summary: When a short seller attacks a competitor, focal-firm CEOs anticipate that they could be the next target and use positive language in earnings calls to persuade short sellers to look elsewhere. Drawing on Chen's (1996) awareness-motivation-capability logic, the authors argue the AIM response is amplified when the focal firm is more aware the threat applies to them (high product-market overlap with the attacked firm), more motivated to act (the attacked competitor suffered a severe stock-price decline), and more capable of credibly projecting positivity (strong recent performance makes the framing believable). Positive framing functions as a deterrent signal that raises short sellers' expected cost of attacking the focal firm.
theoretical_contribution: The study elaborates AIM theory by extending it from known upcoming events with uncertain consequences to potential upcoming events with known adverse consequences, shifting uncertainty from the response to the event itself. By importing Chen's awareness-motivation-capability framework, it specifies the boundary conditions under which anticipation of a potential adverse event triggers AIM and redirects AIM from an investor audience toward an adversarial audience (short sellers). Secondarily, it contributes to the short-selling literature by demonstrating a previously overlooked spillover effect of attacks on non-targeted competitors and showing that CEO language can operate as an ex ante attack-prevention tool.
practical_implication: CEOs should treat a short seller attack on a competitor as a signal to proactively shape firm narrative in earnings calls, since positive framing is associated with reduced short interest and can deter attacks before they happen. Boards can encourage CEOs to use AIM selectively, drawing executives' attention to product-market similarity, the severity of the competitor's stock-price decline, and the firm's own credibility to perform the tactic. However, firms with weak recent performance should be cautious: overly positive language without supporting fundamentals is not credible and can backfire by raising investor expectations.
limitations: The authors could not fully account for why particular firms are susceptible to short seller attacks, and their archival data cannot directly observe the CEO decision-making process that leads to AIM. The goodness-of-fit improvement of the full model over the baseline was only marginal, possibly reflecting nonlinearities or an excess of nonsignificant controls. The study also focuses on one class of potential adverse event (short seller attacks), leaving open how AIM operates against other anticipated threats.
future_research: Scholars could test these ideas experimentally (e.g., policy-capturing studies with executives) to unpack decision-making; explore other forms of awareness such as shared buyers/suppliers or socially interconnected firms; integrate AIM with protection motivation theory, threat rigidity theory, signaling theory, and the attention-based view to build a multiplex theory of proactive responses; and examine other potential adverse events (hostile takeovers, market entry, labor disputes, regulatory changes) where CEO language may serve as a first line of defense.
abstract: Anticipatory impression management (AIM) provides a theoretical foundation for understanding the tactics organizations use to mitigate the potential adverse consequences of known upcoming events. We extend this theorizing to encompass potential upcoming events with known adverse consequences. We posit that a potential event is more likely to induce AIM to the extent that the firm is aware the event might occur, motivated to mitigate its potential consequences, and capable of engaging in tactics that could reduce the event’s likelihood. We test our theorizing in the scenario wherein a competitor has been attacked by a short seller, which raises the specter of a short seller attack on the focal firm. We contend that, following an attack on a competitor, chief executive officers use positive framing in earnings conference calls as an AIM tactic to deter short sellers. Examination of 1,989 firms with a direct competitor attacked by a short seller confirms our ideas. Our study thus extends the boundaries of theory on AIM and broadens research on short selling to encompass principles of attack prevention.
apa_citation: Song, R., Connelly, B. L., Ketchen, D. J., & Shi, W. (2025). Anticipatory impression management for potential adverse events: Positive framing in the wake of short seller attacks on a competitor. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 99-122. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0914

title: Memento Mori: mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption
methods: Cross-sectional online survey; moderated mediation analysis using Hayes' PROCESS macro Model 9 with bootstrap confidence intervals (5,000 resamples)
industry: General consumers (online survey panel)
units: Adults aged over 25
authors: Bae, H.; Jo, S. H.
theories: socioemotional selectivity theory; terror management theory
keywords: Future time perspective; Mortality legacy; Mortality salience; Socioemotional selectivity theory; Sustainable consumption; Terror management theory
research_question: How does mortality salience influence sustainable consumption behavior, and do mortality legacy (as mediator) and limited future time perspective and chronological age (as moderators) shape this relationship, drawing on terror management theory and socioemotional selectivity theory?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on terror management theory, the authors argue that awareness of death prompts individuals to manage existential anxiety through distal defenses such as upholding cultural worldviews and boosting self-esteem. In contemporary society, sustainability functions as a dominant moral worldview, so mortality salience heightens consumers' desire to leave a mortality legacy — a lasting positive contribution that transcends physical death — which in turn channels behavior into sustainable consumption as a form of symbolic immortality. Integrating socioemotional selectivity theory, the authors further theorize that consumers with a limited future time perspective perceive time as scarce and therefore become more motivated to engage in generative, legacy-oriented behaviors, strengthening the mediated path from mortality salience to sustainable consumption via mortality legacy. Chronological age was hypothesized to operate similarly but was not supported empirically.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends terror management theory by demonstrating a novel, constructive distal defense: people adopt sustainable consumption behavior, via the desire for a mortality legacy, as a way to manage death-related anxiety and achieve symbolic immortality. It contributes to the literature on antecedents of sustainable consumption by identifying mortality salience and legacy motivation as psychological drivers, and it advances socioemotional selectivity theory by showing that limited future time perspective — but not chronological age — moderates the indirect effect, suggesting that psychological rather than chronological markers of time horizon matter most in mortality-driven sustainable consumption.
practical_implication: Marketers and policymakers can promote sustainable consumption by designing communications that subtly elicit consumers' awareness of mortality and foster a desire to leave a lasting legacy, framing sustainability around "protecting what matters for future generations" or "leaving a meaningful mark" rather than using explicit fear-based appeals. Because age did not moderate the effect, such interventions can be deployed broadly without age-specific tailoring, though they should be implemented ethically and transparently. Managers should also consider consumers' future time perspective when deploying mortality salience appeals, since legacy motivations are strongest among those who perceive their time as limited.
limitations: The survey data were collected only in the USA, which may limit generalizability, especially given that consumer responses to mortality salience, mortality legacy, and sustainable consumption may differ between Asian and Western cultures. The study did not empirically compare the relative strength of opposing psychological mechanisms — helplessness or avoidance versus legacy motives — that mortality salience can elicit. The lack of a significant age moderation effect also invites reconsideration of how chronological age operates within these processes.
future_research: Future research should examine whether mortality salience similarly affects sustainable consumption behavior in Asian cultural contexts (e.g., South Korea, China, Japan) where religious traditions such as Buddhism may shape death-related cognition. Researchers should explore alternative moderating variables such as cultural values, personal environmental attitudes, and socioeconomic status, and investigate psychological markers of age (e.g., subjective, felt, or cognitive age) rather than chronological age. Further work should also examine boundary conditions under which helplessness versus legacy motives dominate as responses to mortality salience.
abstract: Purpose – The purpose of this study is to advance the understanding of the interplay between mortality salience, mortality legacy and sustainable consumption behavior. Design/methodology/approach – Data were collected through an online questionnaire from 1,000 consumers aged over 25 in the USA. Findings – The results show that mortality salience induces sustainable behavior and that mortality legacy mediates this relationship. Moreover, a limited future time perspective is found to moderate the mediation process in the path from mortality salience to mortality legacy. Furthermore, age did not emerge as a significant moderator of the indirect effect of mortality salience on sustainable consumption behavior through mortality legacy. Originality/value – The findings connect the terror management theory with the socioemotional selectivity theory. They also reveal that marketers can nudge consumers’ sustainable consumption behavior by incorporating mortality salience and legacy appeals into their marketing communications.
apa_citation: Bae, H., & Jo, S. H. (2025). Memento mori: Mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-09-2025-8257

title: 2025 Presidential Address: Activism, Impact and Science: Management Scholarship in a Challenging Era
methods: Editorial essay (AOM Presidential Address); reflective argumentation with illustrative references to prior research and AOM initiatives
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Bamberger, P.
theories: engaged scholarship
keywords: academic activism; academic freedom; business school accreditation; engaged scholarship; global inclusion; open science; rigor and relevance; scholar-practitioner gap; scholarly impact; scientific integrity
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Bamberger advances a normative argument rather than a causal model. He contends that management scholarship can have impact only if it is relevant, but that relevance must be pursued without sacrificing rigor or scientific credibility. He argues that the payoff structures governing scholarship (citation-based metrics, high-impact journal counts, and ranking systems) incentivize questionable research practices and crowd out socially useful work; that academic activism, while understandable, can undermine open science and fuel anti-intellectualism when advocacy shapes design and execution; and that engaged scholarship — agenda-setting, globally inclusive, and communicated accessibly to practitioners and publics — is the route by which management science can simultaneously enhance rigor, relevance, and credibility.
theoretical_contribution: The address synthesizes three AOM-led initiatives into a programmatic vision for the field: (a) reforming the definition and measurement of scholarly impact through an AACSB-led coalition aimed at updating accreditation and ranking criteria to reward evidence-based real-world impact; (b) reaffirming academic freedom alongside open science and scientific integrity, with explicit warnings about how advocacy-driven research erodes credibility; and (c) building bridges for global relevance by extending AOM's infrastructure to underrepresented regions and by translating research for practitioner and public audiences. Bamberger frames these as components of Van de Ven's engaged scholarship, arguing that this stance is how the field can "set the agenda" on issues such as human-capital sustainability, business as a vehicle for peace and development, and redesigning educational organizations.
practical_implication: Business school deans, journal editors, publishers, and tenure committees should adjust incentive structures so that evidence-based, real-world impact and diverse forms of contribution — not just theoretical novelty or high-impact-journal placement — are recognized and rewarded. Scholars studying politically loaded topics (e.g., DEI, pay transparency, inequality) should keep advocacy out of research design and execution and report findings fully, including inconvenient ones. The field should invest in globally inclusive infrastructure (memberships, workshops, open-access outlets, rotating meetings) and in practitioner-accessible communication channels such as AOM Today so that research can reach and inform decision-makers.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Bamberger calls on the field to move from reactive to agenda-setting research, including work that: (a) broadens sustainability research to encompass human-capital sustainability and work-based interventions; (b) examines how business organizations can serve as vehicles for sustainable economic development and regional conflict resolution; and (c) develops and tests new models of educational organizations to replace industrial-scale public schooling grounded in scientific management.
abstract: 2025 PRESIDENTIAL ADDRESS: ACTIVISM, IMPACT AND SCIENCE: MANAGEMENT SCHOLARSHIP IN A CHALLENGING ERA PETER BAMBERGER Tel Aviv University

title: Englishization Beyond the Global North: Dependency, Struggle, and Resistance in Brazilian Management Research
methods: Qualitative study; 55 semi-structured interviews with Brazilian management academics and journal editors conducted in three waves (2020-2023); abductive analysis with constant comparison, line-by-line coding, and axial coding
industry: Higher education / management academia
units: Management academics (young and senior researchers) and journal editors in Brazilian management academia
n_sample: 55 interviews
authors: Boussebaa, M.; Alcadipani, R.; Vianna, F.; Pina e Cunha, M.; Clegg, S.
theories: dependency theory; postcolonial theory
keywords: Brazil; Englishization; Frankenstein papers; Global North; Global South; academic dependency; counter-hegemonic resistance; dependency theory; management research; postcolonial ambivalence; postcolonial theory; strategic compliance
research_question: How are imperatives to publish in English experienced within Brazilian management academia, and how do dynamics of dependency and postcoloniality shape these experiences?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: academic dependency (Alatas); postcolonial ambivalence and mimicry (Bhabha); Global North–South epistemic hierarchies; intra-national institutional divide between elite management schools and public universities
- Process / phases (the paper's three overarching themes): (1) Navigating Englishization — institutional pressures and strategic appropriation by Brazilian journals and evaluation systems; (2) Struggling with Englishization — linguistic barriers and institutional/material constraints producing "Frankenstein" papers; (3) Contesting Englishization — strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance
- Modes of dependency: pressure from international journal rankings; Qualis and SciELO evaluation systems rewarding English-language publication; elite schools' international-publication expectations; Brazilian journals' own adoption of English in pursuit of visibility
- Practices / acts of resistance: perfunctory mimicry "without mastery" via cheap translations; editors' reluctant compliance while criticizing English as colonization; refusing editorial roles in English-only journals; founding Portuguese-only journals (e.g., RECADM, RCD); requesting exclusion from Qualis; withdrawing from doctoral programs requiring English
- Boundary conditions: elite management schools vs. public universities; availability of translation/editing funds; Brazilian currency devaluation; academic seniority and bilingual capital; journals' ranking status on international lists

Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, the paper theorizes Englishization as both reflective and constitutive of academic dependency: global Anglophone hierarchies and rankings pressure Global South research systems to align with Northern epistemic norms, and domestic institutions — Qualis, SciELO, and Brazilian journals themselves — internalize these standards in pursuit of international visibility. Within this structural terrain, materially constrained academics and editors engage in hybrid, ambivalent practices that simultaneously reproduce and destabilize the dominance of English. Englishization is thus enacted as a contested, uneven process shaped by the interplay of Global North–South inequalities, domestic institutional logics, and constrained local agency.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed account of Englishization by (1) challenging the assumption that Englishization unfolds uniformly across national contexts and foregrounding intra-national institutional variation (elite management schools vs. public universities); (2) conceptualizing Englishization as both reflective and constitutive of academic dependency, reproduced through domestic institutions' strategic appropriation of Anglo-American norms under structural constraint; and (3) demonstrating that Englishization is actively negotiated through hybrid practices—strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance—rather than being simply adopted or resisted. By bridging dependency and postcolonial theory, the paper advances a politically grounded understanding of Englishization as entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
practical_implication: Business schools, journal editors, and research-evaluation bodies in the Global South should recognize that imposing English-language publication requirements without supporting linguistic and material resources can produce poorly translated "Frankenstein" papers that undermine rather than enhance international visibility. Domestic evaluation systems (e.g., Qualis, SciELO) and local journals should reconsider policies that reward English-language publication as a proxy for quality, and support linguistic diversity and Portuguese-language scholarship as legitimate venues. More broadly, decolonizing management knowledge production requires attending to the intra-national inequalities and material constraints that shape whose scholarship becomes visible in global academic circuits.
limitations: Findings from Brazil may not generalize to other Global South contexts with different colonial histories, economic structures, and academic traditions. The study focused on academics and journal editors but paid limited attention to subfields or subdisciplines within management, and its interpretive qualitative design limits broad generalizability and may reflect self-reporting biases.
future_research: Future studies should investigate Englishization in other Global South countries (e.g., China, former British and French colonies) and use comparative cross-national designs to examine how different colonial legacies and academic traditions shape dependency, struggle, and resistance. Research should also explore disciplinary variation in Englishization within Brazilian and other Global South contexts, use quantitative methods to systematically examine patterns of Englishization and its impact on visibility, and extend analysis to teaching and service domains of academic work.
abstract: This paper advances understanding of Englishization in management research by addressing a key limitation in the existing literature: its predominant focus on the Global North, especially Europe. Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, we examine how management academics experience and navigate Englishization in Brazil. Based on qualitative research, we found that Brazilian academics, like their European counterparts, faced growing pressures to publish in international journals, effectively compelling publication in English, though such expectations were largely concentrated in elite institutions. We also found that Brazilian journals themselves increasingly adopted English in pursuit of international visibility, thereby intensifying pressure to publish in English and extending Englishization beyond the elite. One consequence was the publication of “Frankenstein” papers—the result of limited English proficiency and financial constraints—which paradoxically risked undermining the visibility Brazilian journals sought to enhance. Yet Englishization was not passively accepted: it was actively negotiated through strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance. These findings contribute to a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed understanding of Englishization by applying postcolonial and dependency perspectives to highlight its uneven trajectory across and within national contexts and to show how it is entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
apa_citation: Boussebaa, M., Alcadipani, R., Vianna, F., Pina e Cunha, M., & Clegg, S. (2025). Englishization beyond the Global North: Dependency, struggle, and resistance in Brazilian management research. *Academy of Management Learning & Education*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2023.0245

title: Social Media Is a Threat for Democracy! A Political Perspective for Analysing and Diminishing Harm
methods: Conceptual essay developing a political perspective on social media platforms. The authors build the argument in three moves: (1) a critical review of two dominant management lenses on platform governance (market and technology perspectives) and their democratic-theoretical foundations; (2) theoretical synthesis drawing on Habermasian deliberative democracy and the political view of the firm to reconceptualize social media companies as political actors responsible for the public sphere; (3) derivation of four deliberative capacities (transparency and accountability, openness and inclusiveness, conduciveness to argumentation, consequentiality) with illustrative examples and multi-level (platform, regulatory, individual) reform proposals. No empirical data collection or analysis.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Castelló, I.; Colleoni, E.; Scherer, A. G.; Trittin-Ulbrich, H.
theories: deliberative democracy (Habermas); participatory democratic theory; political CSR / political view of the firm (Scherer and Palazzo); public sphere theory; representative democratic theory; surveillance capitalism (Zuboff)
keywords: artificial intelligence; deliberation; democracy; digital platforms; governance; organized immaturity; political view of the firm; public sphere; social media; surveillance capitalism
research_question: Is social media threatening the democratic capacity of our societies, and if so, what should we do about it? The authors ask how management scholarship can re-conceptualize social media platforms — beyond market and technological lenses — as political actors whose governance shapes the deliberative quality of the public sphere.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Social media platforms are political actors (not merely market intermediaries or technical infrastructures) that produce and regulate a public sphere, and therefore bear responsibility for its deliberative quality.
- Key constructs: Deliberative capacity of the public sphere; four democratic capacities — (1) transparency and accountability, (2) openness and inclusiveness, (3) conduciveness to argumentation, (4) consequentiality.
- Boundary conditions / existing lenses: Market perspective (representative democracy; transparency + accountability) and technology perspective (participatory democracy; openness + inclusion) — each necessary but individually insufficient for a well-functioning public sphere.
- Proposed mechanisms for reform: Platform-level redesign of algorithmic architectures (e.g., diversity-preserving recommendation, sustained engagement with critical issues); regulatory-level frameworks for platform accountability and deliberative governance; individual-level digital literacy and citizenship education.

The political perspective argues that platforms' business models — surveillance-based attention maximization, algorithmic amplification of emotional engagement, and concentrated ownership — systematically erode the conditions for Habermasian rational-critical discourse, producing polarization, hate speech, misinformation, and democratic fragmentation. Market fixes (fact-checking, antitrust, transparency about algorithms) address information quality but not deliberative quality; technological fixes (decentralization, blockchain verification, open-source protocols) address openness and inclusion but not argumentation or democratic will formation. Enhancing deliberative capacity therefore requires adding conduciveness to argumentation (platform affordances that foster reasoned exchange rather than reactive outrage) and consequentiality (mechanisms that translate deliberative outcomes into binding public decisions), jointly enabling social media to support rather than undermine collective will formation.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes a political perspective of social media platforms grounded in deliberative democracy and the political view of the firm (Scherer and Palazzo), complementing and integrating the dominant market and technology perspectives in management scholarship on platform governance. It introduces two novel capacities of the public sphere — conduciveness to argumentation and consequentiality — and theorizes how platform design, ownership, and governance jointly shape these capacities. It also connects debates on platform governance with social evaluations, stakeholder engagement, and the "political role of business" literature, advocating a deeper engagement with democratic theory in management research.
practical_implication: The essay proposes concrete reforms at three levels. At the platform level, companies should redesign recommendation and engagement systems to foster diverse, sustained, and reasoned discourse (e.g., connecting neighbouring discourses instead of reinforcing echo chambers; rewarding extended engagement with critical issues rather than quick emotional reactions). At the regulatory level, governments should develop frameworks (e.g., in the spirit of the EU Digital Services Act) that hold platforms accountable as political actors with public-sphere responsibilities, including deliberative capacity obligations. At the individual level, digital literacy and civic education — supported by governments, schools, libraries, and non-profits — should equip citizens to participate competently in algorithmically mediated public debate.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further theoretical and empirical investigation of the tensions underlying conducive argumentation (polarization, fragmentation, hate speech, misinformation, short-termism) across different platform governance models, including varying degrees of centralization and accountability, and the internal power dynamics of platform organizations. They also urge research on consequentiality — how deliberative outcomes on social media translate into legitimacy changes, governance practices, stakeholder engagement, and institutional change — and on the cognitive and emotional barriers that hinder this translation. Finally, they call for interdisciplinary collaboration among technologists, philosophers, political theorists, and management scholars to develop the ethical, political, and regulatory frameworks needed for democratic integration of social media.
abstract: Social media platforms, once hailed as potential champions of dialogue, have evolved into commodified spaces in which their business models incentivize hate speech, misinformation, polarization, and the political fragmentation of society, benefiting corporate and political elites while eroding democracy. The existing market and technological conceptualizations of social media platforms offer only partial analysis of and solutions to these issues. This paper advances a political perspective grounded in deliberative democracy, arguing that social media companies are political actors responsible for maintaining a well-functioning public sphere. We argue that transforming social media platforms into true facilitators of democratic discourse requires enhancing their deliberative capacity. Beyond the capacities of the public sphere suggested by the market and technology perspectives (1) transparency and accountability and (2) openness and inclusiveness, we propose that fostering deliberative capacity also requires (3) conduciveness to argumentation and (4) consequentiality. We thus contribute to understanding how management theories can explain and address current threats to democracy.
apa_citation: Castelló, I., Colleoni, E., Scherer, A. G., & Trittin-Ulbrich, H. (2025). Social media is a threat for democracy! A political perspective for analysing and diminishing harm. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70053

title: Do Women Executives Make Workplaces Safer? Evidence from Workplace Safety Violations
methods: Firm-fixed-effects panel regressions on longitudinal firm-year data; linear probability model for binary DV; mediation analysis via Seo-style product-of-coefficients path decomposition.
industry: US publicly traded firms across industries (S&P 500 / 400 mid-cap / 600 small-cap), breakdown by 2-digit SIC reported
units: firm-year observations
n_sample: 15,635 firm-year observations across 1,188 US firms
authors: Chen, Y.; Ofosu, E.; O'Sullivan, D.; Tang, S.; Zolotoy, L.
theories: critical mass theory; gender role theory; upper echelons theory
keywords: corporate social performance; women's executive representation; workplace safety
research_question: Whether, when, and how the prevalence of women executives in firms' top management influences workplace safety — a fundamental 'do no harm' aspect of corporate social performance.
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Employee Workload (first principal component of abnormal production and ln production per employee)
- Controls: board independence and size, CEO-chair duality, firm size, ROA, market-to-book, leverage, turnover, tangibility, capital expenditure, firm fixed effects, year fixed effects

The authors theorize that women executives' communal orientation and greater risk aversion elevate the salience of employee welfare within top management decision-making, discouraging excessive risk-taking and encouraging more deliberative oversight of safety policies, safety climate, and — most directly — employee workload. Because workload decisions cascade from executive priorities, performance targets, and incentive designs, TMTs with critical-mass women's representation are expected to avoid production pressures that push workers beyond safe capacity. The influence of women executives is amplified when structural supports — a gender-diverse board and gender-advocating institutional owners — legitimate and embolden their voice, attenuating the discounting of minority perspectives within the TMT.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes research on executive gender by shifting attention from the 'do good' to the 'do no harm' dimension of corporate social performance, showing that demographic composition at the apex is consequential for firms' basic obligation to protect employee welfare. It bridges the largely disconnected literatures on executive gender and board gender diversity by documenting their reinforcing dynamic, and refines theory on institutional ownership by showing that gender-advocating owners can legitimate — rather than substitute for — women executives' influence. By identifying employee workload as a proximal mediating mechanism, it extends the small body of work that opens the 'black box' between women's executive representation and firm outcomes.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to improve workplace safety have a concrete motivation to recruit, retain, and advance women executives through leadership development, mentorship, and inclusive hiring. Firms with less diverse boards can pursue board-level gender diversity and partnerships with gender-advocating institutional owners to create a reinforcing governance dynamic. Transparent disclosure of workplace safety metrics and diversity initiatives targeting top management can align investor expectations with organizational safety priorities.
limitations: Reliance on US data may limit generalizability to other cultural and institutional contexts with different regulatory enforcement or gender norms. The Violation Tracker proxy captures reported violations and may overlook unreported incidents or latent safety concerns. The workload measure relies on archival proxies (abnormal production, production per employee) rather than employees' self-reported workload, task complexity, or physiological stress indicators.
future_research: Cross-national and cross-cultural studies could uncover additional boundary conditions on how societal, regulatory, and cultural factors shape the gender-safety link. Researchers could examine downstream effects on employee well-being, absenteeism, and long-term firm performance, and explore which specific executive roles (CFO, COO, CHRO) and the alignment between women executives and women middle managers most shape safety outcomes. Employee surveys, qualitative interviews, and third-party safety audits would enrich measurement of both workload and safety.
abstract: In this study, we develop and test theory on whether, when, and how the prevalence of women in firms' top management influences workplace safety – an important 'do no harm' dimension of corporate social performance. Consistent with our theorizing, we find that there is a negative relationship between the prevalence of women executives in firms' top management and workplace safety violations. We show that the relationship is amplified by board gender diversity and ownership by institutional investors who advocate for greater gender diversity in firms' upper echelons. We also show that the relationship is partially mediated by employee workload. Our findings have important implications for the management literature on the gender profile of firms' top management and for the workplace safety literature.
apa_citation: Chen, Y., Ofosu, E., O'Sullivan, D., Tang, S., & Zolotoy, L. (2025). Do women executives make workplaces safer? Evidence from workplace safety violations. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70055

title: Firm Growth and Corporate Social Responsibility
methods: Panel data analysis; OLS regressions with firm and year fixed effects; instrumental-variable two-stage models and ITCV tests for endogeneity; moderation analyses
industry: Multi-industry (firms listed in LSEG ESG database)
country: 46 countries (global sample spanning shareholder- and stakeholder-oriented economies)
units: Firm-year observations
n_sample: 51,649 firm-year observations for 4,305 firms
authors: DesJardine, M. R.; Kim, J.; Bansal, P.
theories: attention-based view of the firm; resource-based trade-offs
keywords: business sustainability; corporate social responsibility; financial slack; firm growth; managerial attention
research_question: How does the rate of firm growth affect firms' CSR engagement, and how is this relationship shaped by firms' financial and attentional resources?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the attention-based view of the firm, the authors argue that managerial attention and financial capital are finite resources that both firm growth and CSR draw upon. When growth accelerates, managers must allocate scarce cognitive and financial resources toward coordinating expansion, scaling operations, and meeting short-term performance targets, leaving less bandwidth and liquidity for CSR initiatives that yield intangible, long-term, and difficult-to-measure returns. Because CSR outcomes are distant and peripheral to core business activity, managers under pressure to deliver measurable growth-related outcomes reallocate attention and resources away from CSR. Performance variability amplifies this trade-off by further depleting attentional reservoirs, while financial slack attenuates it by providing a capital cushion that preserves managerial latitude to pursue non-growth initiatives during periods of rapid expansion.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances CSR research by identifying firm growth—one of the most pervasive and central features of organizational life—as a previously underexplored driver of CSR engagement, complementing work on organizational architecture, identity, and institutional drivers. It integrates the attention-based view with CSR theory to foreground the role of managerial attention (not only financial slack) as a scarce input that shapes corporate commitments, and it links the attention-based view with stakeholder governance by showing how growth pressures condition the shareholder-stakeholder trade-off. In doing so, the paper reframes the growth-sustainability debate away from a purely ideological contest and toward a pragmatic, trade-off-based account of managerial resource allocation.
practical_implication: For companies committed to CSR, the authors recommend establishing systems and controls that insulate CSR from fluctuations in managerial attention and financial resources—for instance, CSR departments with independent budgets (via corporate foundations or trusts) and strategic autonomy, analogous to governance structures afforded to R&D. Elevating CSR to an executive leadership position can help integrate it into the firm's core rather than treating it as peripheral. Socially responsible shareholders and stakeholders can also counteract growth pressures by putting forth CSR-related proposals during high-growth periods to redirect managerial attention and resources back toward CSR.
limitations: The study does not directly observe the microlevel managerial processes that link growth rates to CSR decisions, since attention and resource-allocation decisions are hard to capture with archival data. The analysis also takes a relatively static snapshot and is agnostic about the long-run and overall effect of growth on CSR and business sustainability, which may differ from the short-term trade-offs documented.
future_research: Future work should directly observe managers in high-growth firms (e.g., through qualitative or field methods) to examine whether growth-CSR trade-offs are made deliberately or emerge unintentionally. Researchers should also study the longer-term, historical relationship between growth and CSR, including whether growth-focused firms eventually produce game-changing CSR innovations that offset earlier trade-offs. Further theorizing is needed on how managers make attention-related trade-offs in pursuing CSR and on which organizational structures and characteristics shape those decisions.
abstract: This paper examines how the rate of firm growth influences corporate social responsibility (CSR). Periods of rapid growth can place substantial financial and managerial demands on firms, requiring leaders to prioritize the allocation of limited resources toward managing expansion. We propose that when firms grow quickly, managers may limit CSR investments—not because they undervalue CSR, but because growth itself consumes financial and attentional capacity. Using 19 years of data from 4,305 firms across 46 countries, we find that faster firm growth is associated with lower levels of CSR activity. This relationship is moderated by firms’ financial and attentional resources: firms with greater financial slack and managerial bandwidth maintain stronger CSR engagement even while growing rapidly. Overall, our results suggest that the relationship between growth and CSR reflects a pragmatic balancing of competing demands.
apa_citation: DesJardine, M. R., Kim, J., & Bansal, P. (2025). Firm growth and corporate social responsibility. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251398785

title: The Need for Speed: The Impact of Capital Constraints on Strategic Misconduct
methods: Empirical quantitative analysis using ordinary least squares (OLS), instrumental variable (IV), and coarsened exact matching models on vehicle-level panel data; complemented by abductive qualitative fieldwork including 60+ hours of interviews and a survey of 100 firm owners; leverages a 2017 industry-wide strike in South Africa as a source of quasi-exogenous variation in interest rates
industry: Minibus taxi industry (informal public transport / SMEs)
time_period: Vehicles financed January 2016 to December 2018; outcomes measured over first 15 months of operation
units: Individual minibus taxi vehicles (Toyota 16-seater Quantums) financed by a large South African taxi financier, with GPS telemetry, financing, owner, and insurance-claim data
n_sample: 3,395 vehicle observations in the main regression sample (from 4,407 unique vehicles and ~8 million daily vehicle observations across 7,593 financed vehicles)
authors: Eaglin, F. C.
theories: financial constraints and misconduct; institutional voids; strategic misconduct
keywords: capital constraints; decision making; emerging markets; firm performance; minibus taxi industry; misconduct; organizational studies; small and medium-sized enterprises; strategic misconduct; strategy
research_question: What is the impact of relaxing capital constraints on strategic misconduct among small and medium-sized firms in emerging markets whose survival and profitability depend on misconduct, and why do such firms engage in strategic misconduct even when it carries high risks?
mechanism_summary: High capital costs create high minimum revenue requirements to stay in business; taxi operators speed and drive aggressively to complete as many trips as possible to meet loan obligations and avoid default, whose penalties in this setting extend beyond asset loss to loss of livelihood for extended families, loss of standing in associations, and risk of violence. When interest rates fall, the required monthly revenue drops, reducing the marginal benefit of the most dangerous forms of misconduct; firms therefore cut back on the most extreme speeding and collisions while continuing milder speeding. The mechanism is thus one in which relaxing capital constraints loosens the survival pressure that makes severe strategic misconduct a dominant strategy choice for SMEs operating under intense competition, weak regulation, and institutional voids.
theoretical_contribution: The paper argues that strategic misconduct is a core choice among strategic options for firms facing severe financial pressure, not merely a product of moral failing or perverse incentives, and shows that relaxing capital constraints can reduce the most severe misconduct with broad public-welfare benefits. It contributes to the literatures on misconduct, entrepreneurship in emerging markets, institutional voids, and prosocial firm behavior by studying survival-oriented SMEs rather than large well-resourced firms or individual financial advisors, and by linking strategic misconduct directly to weak regulatory environments and high capital costs. It also suggests that mainstream management strategy theories may have limited applicability for resource-constrained firms operating under intense competition and weak regulation, and that previous estimates of the social cost of expensive capital understate its true welfare impact once misconduct is accounted for.
practical_implication: For financiers serving SMEs in competitive environments with weak regulation, charging excessively high interest rates harms both the lender and the borrower: lower rates improve loan performance, reduce accidents involving third parties, and protect employees and the broader public by lowering the incidence of the most extreme strategic misconduct. Policymakers and funders should therefore consider how to deliver more affordable capital to such firms, for example through working-capital facilities or interest-rate caps, and should attend to the outsized penalties of default that push owners toward dangerous behavior. Improved measurement of misconduct in SME settings can aid the design of better markets for capital and services.
limitations: Firms are not randomly assigned lower interest rates, so selection could bias estimates upward, though OLS, IV, and matching models all yield consistent results and fieldwork suggests selection operates primarily at the point of joining an association rather than at financing. Generalizability is limited because the sample is drawn from a highly competitive, sparsely regulated, and often violent transport setting; effects may be more muted in settings with stronger enforcement or cheaper capital. The study does not evaluate intrafirm dynamics (e.g., owner–driver principal–agent problems) because of data limitations.
future_research: Future research should examine how differing enforcement norms and regulatory regimes mitigate or magnify the responsiveness of misconduct to capital constraints, and whether the findings extend beyond informal transport markets to other SME-intensive industries in emerging economies. Scholars should investigate intrafirm dynamics underlying strategic misconduct, including monitoring technologies, and explore how policymakers and funders can design financing products (e.g., working-capital facilities, lower interest rates) that reduce the penalties of default and the incentives to engage in misconduct. Further work is needed on improved measurement of misconduct in SME settings and on how such measurement can inform the design of better markets for capital and services.
abstract: Abstract. Existing literature suggests that firms engage in misconduct in response to Revised: June 3, 2024; August 5, 2024; financial pressure. However, the extent and severity of misconduct have been challenging November 14, 2024 to determine given the lack of granular data. This limitation is particularly true for smaller Accepted: November 18, 2024 firms in emerging markets whose profitability and survival depend on misconduct. This Published Online in Articles in Advance: study analyzes the impact of reducing capital constraints on misconduct using highJuly 1, 2025 frequency global positioning system and financing data from the minibus taxi industry in https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2023.01957 South Africa. I find that lower interest rates, a proxy for lower capital constraints, decrease the most severe forms of misconduct measured by the propensity to speed and crash yet Copyright: © 2025 INFORMS leave more modest forms of misconduct unchanged. Qualitative interviews and survey data suggest that these results are attributable to firms’ efforts to avoid default. These find­ ings contribute to understanding misconduct as a firm strategy when facing financial pres­ sure and suggest that lower capital constraints might improve firm survival and public safety.

title: A cultural perspective on informal ride sharing
methods: Interpretivist qualitative design; 45 semi-structured telephone interviews analysed using Braun and Clarke's six-step thematic analysis (following Nikitas et al.'s systematised adaptation); inductive coding yielding 33 first-order codes, 12 second-order sub-themes and 4 final themes
industry: Urban mobility / informal transport
units: Lagos residents aged 18-64 (female 53.3%, male 46.6%; car owners 62.2%, non-car owners 37.7%; employed 80%, unemployed 8.8%, students 11.1%)
authors: Ehidiamen, A.; Cheetham, F.; Nikitas, A.
theories: Arnould and Rose's mutuality; Belk's theory of sharing; Marcoux's escaping the gift economy; Schwartz's theory of cultural values
keywords: Cultural values; Informal ride sharing; Ride sharing; Sharing; Sharing practices
research_question: Within a collectivist culture, what cultural sharing values influence consumers' engagement in informal ride sharing?
mechanism_summary: The authors theorise that culturally transmitted value orientations socialise individuals into shared norms about with whom and why one shares rides. In Nigeria's predominantly collectivist context, embeddedness and interdependence cultivate communal practices, group attachment and altruistic cultural morality that motivate informal ride sharing as a normative, non-transactional practice (Belk, 2010). At the same time, the obligation dimension — rooted in hierarchy and reciprocal expectations — can inhibit engagement, especially among younger consumers who prize autonomy and seek to "escape the gift economy" (Marcoux, 2009). Cultural sharing values therefore operate simultaneously as motivating, inhibiting and conflicting forces, shaping whether consumers enter informal ride sharing on the basis of pure sharing, mutuality or transactional avoidance.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends Schwartz's (2006) theory of cultural value dimensions into the sharing-economy literature by identifying four sub-dimensions of cultural sharing values — interdependence, functional value, cultural morality and obligation — that shape consumer engagement in informal ride sharing within a collectivist culture. By integrating Belk's (2010) conceptualisation of sharing, Marcoux's (2009) escaping the gift economy and Arnould and Rose's (2016) mutuality, it uncovers three key roles (motivating, inhibiting and conflicted) played by cultural sharing values, and shows that even within collectivist cultures subgroups may hold divergent values that challenge the dominant cultural narrative.
practical_implication: The findings offer guidance for policymakers and mobility planners in contexts where informal ride sharing coexists with limited formal transport. They should align transport strategies with local cultural norms, integrate informal practices as complementary solutions to gaps in formal infrastructure, and introduce community-based network structures in low-access areas. Mobility planners can scale peer-to-peer informal ride sharing through city-wide schemes targeting underserviced routes and peak periods, delivering both communal and functional value and connecting a broader pool of riders.
limitations: The sample is skewed toward younger consumers because of online recruitment and younger individuals' greater awareness of and activity in shared mobility, leaving older consumers' attitudes underexplored. The study focuses exclusively on informal ride sharing and does not examine the dynamics between informal and dynamic ride sharing within collectivist cultures. As an exploratory qualitative study, the findings provide depth but limit generalisability.
future_research: Further studies should explicitly explore older consumers' attitudes toward informal sharing practices. Future research could expand the inquiry to other sectors and contrasting cultural contexts to offer a more comprehensive understanding of sharing practices across societies. Quantitative methodologies could also be adopted to test relationships between cultural sharing values and consumer motivations for engaging in ride sharing.
abstract: Purpose – Although the literature on dynamic ride sharing has grown significantly, there remains a notable gap in understanding informal ride sharing, particularly within the context of developing countries. Informal ride sharing remains prevalent in sociocultural environments where community-level sharing thrives. The purpose of this study is to explore the cultural sharing values that influence consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing. Design/methodology/approach – This study adopts a qualitative methodology based on semi-structured interviews and uses data-driven thematic analysis approach to analyse 45 semi-structured interviews conducted with participants who reside in Lagos, Nigeria. Findings – This study identifies four sub-dimensions and three key roles of cultural sharing values that influence consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing. These sub-dimensions − interdependence, functional value, cultural morality and obligation are underpinned by distinct cultural value orientations. Together, they serve as motivating and inhibiting factors that shape consumer engagement. Practical implications – This study has implications for policymakers and mobility planners by emphasising the significance of informal ride sharing within specific sociocultural contexts. The findings provide consumer-focused insights that can inform the design of culturally responsive transport strategies that recognise and leverage the prevalence of informal sharing practices. Originality/value – Adopting a cultural lens, this study provides novel insight into the role of cultural sharing values and how it shapes consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing.
apa_citation: Ehidiamen, A., Cheetham, F., & Nikitas, A. (2025). A cultural perspective on informal ride sharing. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-01-2025-7571

title: The Ethics of Global Business, by Denis G. Arnold. Wiley Blackwell, 2023. 161 pp.
methods: Book review; narrative critique of Denis G. Arnold's The Ethics of Global Business (2023)
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Enderle, G.
theories: claim-rights theory; human rights cosmopolitanism
keywords: Base of the Pyramid; Tripartite Framework; corporate social responsibility; global business ethics; human rights; human rights cosmopolitanism; transnational corporations
research_question: Not reported in paper.
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper.
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper.
practical_implication: Enderle emphasizes that Arnold's action-oriented analysis is valuable for practice: TNCs should respect the basic human rights of people impacted at the BoP, ensure minimal health and safety conditions in the workplace, inform employees of workplace hazards during hiring, and pay a weekly wage consistent with basic human dignity. Business ventures engaging Moderate and Extreme Poverty populations should pursue empowerment strategies (assessed against human rights, utility, and capabilities) rather than exploitation strategies. The book is recommended for teaching, research, and training in TNCs.
limitations: Enderle raises four critical remarks about Arnold's book: (1) it does not fully apply Gewirth's five-component structure of claim-rights, leaving imprecision about subjects, objects, respondents, binding nature, and justification of human rights; (2) it conflates "duty" and "responsibility," defining TNCs as "Duty Bearers" rather than "Bearers of Responsibility" and retaining the confusing term CSR; (3) its assessment of the Tripartite Framework is less favorable than warranted, and it omits a philosophical justification grounded in human dignity; and (4) the cited literature is almost exclusively pre-2013 and would benefit from more recent references.
future_research: Enderle suggests that the philosophical justification of human rights in pluralistic societies, the precise division of obligations between states and corporations, and the clarification of responsibility as a three-aspect ethical term (who, for what, towards whom) deserve further exploration. He also notes that the long-term impact of the Tripartite Framework—visible, for instance, in the EU's 2025 Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive—merits continued study, and that the intriguing issues raised in Arnold's book deserve to be further explored and discussed.
abstract: n his book The Ethics of Global Business (2023) Denis Arnold addresses highly important challenges of global business, with a focus on the relations of the powerful transnational corporations (TNCs) to the Base of the Pyramid (BoP)— that is, four billion people below $9.05 per day (107, Figure 7.1)—and the ethical assessment in terms of universal human rights. The author draws on decades of research and his familiarity as a five-year editor of BEQ with a vast part of literature of business ethics. He is also supported by the collaboration of Andrew Valentin and Laura Williams in the last two chapters (7 and 8).
